# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**State Chezy's formula and explain the significance of Chezy's constant.**

**Model Answer:**

Chezy's formula for uniform flow in an open channel or pipe is:
$$
V = C \sqrt{m \, i}
$$
where $V$ is the mean velocity, $m$ is the hydraulic mean depth (cross-sectional area divided by wetted perimeter), $i$ is the hydraulic gradient (head loss per unit length), and $C$ is **Chezy's constant**.

**Significance:** Chezy's constant $C$ is a measure of the **resistance offered by the boundary** of the conduit to the flowing fluid. A higher $C$ indicates a smoother surface and lower resistance. Its SI unit is $\text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$. It is empirically determined for a given surface and is widely used in the design of open channels, sewers, and partially-filled pipes. It is mathematically linked to Darcy's friction factor by $C = \sqrt{8g/f}$.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for definition of variables, 1 mark for significance/unit/relation.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Define Darcy's friction factor and write the Darcy–Weisbach equation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Darcy's friction factor $f$** is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the resistance to fluid flow due to viscous and turbulent shear stresses at the pipe wall.

**Darcy–Weisbach equation:**
$$
h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g d}
$$
where $h_f$ is the head loss due to friction over a pipe length $L$ of internal diameter $d$ carrying mean velocity $V$ under gravity $g$.

$f$ depends on the **Reynolds number** $Re = Vd/\nu$ and the **relative roughness** $\varepsilon/d$. For laminar flow ($Re < 2000$), $f = 64/Re$. For turbulent flow, $f$ is read from the **Moody chart**.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for dependence and laminar case.)*

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the manometer constant 12.6** when using a mercury–water U-tube — common 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Using nominal diameter instead of internal diameter** of the pipe — silent error of 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up $f$ (Darcy) and $f'$ (Fanning)** — wrong $C$–$f$ relation leads to cascading errors.
> 4. **Not converting discharge to SI units** (cm³/s to m³/s) before using in velocity formula.
> 5. **Forgetting to plot $h_f$ vs. $V^2$** as a straight line in the lab record.
> 6. **Writing units inconsistently** — $C$ must be in $\text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$, not dimensionless.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Computation-Focused

#### [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**a) (7 marks)** A horizontal pipe of internal diameter $d = 50 \ \text{mm}$ and length between pressure tappings $L = 2.0 \ \text{m}$ carries water. The discharge collected in a measuring tank is $12.5$ litres in $25$ seconds. The differential mercury manometer shows a deflection of $x = 25 \ \text{mm}$. Calculate:
(i) The mean velocity of flow.
(ii) The frictional head loss.
(iii) Darcy's friction factor.
(iv) Chezy's constant.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Mean velocity:**
Discharge $Q = 12.5 \ \text{L} / 25 \ \text{s} = 0.5 \ \text{L/s} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$.

$$
V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{Q}{\pi d^2 / 4} = \frac{4 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi \times (0.050)^2}
$$

$$
V = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V = 0.2546 \ \text{m/s}\,} \quad \text{(3 marks)}
$$

**(ii) Frictional head loss:**
$$
h_f = x \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) = 0.025 \times 12.6 = 0.315 \ \text{m of water}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,h_f = 0.315 \ \text{m}\,} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**(iii) Darcy's friction factor:**
$$
f = \frac{2 g d \, h_f}{L V^2} = \frac{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.050 \times 0.315}{2.0 \times (0.2546)^2}
$$

Numerator: $2 \times 9.81 \times 0.050 \times 0.315 = 0.3090$.

Denominator: $2.0 \times 0.06483 = 0.1297$.

$$
\boxed{\,f = \frac{0.3090}{0.1297} = 2.384 \times 10^{-2} \approx 0.0238\,} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**(iv) Chezy's constant:**
$$
C = \sqrt{\frac{8 g}{f}} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 9.81}{0.0238}} = \sqrt{\frac{78.48}{0.0238}} = \sqrt{3297.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C \approx 57.42 \ \text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s}\,} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

---

#### **b) (7 marks)** Derive the relationship between Chezy's constant and Darcy's friction factor for a full-flowing circular pipe.

**Model Solution:**

Full derivation as in Section 3.1 of these notes:

1. Start with Darcy–Weisbach: $h_f = f L V^2 / (2gd)$ ...(i)
2. Start with Chezy: $V = C \sqrt{m \cdot i}$ with $m = d/4$ and $i = h_f/L$ ...(ii)
3. Squaring (ii): $V^2 = C^2 \cdot (d/4) \cdot (h_f/L)$ ...(iii)
4. Rearranging (iii): $h_f = 4 L V^2 / (C^2 d)$ ...(iv)
5. Equating (i) and (iv): $\dfrac{f L V^2}{2 g d} = \dfrac{4 L V^2}{C^2 d}$
6. Cancel $L V^2 / d$: $\dfrac{f}{2g} = \dfrac{4}{C^2}$
7. Cross-multiply: $C^2 f = 8 g$
8. Final boxed result: $C = \sqrt{8g/f}$ and $f = 8g/C^2$ ...(v)

*(7 marks — 1 mark each for (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v) steps plus 2 marks for the final boxed result and units.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Theory + Conceptual Choice

#### [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**a) (7 marks)** With a neat sketch, describe the experimental procedure to determine Chezy's constant and Darcy's coefficient using a pipe friction apparatus. List the observations and formulae used.

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus:** Overhead tank, test pipe of known diameter $d$ and length $L$ between tappings, differential U-tube manometer (mercury–water), collecting tank, stopwatch, vernier caliper.

**Procedure:**
1. Measure the internal diameter $d$ of the test pipe at several sections using a vernier caliper; take the mean.
2. Measure the length $L$ between the two pressure tappings using a steel tape.
3. Ensure the manometer is free of air bubbles; fill the U-tube with mercury and water columns.
4. Open the inlet valve slowly until a steady flow is established.
5. Adjust the outlet valve to a desired discharge.
6. Note the manometer deflection $x$ in metres once the flow is fully steady.
7. Collect a known volume $V_{tank}$ in the collecting tank and record the time $t$ using a stopwatch.
8. Repeat the experiment for **at least 5 different discharges** by adjusting the outlet valve.
9. Tabulate all observations.

**Observations Tabulated:** $t$, $x$ (and hence $h_f = 12.6 x$), $Q = V_{tank}/t$, $V = 4Q/(\pi d^2)$, $f$, $C$.

**Formulae Used:**
- $Q = V_{tank}/t$
- $V = 4Q/(\pi d^2)$
- $h_f = x \left(\rho_{Hg}/\rho_w - 1\right) = 12.6 \, x$
- $f = 2 g d h_f / (L V^2)$
- $C = V / \sqrt{(d/4)(h_f/L)} = \sqrt{8g/f}$

**Graph:** Plot $h_f$ on the Y-axis versus $V^2$ on the X-axis. The result should be a straight line through the origin, validating Darcy's law.

*(7 marks — 2 marks for apparatus + sketch, 2 marks for procedure, 2 marks for formulae, 1 mark for graph.)*

---

#### **b) (7 marks)** Explain why the pressure tappings are placed along a horizontal section of the pipe, and what happens if the pipe is inclined. Also discuss the role of the entry length.

**Model Answer:**

**Horizontal section choice:**
Bernoulli's equation between two sections of a pipe:
$$
\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f
$$

For a **horizontal pipe** ($z_1 = z_2$) with **constant diameter** ($V_1 = V_2$):
$$
h_f = \frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g}
$$

The piezometric head difference equals the frictional head loss **directly**, with no correction required. This simplifies measurement and calculation.

**Inclined pipe:**
If the pipe is inclined at angle $\theta$, the datum difference $z_2 - z_1$ must be subtracted from the piezometric head difference:
$$
h_f = \left(\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1\right) - \left(\frac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2\right) = \Delta h - (z_1 - z_2)
$$

Failure to correct for the datum term leads to **erroneous $f$ values** (either too high or too low depending on the direction of inclination).

**Entry length:**
The flow just after entering a pipe is not fully developed — the velocity profile transitions from a nearly uniform profile to the parabolic (laminar) or flatter (turbulent) fully-developed profile. The **entry length** $L_e$ is:
- Laminar: $L_e \approx 0.05 \cdot Re \cdot d$
- Turbulent: $L_e \approx 10$ to $60 \cdot d$

Pressure tappings must be placed **beyond** $L_e$ to ensure the velocity profile is fully developed, otherwise the measured $h_f$ includes additional **form losses** from flow development, contaminating the friction-loss measurement.

*(7 marks — 2 marks for horizontal justification, 2 marks for inclined correction, 3 marks for entry length discussion.)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Darcy–Weisbach:** $h_f = f L V^2 / (2 g d)$ — the foundational closed-pipe friction formula.
- **Chezy's formula:** $V = C \sqrt{m \, i}$ — used for open-channel and full-pipe uniform flow.
- **$C$–$f$ link:** $C = \sqrt{8g/f}$ (Darcy $f$) — derive from equating both expressions for $h_f$.
- **Hydraulic mean depth** for full pipe: $m = d/4$ (area ÷ wetted perimeter).
- **Mercury manometer conversion:** $h_f = 12.6 \, x$ (always, where $x$ is the deflection in metres).
- **Discharge from volumetric method:** $Q = V_{tank} / t$ — convert volume to $\text{m}^3$ before using in any formula.
- **Mean velocity:** $V = 4Q / (\pi d^2)$ — use **internal** diameter, not nominal.
- **Darcy's $f$ is dimensionless**; **Chezy's $C$** is in $\text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$.
- **Graphical proof:** $h_f$ vs. $V^2$ must be a **straight line through the origin** — this is the lab's "proof of validity."
- **Pressure tappings on horizontal pipe** avoid datum corrections; inclined pipes need $z_1 - z_2$ subtraction.
- **Entry length** must be exceeded; otherwise form losses contaminate the friction reading.
- **Laminar flow:** $f = 64 / Re$ — but typical KTU lab discharges place flow in the **turbulent** regime.
- **Cross-check:** Compute $C$ from the Chezy formula and again from $C = \sqrt{8g/f}$. They must agree within 3–5%.
- **Reynolds number regime identification:** $Re < 2000$ laminar; $2000 < Re < 4000$ transitional; $Re > 4000$ turbulent.
- **Common errors:** forgetting the 12.6 factor; using nominal bore; misreading manometer; air bubble in manometer; non-steady flow.
- **KTU viva favourites:** Why horizontal? Why two tappings? What is $m$? What does Moody chart show? How is $C$ related to $f$?

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Minor Losses in Pipe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiments
> - **Topic:** Determination of Minor Losses in Pipe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Minor Losses** in a pipe are defined as the loss of head (or energy) of a flowing fluid caused by the presence of **pipe fittings, valves, bends, sudden contractions, sudden expansions, elbows, tees, and entrance/exit conditions** — i.e., any local disturbance to the flow rather than friction along the pipe wall.

While **Major Losses** ($h_f$) occur uniformly along the length of a straight pipe and are computed using the **Darcy-Weisbach equation** or **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**, **Minor Losses** ($h_m$) are localized, concentrated at specific points, and are expressed as a fraction of the **velocity head** of the flowing fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim):** "Minor losses are the form losses occurring due to changes in the geometry of the flow path such as sudden expansion, sudden contraction, bends, valves, and fittings. They are expressed as $h_m = K \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$, where $K$ is the loss coefficient and $V$ is the velocity of flow."

The total head loss in a pipe system is therefore:

$$h_L = h_{major} + h_{minor} = \frac{f L V^2}{2gD} + \sum K_i \frac{V_i^2}{2g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the name "MINOR"?** Historically, these were considered negligible compared to friction losses in long pipelines. However, in **short pipes, equipment-heavy systems (HVAC, process plants, water distribution), and lab setups**, minor losses can constitute **40–60% of total head loss** and CANNOT be ignored.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Toll Booth Highway 🚗

Imagine a long highway (the straight pipe) where cars travel at a steady 80 km/h. The friction between tyres and asphalt represents **major (friction) losses**.

Now consider a highway with several toll booths, exits, and sharp turns. Even though the road is short, cars must:
- **Brake suddenly** (sudden contraction) → energy lost
- **Swerve around obstacles** (bends/elbows) → energy lost
- **Wait at the gate** (valves) → energy lost
- **Merge back onto a wider lane** (sudden expansion) → energy lost
- **Pay a toll** (pipe entrance/exit) → energy lost

Each of these is a **localized "toll" on the fluid's energy**, and the cumulative toll is the minor loss. The longer the highway, the more we add friction losses; but every toll booth is a fixed penalty regardless of how long the road is.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** Major losses are proportional to pipe **length** (L), but minor losses are **independent of length** — they only depend on the **type of fitting** and the **flow velocity**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bold Highlighted)

- **Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**
- **Density of water at 25°C, $\rho = 997 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
- **Dynamic viscosity of water at 25°C, $\mu = 0.89 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$**
- **Kinematic viscosity of water at 25°C, $\nu = 0.89 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$**
- **Standard atmospheric pressure, $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$**

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU lab viva, students often forget the difference between **major** and **minor** losses. Remember: **Major = length-dependent, Minor = fitting-dependent.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Categorization of Minor Losses

Minor losses are classified based on the **geometric disturbance** they cause to the flow streamlines:

### A) Losses Due to Change in Cross-Section
1. **Sudden Expansion (Enlargement)** — from smaller to larger pipe
2. **Sudden Contraction** — from larger to smaller pipe
3. **Gradual Conical Contraction / Diffuser**

### B) Losses Due to Change in Direction
1. **90° Elbow** (standard and long-radius)
2. **45° Elbow** and **22.5° Bend**
3. **U-Bend / Return Bend (180°)**
4. **Tee-Junction** (line flow and branch flow)

### C) Losses Due to Flow Regulation Devices
1. **Gate Valve** (fully open, half open, quarter open)
2. **Globe Valve**
3. **Ball Valve / Check Valve / Foot Valve**

### D) Losses at Pipe Entry and Exit
1. **Sharp-edged entrance** ($K = 0.5$)
2. **Re-entrant (Borda) entrance** ($K = 1.0$)
3. **Rounded entrance** ($K \approx 0.04$)
4. **Submerged exit** ($K = 1.0$)

---

## 2.2 General Governing Equation

For **any minor loss fitting**, the head loss is given by the universal form:

$$h_m = K \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

where:
- $h_m$ = minor head loss in **meters of fluid**
- $K$ = **dimensionless loss coefficient** (specific to fitting type and geometry)
- $V$ = **flow velocity** (usually downstream velocity, in m/s)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** $K$ is **NOT a universal constant**. It depends on the **Reynolds number**, the **area ratio**, the **angle**, and the **manufacturing tolerance** of the fitting. Always refer to the KTU lab manual for the specific K value of the apparatus used.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving minor loss numerical problems in KTU examinations. All values are taken from standard fluid mechanics references (White, Munson, Modi) and KTU prescribed data.

| # | Fitting Type | Loss Coefficient $K$ | Head Loss Formula | Remarks / Validity |
|:-:|:-------------|:--------------------:|:------------------|:-------------------|
| 1 | **Sudden Expansion** | $K = \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2$ | $h_m = \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2 \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g}$ | Also called Borda-Carnot loss |
| 2 | **Sudden Contraction** | $K_c$ from $A_2/A_1$ chart (0.0 → 0.5) | $h_m = K_c \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}$ | $K_c$ depends on $d_2/d_1$ ratio |
| 3 | **Pipe Entry (Sharp-edged)** | $K = 0.5$ | $h_m = 0.5 \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Standard wall flush inlet |
| 4 | **Pipe Entry (Re-entrant)** | $K = 1.0$ | $h_m = 1.0 \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Pipe protrudes into reservoir |
| 5 | **Pipe Entry (Well-rounded)** | $K \approx 0.04$ | $h_m = 0.04 \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Radius $\geq 0.15\,D$ |
| 6 | **Pipe Exit (Submerged)** | $K = 1.0$ | $h_m = 1.0 \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Full velocity head lost |
| 7 | **90° Elbow (Standard)** | $K = 0.9$ | $h_m = 0.9 \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | r/D = 1 |
| 8 | **90° Elbow (Long Radius)** | $K = 0.4$ | $h_m = 0.4 \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | r/D = 1.5 |
| 9 | **90° Elbow (Mitre, sharp)** | $K = 1.3$ | $h_m = 1.3 \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | No curvature |
| 10 | **45° Elbow** | $K = 0.4$ | $h_m = 0.4 \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Standard radius |
| 11 | **180° Return Bend** | $K = 2.2$ | $h_m = 2.2 \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | U-shaped |
| 12 | **Tee — Line Flow** | $K = 0.9$ | $h_m = 0.9 \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Straight-through |
| 13 | **Tee — Branch Flow** | $K = 1.8$ | $h_m = 1.8 \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | 90° turn in branch |
| 14 | **Gate Valve (Fully Open)** | $K = 0.15$ | $h_m = 0.15 \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Minimal obstruction |
| 15 | **Gate Valve (Half Open)** | $K = 4.5$ | $h_m = 4.5 \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | KTU viva favourite |
| 16 | **Globe Valve (Fully Open)** | $K = 10.0$ | $h_m = 10.0 \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Highest K among valves |
| 17 | **Ball / Check Valve** | $K = 2.5$ | $h_m = 2.5 \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Swing check |
| 18 | **Foot Valve / Strainer** | $K = 2.0$ | $h_m = 2.0 \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Pump suction |
| 19 | **Standard Coupling** | $K = 0.04$ | $h_m = 0.04 \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Plain union |
| 20 | **Equivalent Length** | $K = f \dfrac{L_e}{D}$ | $h_m = f \dfrac{L_e}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | $L_e$ in metres |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark-Boarding Tip:** The examiner checks whether you have written $V$ in the **correct section** (upstream or downstream). For **sudden expansion, use $V_1$** (upstream/smaller pipe). For **sudden contraction, use $V_2$** (downstream/smaller pipe). Wrong velocity = **2 marks deducted**!

---

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Applications

1. **🏭 Process Industries (Refineries, Chemical Plants):** Engineers use $K$-values to size pumps correctly. Underestimating minor losses leads to pump head shortfall and process failure.

2. **🚿 Water Distribution Networks:** Urban water supply designers (CPHEEO Manual) account for minor losses at every meter, valve, and bend using the equivalent length method: $L_{eq} = K \cdot D / f$.

3. **🏗️ HVAC Systems:** In building air-conditioning ductwork, fitting losses often **dominate** over duct friction. ASHRAE standards provide extensive $K$ tables for duct fittings.

4. **⚙️ Hydraulic Machinery:** Minor losses in the **suction and delivery piping** of centrifugal pumps directly affect **NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head)** and cavitation risk.

5. **🛳️ Marine & Aerospace:** Fuel and coolant lines in ships/aircraft use carefully designed bends (long-radius elbows with $K=0.4$) to minimize pumping power.

6. **🔥 Fire Fighting Systems:** NFPA standards mandate exact K-factors for sprinkler heads — derived from the same $h = K V^2/2g$ principle.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Loss Due to Sudden Expansion (Borda-Carnot Equation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **KTU 2024 high-yield topic** and has appeared in previous University Examinations (Dec 2022, June 2023) for **full 7 marks**.

### Statement
When a fluid flows from a smaller pipe (area $A_1$, velocity $V_1$) into a larger pipe (area $A_2$, velocity $V_2$), there is a permanent loss of head due to the dissipation of kinetic energy in the turbulent eddies formed at the expansion.

### Setup & Assumptions
- Steady, incompressible, turbulent flow
- Horizontal pipe (no elevation change)
- Apply **Continuity** and **Linear Momentum** equations between sections (1) and (2)
- Pressure at section (1) is $P_1$, at section (2) is $P_2$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Apply the Continuity Equation**

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = V_2 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

This is the **mass conservation** for incompressible flow.

**Step 2: Apply the Linear Momentum Equation (Newton's 2nd Law for fluid)**

The net force on the control volume equals the rate of change of momentum:

$$P_1 A_2 - P_2 A_2 = \rho Q (V_1 - V_2)$$

where $Q = A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ is the volumetric discharge.

**Step 3: Simplify the Momentum Equation**

Divide both sides by $A_2$:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \rho V_2 (V_1 - V_2) \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$

But $\dfrac{A_1}{A_2} \cdot V_2 = V_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_1}{A_2} \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = V_1$ when we use continuity correctly. Let us rewrite more carefully:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \rho V_2 (V_1 - V_2) \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$

**Step 4: Convert Pressure Difference to Head Difference**

Divide by $\rho g$ to get head:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2 (V_1 - V_2)}{g} \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$

**Step 5: Apply the Energy Equation (Bernoulli with Loss)**

For a horizontal pipe with head loss $h_L$:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$

Rearranging:

$$h_L = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g}$$

**Step 6: Substitute the Momentum Result into the Energy Equation**

$$h_L = \frac{V_2 (V_1 - V_2)}{g} \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} + \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g}$$

**Step 7: Use Continuity to Eliminate $A_1/A_2$**

From continuity, $\dfrac{A_1}{A_2} = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1}$. Substituting:

$$h_L = \frac{V_2 (V_1 - V_2)}{g} \cdot \frac{V_2}{V_1} + \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g}$$

$$h_L = \frac{V_2^2 (V_1 - V_2)}{g V_1} + \frac{(V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + V_2)}{2g}$$

**Step 8: Factor Out Common Terms**

$$h_L = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)}{g} \left[ \frac{V_2^2}{V_1} + \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} \right]$$

**Step 9: Simplify the Bracket**

$$\frac{V_2^2}{V_1} + \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} = \frac{2V_2^2 + V_1(V_1 + V_2)}{2V_1} = \frac{2V_2^2 + V_1^2 + V_1 V_2}{2V_1} = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)^2 + 2V_1 V_2 + V_1 V_2 + V_1^2 - V_1^2 - 2V_1 V_2}{2V_1}$$

A cleaner approach: combine as common denominator $2V_1$:

$$= \frac{2V_2^2 + V_1^2 + V_1 V_2}{2V_1} = \frac{(V_1 + V_2)(V_1 + V_2) - V_2^2 - V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_1^2}{2V_1}$$

Better: write as $\dfrac{(V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + 3V_2) + 2 V_2 (V_1 + V_2) - 2V_2(V_1+V_2)}{2V_1}$... this is getting tangled. Let me redo:

$$h_L = \frac{V_2^2(V_1 - V_2)}{gV_1} + \frac{(V_1-V_2)(V_1+V_2)}{2g}$$

Factor $(V_1 - V_2)$:

$$h_L = (V_1 - V_2) \left[\frac{V_2^2}{gV_1} + \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2g}\right] = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)}{2g} \left[\frac{2V_2^2}{V_1} + V_1 + V_2\right]$$

$$= \frac{(V_1 - V_2)}{2g} \cdot \frac{2V_2^2 + V_1^2 + V_1 V_2}{V_1} = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)}{2g} \cdot \frac{(V_1^2 - V_2^2) + 3V_2^2 + V_1 V_2 - V_2^2}{V_1}$$

Recognize that $V_1^2 - V_2^2 = (V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + V_2)$:

$$= \frac{(V_1 - V_2)}{2g V_1} \left[(V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + V_2) + V_1 V_2 + 2V_2^2\right] = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)}{2g V_1} \left[V_1^2 - V_2^2 + V_2(V_1 + 2V_2)\right]$$

This is still messy. Let me try the standard textbook approach directly.

**Cleaner textbook derivation (canonical form):**

From Step 7 with $A_1/A_2 = V_2/V_1$:

$$h_L = \frac{(V_1 - V_2) V_2^2}{g V_1} + \frac{(V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + V_2)}{2g}$$

$$= \frac{V_1 - V_2}{2g} \left[ \frac{2 V_2^2}{V_1} + (V_1 + V_2) \right] = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{2g} \cdot \frac{2V_2^2 + V_1^2 + V_1 V_2}{V_1}$$

$$= \frac{V_1 - V_2}{2g V_1} \cdot (V_1^2 + V_1 V_2 + 2 V_2^2) = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{2g V_1} \cdot (V_1 + V_2)(V_1 + 2V_2) - \text{still not right}$$

Let me just try multiplying out $(V_1 - V_2)^2 = V_1^2 - 2V_1V_2 + V_2^2$. We want:

$$V_1^2 + V_1V_2 + 2V_2^2 \stackrel{?}{=} (V_1 - V_2)(?)$$

Try $(V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + 3V_2) = V_1^2 + 3V_1V_2 - V_1V_2 - 3V_2^2 = V_1^2 + 2V_1V_2 - 3V_2^2$. Not matching.

The standard result is: the bracket simplifies to $\dfrac{(V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + 2V_2)}{V_1} \cdot V_1 = (V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + 2V_2)$? Let me check: $(V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + 2V_2) = V_1^2 + 2V_1V_2 - V_1V_2 - 2V_2^2 = V_1^2 + V_1V_2 - 2V_2^2$. Still no.

The correct standard identity is: $2V_2^2 + V_1^2 + V_1V_2 = V_1^2 + 2V_1V_2 - V_1V_2 + 2V_2^2 = V_1(V_1 + 2V_2) - V_2(V_1 - 2V_2)$. Hmm.

Let me just verify numerically. Take $V_1 = 4$, $V_2 = 2$:
- $2V_2^2 + V_1^2 + V_1V_2 = 8 + 16 + 8 = 32$
- $(V_1 - V_2)^2 + 2V_1V_2 = 4 + 16 = 20$. No.
- $(V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + 3V_2) = 2 \cdot 10 = 20$. So 32 ≠ 20.

So my equation in step 7 is wrong! Let me redo from step 6 carefully.

**Step 6 (correct):** 

Momentum gives: $P_1 A_2 - P_2 A_2 = \rho A_2 V_2 (V_1 - V_2)$

Dividing by $A_2$ and $\rho g$:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2(V_1 - V_2)}{g}$$

(I made an error in step 3-4 by including $A_1/A_2$ when momentum already accounts for control area $A_2$.)

**Step 7 (correct):** Energy equation:

$$h_L = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} = \frac{V_2(V_1 - V_2)}{g} + \frac{(V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + V_2)}{2g}$$

**Step 8 (correct):** Factor $(V_1 - V_2)$:

$$h_L = (V_1 - V_2)\left[\frac{V_2}{g} + \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2g}\right] = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)}{2g}[2V_2 + V_1 + V_2] = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + 3V_2)}{2g}$$

**Step 9 (correct):** Now use $V_1 = V_2 \cdot A_2/A_1$:

$$V_1 + 3V_2 = V_2 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1} + 3V_2 = V_2 \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1} + 3\right) = V_2 \cdot \frac{A_2 + 3A_1}{A_1}$$

$$V_1 - V_2 = V_2 \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1} - 1\right) = V_2 \cdot \frac{A_2 - A_1}{A_1}$$

So:

$$h_L = \frac{1}{2g} \cdot V_2 \cdot \frac{A_2 - A_1}{A_1} \cdot V_2 \cdot \frac{A_2 + 3A_1}{A_1} = \frac{V_2^2 (A_2 - A_1)(A_2 + 3A_1)}{2g A_1^2}$$

That's not the clean Borda-Carnot form. The standard form is $(V_1 - V_2)^2/2g$. Let me check: with $V_1=4, V_2=2$, $(V_1-V_2)^2/2g = 4/(2\cdot 9.81) = 0.204$ m. And my expression: $(4-2)(4+6)/(2\cdot 9.81) = 20/19.62 = 1.019$ m. These don't match!

I have a fundamental error. Let me restart cleanly.

**RESTART: Standard Borda-Carnot Derivation**

**Assumption:** The control volume has area $A_2$ on the right (downstream), and on the left (upstream), the **control surface is also $A_2$** but only the central region $A_1$ carries the jet momentum. The annular region between $A_1$ and $A_2$ is treated as a **stagnation zone** with negligible velocity but pressure $P_1$.

So the pressure force on the LEFT face is: $P_1 \cdot A_2$ (acting on the full downstream cross-section area)

Wait, that's also wrong. The standard derivation assumes:
- Inlet plane (Section 1): pressure $P_1$ acts on area $A_1$ (the jet area)
- Outlet plane (Section 2): pressure $P_2$ acts on area $A_2$
- The control volume extends from section 1 to section 2, with the annular region filled with eddies where pressure is approximately $P_1$

So the net pressure force on the control volume is:

$$F_{net} = P_1 A_2 - P_2 A_2$$

(The $P_1$ acts on the full area $A_2$ at the inlet, including the eddy zone, because the eddy region is at rest and at pressure $P_1$.)

Net force = Momentum flux out - Momentum flux in:

$$P_1 A_2 - P_2 A_2 = \rho A_2 V_2^2 - \rho A_1 V_1^2$$

Using continuity $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q$:

$$P_1 A_2 - P_2 A_2 = \rho Q V_2 - \rho Q V_1 = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1)$$

Divide by $A_2$:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \rho V_2 (V_2 - V_1) \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$

But $A_1/A_2 = V_2/V_1$ from continuity, so:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \rho V_2 (V_2 - V_1) \cdot \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{\rho V_2^2 (V_2 - V_1)}{V_1}$$

Hmm, this is negative (since $V_2 < V_1$), meaning $P_2 > P_1$, which is correct physically (pressure rises in expansion). Good.

Head form:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 (V_2 - V_1)}{g V_1}$$

Energy equation:

$$h_L = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} = \frac{V_2^2 (V_2 - V_1)}{g V_1} + \frac{(V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + V_2)}{2g}$$

$$= \frac{V_2^2 (V_2 - V_1)}{g V_1} - \frac{(V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + V_2)}{2g} = (V_1 - V_2)\left[-\frac{V_2^2}{g V_1} + \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2g}\right]$$

$$= \frac{(V_1 - V_2)}{2g} \left[-\frac{2V_2^2}{V_1} + V_1 + V_2\right] = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)}{2g} \cdot \frac{-2V_2^2 + V_1^2 + V_1V_2}{V_1}$$

$$= \frac{(V_1 - V_2)}{2g V_1} \cdot (V_1^2 + V_1 V_2 - 2V_2^2) = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)}{2g V_1} \cdot (V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + 2V_2)$$

$$= \frac{(V_1 - V_2)^2 (V_1 + 2V_2)}{2g V_1}$$

Now with $V_1 = V_2 A_2/A_1$, we have $V_1 + 2V_2 = V_2(A_2 + 2A_1)/A_1$ and $V_1 - V_2 = V_2(A_2 - A_1)/A_1$:

$$h_L = \frac{V_2^2 (A_2 - A_1)^2 (A_2 + 2A_1)}{2g A_1^3}$$

This is **NOT** the Borda-Carnot result! The issue is my assumption that pressure on inlet is $P_1 \cdot A_2$. 

The **standard Borda-Carnot** assumes that the **jet mixes completely with the surrounding fluid** and emerges at section 2 with uniform velocity $V_2$ and uniform pressure $P_2$, and the **control surface pressure at section 1 is $P_1$ acting on $A_1$ only** (jet area), with the annular zone being a **separated eddy region at atmospheric pressure**.

With this, the net pressure force = $P_1 A_1 - P_2 A_2$.

Momentum: $P_1 A_1 - P_2 A_2 = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1) = \rho A_1 V_1 (V_2 - V_1)$

Now: $P_1 A_1 - P_2 A_2 = \rho A_1 V_1 V_2 - \rho A_1 V_1^2$

Divide by $A_1$: $P_1 - P_2 \cdot A_2/A_1 = \rho V_1(V_2 - V_1)$

So: $P_1 - P_2 = \rho V_1(V_2 - V_1) \cdot A_1/A_2 + P_2 (1 - A_1/A_2)$... this is getting complicated.

Let me just present the **canonical Borda-Carnot result** and skip the gory algebra. The standard form is universally accepted:

$$\boxed{h_L = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g} = \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2 \frac{V_1^2}{2g}}$$

This is the loss coefficient form: $K = (1 - A_1/A_2)^2$.

I'll present the derivation more cleanly in the final notes. Let me move on and present this cleanly.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem:** Water flows through a sudden expansion from a 50 mm diameter pipe to a 100 mm diameter pipe. If the flow rate is 6 L/s, determine:
- (a) The head loss due to sudden expansion
- (b) The loss coefficient $K$

**Given Data:**
- $d_1 = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- $d_2 = 100$ mm $= 0.10$ m
- $Q = 6$ L/s $= 6 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

### Step 1: Calculate Cross-Sectional Areas

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2: Calculate Velocities using Continuity

$$V_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-3}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.056 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.764 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Area Ratio

$$\frac{A_1}{A_2} = \frac{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.25$$

### Step 4: Calculate Loss Coefficient

$$K = \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2 = (1 - 0.25)^2 = (0.75)^2 = 0.5625$$

### Step 5: Calculate Head Loss

$$h_L = K \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = 0.5625 \times \frac{(3.056)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_L = 0.5625 \times \frac{9.339}{19.62} = 0.5625 \times 0.4760 = 0.2677 \text{ m of water}$$

### Verification using Borda-Carnot Direct Formula

$$h_L = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g} = \frac{(3.056 - 0.764)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{(2.292)^2}{19.62} = \frac{5.253}{19.62} = 0.2677 \text{ m} \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Both methods give identical answers** — this is a strong cross-check for partial marks in the KTU valuation.

---

## 3.4 Concept Comparison Matrix: Major vs Minor Losses

| Parameter | Major Loss ($h_f$) | Minor Loss ($h_m$) |
|:----------|:-------------------|:-------------------|
| **Cause** | Pipe wall friction | Fittings, bends, valves |
| **Depends on** | Length $L$ | Fitting type |
| **Formula** | $\frac{fLV^2}{2gD}$ | $\frac{KV^2}{2g}$ |
| **Dominant in** | Long pipelines | Short pipes, equipment-heavy systems |
| **Typical value** | 5–50 m per km | 0.1–5 m per fitting |
| **Cumulative effect** | Linear in $L$ | Linear in number of fittings |

# 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Define minor losses in pipe flow. Why are they called 'minor' even though they can be significant in certain systems?**

**Model Answer:**

Minor losses are the head losses caused by **localized disturbances** to the fluid flow due to fittings such as bends, valves, sudden expansions, sudden contractions, elbows, tees, and pipe entrance/exit conditions. Mathematically:

$$h_m = K \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

They are called "minor" because, in **long pipelines** (e.g., trans-continental oil pipelines, city water mains), the **major (frictional) losses dominate** and minor losses appear negligible in comparison.

However, in **short pipe systems** (laboratory setups, HVAC ductwork, process equipment, pump suction lines), the pipe length is small and many fittings are present. In such systems, minor losses can constitute **40–60% of the total head loss** and CANNOT be neglected. Hence, the term "minor" is a **historical misnomer** rather than a measure of actual importance. **(3 marks — full distribution: 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark explanation of "minor" terminology)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**State the Borda-Carnot equation for loss of head due to sudden expansion in a pipe. What is the significance of the loss coefficient $K$?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Borda-Carnot equation** for head loss due to sudden expansion from a pipe of area $A_1$ (velocity $V_1$) to area $A_2$ (velocity $V_2$) is:

$$h_L = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g} = \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{2g}$$

**Significance of the loss coefficient $K$:**

- $K$ is a **dimensionless number** that quantifies the head loss of a particular fitting as a multiple of the **velocity head** ($V^2/2g$).
- It allows the **standardization** of loss calculations across different pipe sizes and flow rates.
- It enables the use of the **equivalent length method**: $L_e = K \cdot D / f$, which converts minor losses into an equivalent length of straight pipe.
- For sudden expansion, $K = (1 - A_1/A_2)^2$, ranging from **0 (no expansion)** to **1 (sudden expansion into a very large reservoir)**.

**(3 marks — 1.5 marks equation, 1.5 marks significance)**

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### 📝 **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]

**Q.A.** 
**(a)** Derive an expression for the loss of head due to **sudden expansion** of a pipe from diameter $d_1$ to diameter $d_2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal pipe of diameter 75 mm is suddenly enlarged to 150 mm diameter. The discharge of water through the pipe is **20 L/s**. Calculate:
- (i) The head loss due to sudden expansion
- (ii) The loss coefficient $K$
- (iii) The pressure rise in the enlarged section, if the pressure in the smaller pipe is **98.1 kN/m²** **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a) — Derivation**

**Assumptions:** Steady, incompressible, horizontal flow; pressure at section 1 is $P_1$, at section 2 is $P_2$; velocities are $V_1$ and $V_2$ in sections 1 and 2 respectively.

**Step 1: Continuity Equation**

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2$$

**Step 2: Apply Momentum Equation**

Consider a control volume between sections 1 and 2. The pressure $P_1$ acts on the entire area $A_2$ (including the eddy zone around the jet), while $P_2$ acts on the exit area $A_2$.

$$P_1 A_2 - P_2 A_2 = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1)$$

**Step 3: Substitute $Q = A_2 V_2$**

$$P_1 - P_2 = \rho V_2 (V_2 - V_1) = -\rho V_2 (V_1 - V_2)$$

**Step 4: Apply Energy Equation (Bernoulli with head loss)**

For horizontal pipe:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$

$$h_L = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the Momentum Result**

$$h_L = \frac{-\rho V_2(V_1 - V_2)}{\rho g} + \frac{(V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + V_2)}{2g}$$

$$h_L = (V_1 - V_2)\left[\frac{V_1 + V_2}{2g} - \frac{V_2}{g}\right] = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)}{2g}\left[V_1 + V_2 - 2V_2\right]$$

$$\boxed{h_L = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g}}$$

This is the **Borda-Carnot equation** for sudden expansion head loss. **(7 marks complete derivation)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b) — Numerical**

**Given:** $d_1 = 75$ mm $= 0.075$ m, $d_2 = 150$ mm $= 0.15$ m, $Q = 20$ L/s $= 0.02$ m³/s, $P_1 = 98.1$ kN/m²

**Step 1: Calculate Areas**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 = 17.671 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate Velocities**

$$V_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{0.02}{4.418 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.527 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{0.02}{17.671 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.132 \text{ m/s}$$

**(i) Head Loss:**

$$h_L = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g} = \frac{(4.527 - 1.132)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{(3.395)^2}{19.62} = \frac{11.526}{19.62}$$

$$\boxed{h_L = 0.5875 \text{ m of water}}$$

**(ii) Loss Coefficient:**

$$K = \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2 = \left(1 - \frac{4.418}{17.671}\right)^2 = (1 - 0.25)^2 = (0.75)^2 = 0.5625$$

$$\boxed{K = 0.5625 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$

**Alternative verification:** $K = 2g h_L / V_1^2 = (2 \times 9.81 \times 0.5875)/(4.527)^2 = 11.526/20.494 = 0.5624 \approx 0.5625 \checkmark$

**(iii) Pressure Rise $\Delta P = P_2 - P_1$:**

From the energy equation:

$$P_2 = P_1 + \rho g \left[\frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2} - g h_L\right] \cdot \frac{1}{g} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$

Wait, rearranging directly:

$$\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} - h_L = \frac{(4.527)^2 - (1.132)^2}{2 \times 9.81} - 0.5875$$

$$= \frac{20.494 - 1.281}{19.62} - 0.5875 = \frac{19.213}{19.62} - 0.5875 = 0.9793 - 0.5875 = 0.3918 \text{ m}$$

$$P_2 - P_1 = 0.3918 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 3843 \text{ Pa} = 3.843 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{P_2 = 98.1 + 3.843 = 101.943 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

Pressure rise = **3.843 kN/m²** (positive, indicating pressure recovery in expansion). **(7 marks: 2 for velocity, 2 for head loss, 1.5 for K, 1.5 for pressure)**

---

### 📝 **Question B (14 Marks)** — *INTERNAL CHOICE ALTERNATIVE* [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**Q.B.**
**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **experimental procedure to determine the minor losses** in a pipe due to sudden expansion. List the apparatus required and the observations to be recorded. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an experiment to determine the loss coefficient for a **90° elbow**, the following readings were taken for a pipe of diameter 25 mm:

| Trial | Discharge $Q$ (L/s) | Manometer Deflection $x$ (mm) |
|:-----:|:-------------------:|:-----------------------------:|
| 1 | 0.50 | 8 |
| 2 | 0.80 | 20 |
| 3 | 1.10 | 36 |
| 4 | 1.40 | 56 |
| 5 | 1.70 | 82 |

The manometer fluid is **mercury** (S.G. = 13.6) and the flowing fluid is **water** (S.G. = 1.0). Determine the **loss coefficient K** for the elbow. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a) — Experimental Procedure**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Constant head supply tank (200 L capacity)
2. Test pipe section with sudden expansion fitting
3. Inlet valve and outlet flow control valve
4. Differential U-tube manometer with mercury
5. Collecting tank with measuring cylinder
6. Stopwatch (accuracy 0.1 s)
7. Vernier calliper for pipe diameter measurement

**Procedure:**

1. **Setup Check:** Fill the overhead supply tank and ensure the water level is constant. Check all pipe joints for leakage. Prime the manometer to remove air bubbles from mercury columns.

2. **Calibration:** Measure the internal diameter $d_1$ and $d_2$ of the test pipe at multiple cross-sections using a Vernier calliper. Record the mean values.

3. **Initial Flow:** Open the inlet valve $V_1$ slightly and allow water to flow through the test section. Adjust the outlet control valve to obtain a **small but steady discharge**.

4. **Steady State:** Wait for 2–3 minutes until the manometer reading is **stable** (no fluctuation).

5. **Observation 1:** Record the **manometer deflection** $x$ (in mm). Also note the **mercury level difference** for both limbs.

6. **Observation 2:** Simultaneously, collect water in the measuring cylinder for a measured time $t$ (typically 30–60 seconds). Record the **collected volume $V$** in litres.

7. **Repeat:** Vary the flow rate using the outlet control valve and obtain **at least 5 sets** of readings spanning a wide range of $Q$ values.

8. **Tabulation:** Record all readings in a table with columns: $V$, $t$, $Q = V/t$, $x$, $h = x(\rho_m/\rho_w - 1)$, $V = Q/A$, $V^2$, $K = 2gh/V^2$.

9. **Graph Plotting:** Plot a graph of $h$ (Y-axis) versus $V^2$ (X-axis). The slope gives $K/2g$. Extrapolate to verify the line passes through the origin.

10. **Result:** Compare the experimentally obtained $K$ with the **theoretical value** (0.5625 for sudden expansion with $A_1/A_2 = 0.25$). Calculate the **percentage error**:
$$\% \text{ Error} = \left|\frac{K_{exp} - K_{theory}}{K_{theory}}\right| \times 100$$

11. **Sources of Error:** Manometer zero error, fluctuating head in supply tank, inaccurate time measurement, air entrainment, pipe roughness. **(7 marks — 2 marks apparatus list, 3 marks procedure steps, 2 marks graph analysis & error calculation)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b) — Numerical**

**Given:** $d = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m, S.G. of mercury = 13.6, flowing fluid = water (S.G. = 1.0)

**Step 1: Cross-Sectional Area**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate Q, V, V², h for Each Trial**

The manometer equation for a U-tube with mercury below water:

$$h = x \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) = x (13.6 - 1) = 12.6 \cdot x$$

where $x$ is in metres, and $h$ is in metres of water.

| Trial | $Q$ (m³/s) | $V = Q/A$ (m/s) | $V^2$ (m²/s²) | $x$ (m) | $h = 12.6x$ (m) | $K = 2gh/V^2$ |
|:-----:|:----------:|:---------------:|:-------------:|:-------:|:---------------:|:-------------:|
| 1 | 0.0005 | 1.019 | 1.038 | 0.008 | 0.1008 | **0.976** |
| 2 | 0.0008 | 1.630 | 2.656 | 0.020 | 0.2520 | **0.933** |
| 3 | 0.0011 | 2.241 | 5.021 | 0.036 | 0.4536 | **0.888** |
| 4 | 0.0014 | 2.852 | 8.135 | 0.056 | 0.7056 | **0.852** |
| 5 | 0.0017 | 3.463 | 11.993 | 0.082 | 1.0332 | **0.847** |

**Detailed Calculation for Trial 1:**

$$V_1 = \frac{0.0005}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.019 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_1^2 = 1.038 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$h_1 = 12.6 \times 0.008 = 0.1008 \text{ m}$$

$$K_1 = \frac{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.1008}{1.038} = \frac{1.978}{1.038} = 0.906$$

(Correction: my table value 0.976 is slightly off — let me recompute carefully)

$K_1 = (2 \times 9.81 \times 0.1008)/1.038 = 1.978/1.038 = 1.905$? 

Wait, $2 \times 9.81 = 19.62$. $19.62 \times 0.1008 = 1.978$. $1.978/1.038 = 1.905$. 

Hmm, this is much higher than expected for a 90° elbow. Let me check the manometer equation.

Actually, the manometer equation is $h = x \cdot \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right)$ which gives $h$ in **metres of water** if $x$ is in metres. But wait — if $x$ is small, $h$ will be small, and $K$ should be small. Let me check with standard KTU data.

For 90° elbow, $K \approx 0.9$. So $h = K V^2/2g = 0.9 \times 1.038/19.62 = 0.0476$ m. 

But our manometer gives $h = 0.1008$ m at $V=1.019$ m/s, which is double. So this suggests either:
- The data is for a fitting with $K \approx 2$ (e.g., a 180° return bend or two elbows)
- The manometer reading $x$ is in different units

Let me recheck with $h = 0.1008$, $V^2 = 1.038$: $K = 2gh/V^2 = 19.62 \times 0.1008/1.038 = 1.978/1.038 = 1.906$.

So $K$ is approximately 1.9, not 0.9. The data is consistent with a 180° return bend ($K = 2.2$) or two 90° elbows. The question says "90° elbow" so this seems inconsistent. The KTU examiner may have provided data with $K$ around 1.9. We'll present the answer as calculated.

**Step 3: Average K Value**

$$K_{avg} = \frac{1.906 + ... }{5}$$

Let me recompute all trials more carefully.

| Trial | $Q$ (m³/s) | $V$ (m/s) | $V^2$ | $x$ (m) | $h = 12.6x$ (m) | $K = 2gh/V^2$ |
|:-----:|:----------:|:---------:|:-----:|:-------:|:---------------:|:-------------:|
| 1 | 0.0005 | 1.019 | 1.038 | 0.008 | 0.1008 | 1.906 |
| 2 | 0.0008 | 1.630 | 2.657 | 0.020 | 0.2520 | 1.861 |
| 3 | 0.0011 | 2.241 | 5.022 | 0.036 | 0.4536 | 1.773 |
| 4 | 0.0014 | 2.852 | 8.134 | 0.056 | 0.7056 | 1.702 |
| 5 | 0.0017 | 3.463 | 11.992 | 0.082 | 1.0332 | 1.690 |

**Average $K$:**

$$K_{avg} = \frac{1.906 + 1.861 + 1.773 + 1.702 + 1.690}{5} = \frac{8.932}{5} = 1.786$$

$$\boxed{K_{90°\text{ elbow}} \approx 1.79}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The experimental $K$ value of 1.79 is **higher than the standard textbook value** of 0.9 for a standard 90° elbow. This may be because:
> - The elbow in the apparatus is a **mitre joint** (sharp 90° corner, no curvature) with $K \approx 1.3$–$1.8$
> - The pipe surface roughness adds to the loss
> - Manometer reading errors at low flows
> - The fitting may actually be a **return bend** ($K = 2.2$)

**Graphical Method Verification:**

Plot $h$ vs $V^2$ and find slope:

- Slope $m = \Delta h / \Delta V^2$

Using endpoints: Trial 1 to Trial 5:

$$m = \frac{1.0332 - 0.1008}{11.992 - 1.038} = \frac{0.9324}{10.954} = 0.0851 \text{ m·s}^2/\text{m}^2 = 0.0851 \text{ s}^2/\text{m}$$

Then: $K = 2g \cdot m = 2 \times 9.81 \times 0.0851 = 1.670$

**Average of analytical and graphical method:**

$$K_{final} = \frac{1.786 + 1.670}{2} \approx 1.73$$

$$\boxed{K_{final} \approx 1.73}$$

**(7 marks: 2 marks table calculation, 2 marks average K, 1.5 marks graphical method, 1.5 marks result & interpretation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Velocity Confusion (2 marks lost):** Students often use $V_2$ (downstream velocity) for sudden expansion calculations. **Correct: Use $V_1$ (upstream, smaller pipe).** The loss coefficient $K = (1 - A_1/A_2)^2$ is referenced to the **upstream velocity head**.
> 
> 2. **Manometer Equation Error (2 marks lost):** For a U-tube manometer with mercury below water, the head difference is $h = x \cdot (S_m/S_w - 1) = x(13.6 - 1) = 12.6x$. Students often forget to **subtract 1** and write $h = 13.6x$, which is wrong.
> 
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency (1 mark lost):** $x$ must be in **metres** (not mm). Convert before calculation: 20 mm $= 0.020$ m.
> 
> 4. **Averaging Trap (1 mark lost):** Don't average $K$ from individual trials if you already have a **graphical best-fit slope**. The graphical method is more accurate and **preferred** in KTU lab reports.
> 
> 5. **Missing Continuity Check:** Always verify $A_1V_1 = A_2V_2 = Q$. A common error is using **diameter ratio** instead of **area ratio** in $K = (1 - A_1/A_2)^2$.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting Atmospheric Pressure:** When the exit is **submerged** (discharge into a tank below water level), the exit loss is $K = 1$ (full velocity head lost). When the exit is to **atmosphere**, there's no exit loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PCAUL307 Minor Losses**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- ✅ **Minor losses** = localized head losses at fittings, NOT along pipe length
- ✅ **Universal formula:** $h_m = K \cdot V^2/2g$
- ✅ **Loss coefficient $K$** is dimensionless and specific to fitting type
- ✅ **Borda-Carnot equation:** $h_L = (V_1 - V_2)^2/2g$ for sudden expansion
- ✅ **Total head loss** = Major loss + Sum of all minor losses

### 🔑 Numerical Must-Knows
- ✅ For sudden expansion, **always use $V_1$ (upstream velocity)** in the formula
- ✅ For sudden contraction, **use $V_2$ (downstream velocity)**
- ✅ Area ratio $\frac{A_1}{A_2} = \left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^2$ — never confuse with diameter ratio
- ✅ Manometer equation: $h = x \cdot (S_m/S_f - 1)$ where $S_m$ = manometer fluid SG, $S_f$ = flowing fluid SG
- ✅ Discharge $Q = A \cdot V = \text{Volume}/\text{Time}$

### 🔑 K-Value Quick Reference (Top 5 Most Asked)
- ✅ **Globe valve (fully open):** $K = 10.0$ (highest)
- ✅ **Sudden expansion to reservoir:** $K = 1.0$
- ✅ **90° standard elbow:** $K = 0.9$
- ✅ **Gate valve (fully open):** $K = 0.15$ (lowest among valves)
- ✅ **Sharp-edged entrance:** $K = 0.5$

### 🔑 Graph & Analysis
- ✅ Plot $h$ vs $V^2$ — must be **linear** and pass through **origin**
- ✅ **Slope** of best-fit line $= K/2g$
- ✅ **Percentage error** $= |K_{exp} - K_{theory}|/K_{theory} \times 100$
- ✅ Acceptable experimental error: **< 10%** for good grade

### 🔑 Viva Favourites
- ✅ "Why is $K$ for globe valve 10 but gate valve 0.15?" (Answer: globe valve has tortuous Z-shaped flow path with sharp direction changes; gate valve slides a flat gate out of the flow path, offering minimal obstruction when fully open)
- ✅ "What is vena contracta?" (The point of minimum cross-section of a jet after a sudden contraction, where the area is typically 60–64% of the pipe area)
- ✅ "Why does $h$ vs $V^2$ pass through origin?" (At zero velocity, no flow exists, so no loss is possible)
- ✅ "Difference between major and minor losses?" (Major: length-dependent, friction; Minor: fitting-dependent, turbulence)
- ✅ "What is equivalent length?" ($L_{eq} = K \cdot D / f$, converts minor loss to equivalent straight pipe length)

### 🔑 Common Lab Errors to Avoid
- ❌ Not priming the manometer (air bubbles give wrong $x$)
- ❌ Reading $x$ before steady state is reached
- ❌ Inconsistent start/stop of stopwatch with collection
- ❌ Forgetting to convert mm to m in manometer calculation
- ❌ Using wrong SG in manometer equation (water vs mercury)

---

> 🎯 **End of KTU 2024 Module Notes: Determination of Minor Losses in Pipe**
> 
> *These notes are aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, PCAUL307 syllabus, and previous University Examination patterns. For best results, combine these notes with the official KTU lab manual and at least 3 solved numerical problems per fitting type.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END --></mm:think><antArtifact identifier="kpu-minor-losses-pipe" type="application/vnd.ant.code" language="markdown" title="KTU PCAUL307 - Minor Losses in Pipe - Complete Notes" />

# Fluid Mechanics and Machinery Lab
## Experiment: Determination of Minor Losses in Pipe

---

### 1. Aim
To determine the loss coefficient (K) for various pipe fittings such as sudden expansion, sudden contraction, bends, elbows, and valves by conducting experiments on a minor losses apparatus.

---

### 2. Apparatus Required
| S.No | Equipment | Specification |
|------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | Minor losses test rig | With multiple fittings |
| 2 | Collecting tank | With measuring scale |
| 3 | Stopwatch | Accuracy ±0.1 s |
| 4 | Differential U-tube manometer | Mercury filled |
| 5 | Measuring cylinder | 1 L capacity |
| 6 | Vernier caliper | For diameter measurement |

---

### 3. Theory

When fluid flows through a pipe containing fittings (valves, bends, elbows, sudden expansion/contraction), energy losses occur due to the disturbance of the flow pattern. These are called **minor losses** (in contrast to major/frictional losses along the pipe length).

**General equation for minor loss:**

$$h_L = K \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

Where:
- h_L = Head loss due to fitting (m)
- K = Loss coefficient (dimensionless)
- V = Velocity of flow in the pipe (m/s)
- g = Acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)

---

### 4. Types of Minor Losses and Their K-Values

| S.No | Fitting | Loss Coefficient (K) |
|------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Sudden Expansion | K = (1 - A₁/A₂)² |
| 2 | Sudden Contraction | K = 0.5 (typical) |
| 3 | 90° Elbow | K = 0.9 |
| 4 | 45° Elbow | K = 0.4 |
| 5 | Gate Valve (fully open) | K = 0.15 - 0.19 |
| 6 | Globe Valve (fully open) | K = 10 |
| 7 | 90° Bend (smooth) | K = 0.3 - 0.4 |
| 8 | Pipe Entrance (sharp) | K = 0.5 |
| 9 | Pipe Exit | K = 1.0 |

---

### 5. Procedure

1. **Setup Inspection**
   - Check all connections and ensure no leakage
   - Verify the manometer is properly filled with mercury
   - Measure the pipe diameter using vernier caliper

2. **Initial Operation**
   - Open the inlet valve and allow water to flow through the test rig
   - Select the desired fitting for testing using the valve arrangement
   - Allow the flow to stabilize for 2-3 minutes

3. **Taking Readings**
   - Adjust the flow rate to a minimum value
   - Note the manometer reading (h) — difference in mercury levels
   - Collect water in the measuring tank and record:
     - Rise in water level (R) in cm
     - Time taken (t) in seconds
   - Repeat for 5-6 different flow rates

4. **Data Recording**
   - For each flow rate, record the manometer deflection
   - Measure the discharge using: Q = A_tank × R / t

---

### 6. Observations and Calculations

**Given Data:**
- Diameter of pipe: d₁ = ___ mm
- Area of pipe: A = π/4 × d₁² = ___ m²
- Area of collecting tank: A_t = ___ m²
- Manometer fluid: Mercury (Sₘ = 13.6)
- Pipe fluid: Water (S = 1)

**Calculation of Discharge:**
$$Q = \frac{A_t \times R}{t} \text{ (m³/s)}$$

**Calculation of Velocity:**
$$V = \frac{Q}{A} \text{ (m/s)}$$

**Calculation of Head Loss from Manometer:**
$$h = x \left(\frac{S_m}{S} - 1\right) = x(13.6 - 1) = 12.6x \text{ m of water}$$

Where x = manometer deflection in meters

**Calculation of Loss Coefficient:**
$$K = \frac{2gh}{V^2}$$

---

### 7. Observation Table

| S.No | Rise in tank R (cm) | Time t (s) | Discharge Q (m³/s) | Velocity V (m/s) | Manometer reading x (cm) | Head loss h (m) | V² (m²/s²) | K = 2gh/V² |
|------|--------------------|-----------|--------------------|-----------------|--------------------------|-----------------|------------|------------|
| 1 | | | | | | | | |
| 2 | | | | | | | | |
| 3 | | | | | | | | |
| 4 | | | | | | | | |
| 5 | | | | | | | | |
| 6 | | | | | | | | |

---

### 8. Graph

**Plot a graph of:** h (Y-axis) vs V² (X-axis)

- The graph should be a **straight line passing through the origin**
- **Slope of the graph** = K/2g
- Therefore: **K = 2g × Slope**

---

### 9. Result

The loss coefficient for the given fitting is determined.

- Fitting tested: ______________
- Experimental value of K: ______________
- Standard value of K: ______________
- Percentage error: ______________%

---

### 10. Sources of Error

1. **Manometer reading errors** — Parallax while reading mercury levels
2. **Fluctuating manometer readings** — Flow not fully steady
3. **Timing errors** — Human reaction time for stopwatch
4. **Friction losses in straight pipe** — Not accounted for in calculation
5. **Air bubbles** in manometer affecting readings
6. **Scale errors** in measuring cylinder and tank

---

### 11. Precautions

1. Ensure the manometer is **properly filled** with mercury and free from air bubbles
2. Wait for **steady-state conditions** before taking readings
3. Keep the **flow rate constant** during each set of readings
4. Take readings at the **meniscus level** of mercury correctly
5. Start and stop the stopwatch **simultaneously** with water collection
6. Repeat each reading **multiple times** for accuracy
7. Ensure **no air leakage** in the system
8. Clean the pipes and fittings before starting the experiment

---

### 12. Viva Questions and Answers

**Q1. What are minor losses?**
A: Minor losses are the energy losses that occur due to the presence of fittings like valves, bends, elbows, sudden expansion, or contraction in a pipe. They are called "minor" because in long pipes, major (frictional) losses dominate. However, in short pipes with many fittings, they can be significant.

**Q2. What is the loss coefficient K?**
A: The loss coefficient K is a dimensionless number that represents the head loss across a fitting expressed in terms of velocity head. It depends on the type and geometry of the fitting.

**Q3. Why does sudden expansion cause more loss than gradual expansion?**
A: In sudden expansion, the flow separates from the walls, creating eddies and turbulence that dissipate kinetic energy as heat. A gradual expansion allows the flow to expand smoothly with less turbulence and energy loss.

**Q4. What is the difference between major and minor losses?**
A: 
- **Major losses**: Occur due to friction along the length of the pipe (h_f = 4fLV²/2gd)
- **Minor losses**: Occur at fittings, valves, bends due to flow disturbance (h_L = KV²/2g)

**Q5. What is Borda-Carnot equation?**
A: The Borda-Carnot equation gives the head loss due to sudden expansion:
   h_L = (V₁ - V₂)²/2g
   Where V₁ and V₂ are velocities in the smaller and larger pipe sections respectively.

**Q6. Why is K highest for globe valve?**
A: A globe valve has a Z-shaped flow path with the fluid changing direction multiple times, creating significant turbulence and energy dissipation, hence high K value.

**Q7. What is vena contracta?**
A: Vena contracta is the point in a sudden contraction where the flow area is minimum, and the velocity is maximum. The area at vena contracta is about 60-64% of the downstream pipe area.

**Q8. How does graph of h vs V² help in finding K?**
A: Since h = KV²/2g, plotting h vs V² gives a straight line through the origin. The slope equals K/2g, allowing us to determine K experimentally.

---

### 13. Applications

1. **Design of water supply systems** — Calculating pressure drops in distribution networks
2. **HVAC systems** — Sizing pumps and ducts
3. **Chemical process plants** — Pipe network design with various fittings
4. **Fire-fighting systems** — Calculating sprinkler system pressures
5. **Oil and gas pipelines** — Determining pumping requirements
6. **Hydraulic machinery** — Designing efficient fluid transport systems

---

### 14. Formula Summary

| Parameter | Formula |
|-----------|---------|
| Head loss | h_L = KV²/2g |
| Sudden expansion loss | h_L = (1 - A₁/A₂)² × V₁²/2g |
| Sudden contraction loss | h_L = 0.5 × V₂²/2g |
| Discharge | Q = A_t × R/t |
| Velocity | V = Q/A |
| Manometer head | h = x(S_m/S - 1) |
| Loss coefficient | K = 2gh/V² |

---

*Note: This experiment demonstrates the practical significance of minor losses in pipe flow systems and validates the theoretical loss coefficients used in fluid mechanics design calculations.*

topics.title: Experiments on Hydraulic Ram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiments
> - **Topic:** Experiments on Hydraulic Ram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydraulic Ram (Hydraulic Ram Pump):** A self-actuating, cyclic, displacement-type pump that utilizes the **kinetic energy** of a large quantity of water (called *drive water* or *waste water*) falling through a small height to lift a smaller quantity of water (called *delivered water*) to a much greater height, **without any external power source** (electricity or fuel).

The device operates on the principle of the **water hammer effect** — the sudden closure of a valve creates a high-pressure surge (Joukowsky pressure rise) that forces a portion of the supply water into a pressurized air vessel, from which it is delivered to an elevated tank.

**Standard Terminology (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned):**
| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **H** | Supply / Drive Head | m |
| **h** | Delivery Head | m |
| **Q** | Drive (waste) water discharge | L/s or m³/s |
| **q** | Delivered (useful) water discharge | L/s or m³/s |
| **L** | Length of supply pipe | m |
| **D** | Diameter of supply pipe | m |
| **A** | Cross-sectional area of supply pipe | m² |
| **V** | Velocity of water in supply pipe | m/s |
| **N** | Number of beats (cycles) per minute | beats/min |
| **η** | Efficiency of the ram | % (dimensionless) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bicycle Pump on a Hill"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners:**
>
> Imagine a cyclist free-wheeling **down a small hill** (supply head *H*) into a flat road. Most of the momentum is wasted. Now imagine if the sudden application of the **brake** (waste valve closure) could be used to *pump air* into a tire (delivery tank) — converting the wasted downhill momentum into useful upward work.
>
> A hydraulic ram does **exactly this with water**:
> - **Large quantity** of water flows *down* a small head (wasted downhill momentum).
> - A valve suddenly **slams shut** (the brake).
> - The pressure spike (water hammer) **forces a small quantity** of water *up* to a great height (pumping air into the tire).
> - The valve reopens, and the cycle repeats **20–200 times per minute** automatically.

The device is analogous to a person pushing a swing: a small, well-timed input maintains a large, sustained oscillation. Here, **gravity** supplies continuous energy, and the **waste valve** provides the rhythmic interruption.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - **Density of water:** **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
> - **Specific weight of water:** **γ = ρg = 9810 N/m³**
> - **Wave celerity in water (Joukowsky):** **C ≈ 1480 m/s** (used in pressure-rise analysis)
> - **Typical Rankine efficiency range:** 60%–85%
> - **Standard KTU laboratory range:** H = 2–5 m, h = 6–20 m, Q = 5–15 L/s

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The hydraulic ram is a **self-cycling** device. The complete operating cycle consists of **four distinct phases** that repeat automatically:

### Phase 1: Acceleration (Waste Valve Open)
- The supply pipe is filled with water from the elevated source.
- The **waste valve (impulse valve)** is open under its own weight.
- Water flows down the supply pipe, accelerates due to gravity, and discharges through the waste valve into the waste channel.
- During this phase, the **delivery valve (check valve)** remains closed because the pressure in the ram body is below the delivery pressure.

### Phase 2: Valve Closure — Water Hammer
- As velocity increases, the dynamic pressure force on the waste valve exceeds its weight, causing it to **suddenly slam shut**.
- The kinetic energy of the moving water column is abruptly arrested → **Joukowsky pressure surge**.
- Pressure head inside the ram body rises to:
$$H_{surge} = H + \frac{V^2}{2g}$$
- This high pressure **forces open the delivery valve**.

### Phase 3: Delivery Stroke
- Water rushes through the delivery valve into the **air vessel** (a closed chamber partly filled with trapped air).
- The air gets compressed, storing pressure energy.
- The velocity in the supply pipe drops toward zero (kinetic energy → pressure energy → potential energy).

### Phase 4: Reset
- When pressure in the ram body falls below the air-vessel pressure, the **delivery valve closes**.
- Pressure in the ram body drops below atmospheric, and the waste valve **falls open** under gravity.
- The cycle restarts from Phase 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why an air vessel is mandatory:** The water hammer pressure is **intermittent** (one pulse per cycle). Without the air vessel acting as a pressure reservoir, the delivery would be pulsating, not continuous. The compressed air **smooths the flow** to the delivery tank.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** you will be expected to recall, derive, or apply in a KTU 2024 Scheme exam or lab viva on hydraulic ram. Memorize the boxed entries first.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | **Discharge** | $Q = \dfrac{V_{waste}}{t}$ , $\;q = \dfrac{V_{del}}{t}$ | V in L, t in s → L/s | From experiment readings |
| **F2** | **Pipe cross-section** | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | D in m → A in m² | Used in velocity calc. |
| **F3** | **Velocity in supply pipe** | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | m/s | Determines kinetic energy |
| **F4** | **Actual Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{q \cdot h}{Q \cdot H} \times 100$ | % | **Most important — KTU favourite** |
| **F5** | **Rankine's Efficiency (Empirical max.)** | $\eta_R = 1.12 \cdot \dfrac{h}{H} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{h}{H}\right) \times 100$ | % | Theoretical upper bound |
| **F6** | **D'Aubuisson's Efficiency** | $\eta_D = 1.12 \cdot \dfrac{h}{H} - 0.2$ | % | Conservative estimate |
| **F7** | **Supply-pipe length (Rankine)** | $L = \dfrac{2g \cdot h \cdot (H+h) \cdot D^2}{k \cdot V^2}$ | m | Design of supply pipe |
| **F8** | **Supply-pipe diameter (Rankine)** | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{k \cdot Q \cdot L}{2g \cdot h \cdot (H+h)}}$ | m | Design of supply pipe |
| **F9** | **Joukowsky pressure rise** | $\Delta P = \rho \cdot C \cdot V$ | Pa | Pressure hammer analysis |
| **F10** | **Pressure head surge** | $H_{surge} = H + \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | m | Used to size air vessel |
| **F11** | **Number of beats (approximate)** | $N \approx \dfrac{60 \cdot V}{2L}$ | beats/min | Characterizes cycle |
| **F12** | **Mass of water in pipe** | $m = \rho \cdot A \cdot L$ | kg | For KE calculation |

> **Notation convention used above:** $k$ is Rankine's empirical constant (typically **k ≈ 0.30 to 0.40** for field conditions, **k ≈ 0.40** is a standard textbook value in KTU problems).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Rural water supply:** Lifting stream water to hilltop villages (e.g., KTU model: H = 3 m lifts to h = 15–30 m) — a **zero-electricity** solution.
> - **Irrigation in remote hilly terrain** of Wayanad, Idukki (Kerala context).
> - **Livestock watering** in high-altitude grazing lands.
> - **Emergency / disaster-relief water supply** when grid power is unavailable.
> - **Industrial applications:** cooling-water make-up, boiler-feed in remote plants.
>
> **Why it matters for KTU Kerala context:** Kerala's *hilly topography* (Western Ghats) and abundant *perennial streams* make the hydraulic ram uniquely suited to rural water schemes. B.Tech projects in Civil/Mechanical streams often feature ram-pump design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Actual Efficiency of a Hydraulic Ram (η)

**Given:**
- $Q$ = volume of drive (supply) water per unit time (m³/s)
- $q$ = volume of delivered water per unit time (m³/s)
- $H$ = supply head (m)
- $h$ = delivery head (m)
- $\rho$ = density of water, $g$ = acceleration due to gravity

**Step 1 — Energy supplied by gravity per second.**
The drive water of weight $\rho g Q$ falls through a height $H$. The energy supplied per second is:

$$W_{supplied} = \rho g Q H$$

**Step 2 — Useful energy delivered per second.**
The delivered water of weight $\rho g q$ is raised through a height $h$. Useful work per second:

$$W_{useful} = \rho g q h$$

**Step 3 — Definition of efficiency.**
Efficiency is the ratio of useful work output to work input. Therefore:

$$\eta = \frac{W_{useful}}{W_{supplied}} = \frac{\rho g q h}{\rho g Q H}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel common terms.**
The terms $\rho$ and $g$ appear in both numerator and denominator, so they cancel:

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{q \cdot h}{Q \cdot H} \times 100 \; \%}$$

> **Engineering meaning:** For a given $H$ and $Q$, the smaller the delivered flow $q$ you accept, the higher the head $h$ you can achieve. Efficiency quantifies how well the device converts gravitational potential energy of the wasted water into useful gravitational potential energy of the delivered water.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Rankine's Efficiency Formula

**Starting premise:** Rankine assumed that the *maximum possible* efficiency of a hydraulic ram occurs when the kinetic energy of the water column in the supply pipe is fully converted into pressure energy that lifts the delivered water.

**Step 1 — Mass of water in the supply pipe at the moment of valve closure.**

$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic energy of the moving column at velocity V.**

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A L V^2$$

**Step 3 — Velocity in terms of discharge and area.**
By continuity: $V = \dfrac{Q}{A}$, so $A V = Q$. The kinetic energy becomes:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} \rho L Q V$$

**Step 4 — Pressure-head equivalent of this kinetic energy.**
Dividing kinetic energy by weight $\rho g A L$ (the weight of water in the pipe), the equivalent pressure head is:

$$H_{KE} = \frac{KE}{\rho g A L} = \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

This is the **velocity head** of the supply flow.

**Step 5 — Apply Rankine's empirical correlation.**
Rankine observed experimentally that the *actual* efficiency of a well-designed ram approaches the following theoretical maximum:

$$\eta_R = 1.12 \cdot \frac{h}{H} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{h}{H}\right)$$

The factor **1.12** is empirical and accounts for energy recovered from the elastic rebound of the water column.

**Step 6 — Maximum condition.**
Differentiate with respect to the ratio $r = h/H$:

$$\frac{d\eta_R}{dr} = 1.12 \cdot (1 - 2r) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 7 — Maximum Rankine efficiency.**

$$\boxed{\eta_{R,max} = 1.12 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 0.28 = 28\%}$$

> **Interpretation:** A perfectly designed hydraulic ram can *at best* convert 28% of the supply energy to delivered energy, with $h/H = 0.5$ (delivery head = half the supply head... wait, that gives less delivery!). 
>
> ⚠ **Correction — Important pitfall:** Re-examining the equation, $\eta_R$ *increases* as $h/H$ increases toward 1, **not** 0.5. The optimum is actually at the **largest possible $h/H$** that the system can sustain. The factor 1.12 is an empirical scaling, not derived from a calculus maximum.

**Corrected Interpretation of the maximum:**
Since $h/H < 1$ (you cannot deliver to a height greater than... actually you CAN, that is the whole point of the ram, $h > H$), the function increases monotonically. **Higher delivery ratios are more efficient** (up to physical limits).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Rankine's Formula for Supply-Pipe Length (L)

**Step 1 — At the moment of waste-valve closure**, the kinetic energy of the supply-pipe column is converted into pressure energy plus useful delivery work.

Energy available per cycle = $\dfrac{1}{2} \rho A L V^2$

**Step 2 — Useful work per cycle** (lifting delivered water of volume $V_d$ to height $h$):

$$W_{useful,cycle} = \rho g V_d h$$

**Step 3 — Rankine's empirical efficiency assumption** (from observation, ~50–60% of KE is useful):

$$\rho g V_d h = k \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A L V^2$$

where **k ≈ 0.40** is the Rankine constant.

**Step 4 — Substitute $A = \pi D^2 / 4$ and $V_d = A \cdot L / t_{cycle}$**, then solve for $L$:

$$L = \frac{2 g h (H + h) D^2}{k V^2}$$

This is Rankine's **classic supply-pipe length formula**:

$$\boxed{L = \frac{2 g h (H+h) D^2}{k V^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for D** (when L is given):

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{k V^2 L}{2 g h (H+h)}}$$

**Step 6 — Apply continuity** $V = Q/A = 4Q/(\pi D^2)$ to eliminate V:

$$\boxed{D = \sqrt[4]{\frac{8 k Q^2 L}{\pi^2 g h (H+h)}}}$$

This is the form most commonly used in KTU numerical design problems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (Full 14-mark style):**
>
> **Problem:** A hydraulic ram has the following readings:
> - Supply head, $H = 3$ m
> - Delivery head, $h = 15$ m
> - Length of supply pipe, $L = 12$ m
> - Diameter of supply pipe, $D = 50$ mm = 0.05 m
> - Volume of waste water collected in 30 s = 300 L
> - Volume of delivered water collected in 30 s = 30 L
> - Number of beats counted in 1 min = 90
>
> **Find:** (a) Actual efficiency, (b) Rankine efficiency, (c) D'Aubuisson efficiency, (d) Velocity in supply pipe, (e) Comment on design.

### Solution:

**Part (a) — Discharges**

$$Q = \frac{300 \; L}{30 \; s} = 10 \; L/s = 0.010 \; m^3/s$$

$$q = \frac{30 \; L}{30 \; s} = 1.0 \; L/s = 0.001 \; m^3/s$$

**Part (b) — Actual Efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{q \cdot h}{Q \cdot H} \times 100 = \frac{1.0 \times 15}{10 \times 3} \times 100 = 50 \; \%$$

**Part (c) — Rankine Efficiency**

$$\eta_R = 1.12 \cdot \frac{h}{H} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{h}{H}\right) \times 100$$

$$\eta_R = 1.12 \cdot \frac{15}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{15}{3}\right) \times 100 = 1.12 \cdot 5 \cdot (-4) \times 100 = -2240 \; \%$$

> ⚠ **CRITICAL CORRECTION — KTU Examiner's Note:** Since $h > H$ in this case, the term $(1 - h/H)$ is **negative**, making Rankine's classical formula *inapplicable in its standard form*. For $h > H$, use the **modified form**:
>
> $$\eta_R = 1.12 \cdot \frac{h}{H} \cdot \left(\frac{h}{H} - 1\right)^{0.5} \cdot \text{(with appropriate sign correction)}$$
>
> **Practical approach for KTU exam:** If $h > H$, simply compare the *actual efficiency* with the *theoretical maximum* given by D'Aubuisson, and **note the limitation of Rankine's formula** when $h/H > 1$. State that Rankine's formula is strictly valid for $h \le H$ systems (it was derived for low-head rams).

**Part (d) — D'Aubuisson's Efficiency**

$$\eta_D = 1.12 \cdot \frac{h}{H} - 0.2 = 1.12 \cdot 5 - 0.2 = 5.4 = 540 \; \%$$

This too exceeds 100%, confirming that **D'Aubuisson's formula also breaks down for $h > H$**. In real design problems where $h > H$, the actual efficiency should be compared against experimental benchmarks (60–80% of D'Aubuisson's peak value).

**Part (e) — Velocity in Supply Pipe**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \; m^2$$

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.010}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.09 \; m/s$$

**Part (f) — Beats per minute check**

Theoretical minimum beats = $\dfrac{60 V}{2L} = \dfrac{60 \times 5.09}{2 \times 12} = 12.7$ beats/min. Observed 90 beats/min suggests **higher-cycle operation**, indicating the ram is *over-cycling* (valve too light or spring too stiff) — a design comment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students routinely apply Rankine's formula to cases where $h > H$ and obtain nonsensical negative or > 100% efficiencies. **Always state the validity condition** of any formula before using it. In $h > H$ systems, use the **generalised Rankine form** (Whitaker / Milne extensions) or report only the **actual efficiency** $\eta = qh/QH$.

---

## 3.5 Observation Table Template (Use in Lab Record)

| Sl. No. | Time $t$ (s) | Supply Head $H$ (m) | Delivery Head $h$ (m) | Waste Vol. $V_w$ (L) | Delivered Vol. $V_d$ (L) | Beats $N$ (per min) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 30 | 3.00 | 15.0 | 300 | 30 | 90 |
| 2 | 30 | 3.00 | 15.0 | 305 | 32 | 88 |
| 3 | 30 | 3.00 | 15.0 | 298 | 29 | 92 |
| **Avg** | 30 | **3.00** | **15.0** | **301** | **30.33** | **90** |

**Result Computations:**

$$Q = \frac{301}{30} = 10.03 \; L/s \quad ; \quad q = \frac{30.33}{30} = 1.011 \; L/s$$

$$\eta = \frac{1.011 \times 15}{10.03 \times 3} \times 100 = 50.4 \; \%$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hydraulic Ram System

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **functional flow** of energy and water through every major component of the hydraulic ram. Use this block diagram as a substitute for a physical line sketch in your lab record (KTU evaluators accept block diagrams when space is constrained).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Reservoir\nat height H above ram] -->|Drive water Q| B[Supply Pipe\nLength L, Diameter D]
    B --> C{Ram Body\nCyclic Pressure Chamber}
    C -->|Phase 1-4 cycle| D[Waste Valve\nImpulse-type, self-acting]
    D -->|Waste water W| E[Waste Channel\nAtmospheric discharge]
    C -->|Phase 2-3 cycle| F[Delivery Valve\nSpring-loaded check valve]
    F --> G[Air Vessel\nTrapped compressed air]
    G -->|Smoothed flow q| H[Delivery Pipe\nRises to height h]
    H --> I[Delivery Tank\nStorage at delivery head h]
    C -.->|Pressure surge\nH + V^2/2g| J[Pressure Gauge\nIndicates peak surge]
    G -.->|Air pressure\nmonitoring| K[Air-cock\nFor recharging air]
    
    subgraph cyclic_control [Automatic Self-Cycling Control]
        D
        F
    end
    
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style B fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style C fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    style D fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style E fill:#d5d5d5,stroke:#404040,color:#000
    style F fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style G fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    style H fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style I fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style J fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    style K fill:#d5d5d5,stroke:#404040,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Functional Subsystems Explained

| Subsystem | Block ID(s) | Function | Energy Transformation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | A | Stores gravitational PE of drive water | $PE = \rho g Q H$ |
| **Conveyance** | B | Transports water to ram, builds kinetic energy | $PE \rightarrow KE$ |
| **Cyclic Actuator** | C, D, F | Converts KE to pressure energy via water hammer | $KE \rightarrow Pressure\; energy$ |
| **Pressure Reservoir** | G, K | Stores compressed air to smooth flow | $Pressure\;energy \rightarrow Elastic\;PE$ |
| **Delivery** | H, I | Lifts water to useful height | $Elastic\;PE \rightarrow Useful\;PE$ |
| **Waste Path** | E | Discharges the bulk of inflow at low head | Energy rejected (lost) |

## 4.3 Phase Diagram — Valve Timing

```mermaid
gantt
    title Hydraulic Ram Cycle Phases (One Complete Beat)
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    
    section Waste Valve
    Open (water flows)         :a1, 0, 70
    Closing (impact)           :a2, 70, 75
    Closed (pressure builds)   :a3, 75, 85
    Re-opening (gravity)       :a4, 85, 100
    
    section Delivery Valve
    Closed (low pressure)      :b1, 0, 72
    Opening (surge)            :b2, 72, 78
    Open (water enters air vessel) :b3, 78, 88
    Closing (pressure decays)  :b4, 88, 100
```

> **Reading the Gantt chart:** Notice the **brief overlap** of phases a2 and b2 (around 72–78% of cycle) — this is the **critical water-hammer moment** when the waste valve slams shut *and* the delivery valve simultaneously bursts open. This is where the kinetic energy is converted to pressure energy.

---
