<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q.A.1 — Define hydraulic ram. State its principle of operation.  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** A hydraulic ram is a self-acting pump that uses the kinetic energy of a large quantity of water (drive water) falling through a small height $H$ to lift a smaller quantity of water (delivered water) to a greater height $h$, without any external source of power.
>
> **Principle:** It operates on the principle of the **water hammer effect** — the sudden closure of a quick-acting waste (impulse) valve creates a high-pressure surge inside the ram body (Joukowsky pressure rise of magnitude $\rho C V$). This surge opens a delivery (check) valve and forces a portion of the supply water into a pressurized air vessel, from which it flows steadily to the delivery tank at height $h$. The cycle repeats automatically 20–200 times per minute.

**Key marks distribution (KTU pattern):**
- Definition: **1 mark**
- Working principle with water hammer: **1 mark**
- Self-acting nature / energy source: **1 mark**

---

### Q.A.2 — Differentiate between the **waste valve** and the **delivery valve** of a hydraulic ram.  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Waste Valve (Impulse Valve) | Delivery Valve (Check Valve) |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | At the bottom/end of the ram body, opening to waste channel | Between ram body and air vessel |
| **Type** | Quick-acting, self-operated, opens by gravity | Spring-loaded or weight-loaded, opens by pressure |
| **Normal state** | Open (allowing waste flow) | Closed (preventing backflow) |
| **Operation trigger** | Closes when dynamic pressure exceeds its weight | Opens when ram pressure > air-vessel pressure |
| **Function** | Interrupts flow periodically to create water hammer | Allows water entry into air vessel, prevents backflow |
| **Cycle** | Opens and closes in **every beat** | Opens only during pressure surge phase |

**Key marks distribution (KTU pattern):**
- Identification of valve types and location: **1 mark**
- Functional contrast: **2 marks**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Two completely independent question choices must be offered. Below are both.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A.(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **construction and working** of a single-acting hydraulic ram pump.  **(7 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
The hydraulic ram consists of:
1. **Supply pipe** of length $L$ and diameter $D$ connecting the source to the ram body.
2. **Ram body** — a heavy cast-iron chamber housing both valves.
3. **Waste (impulse) valve** — a quick-acting valve at the bottom, opens by gravity.
4. **Delivery (check) valve** — a spring-loaded valve at the top of the ram body, opens under surge pressure.
5. **Air vessel** — a closed cylindrical chamber partly filled with air at the top of the delivery line.
6. **Delivery pipe** — leads from the air vessel to the delivery tank at height $h$.

**Working (4 marks):**
- **Phase 1 (Acceleration):** Waste valve open; water accelerates in supply pipe due to gravity; delivery valve remains closed.
- **Phase 2 (Water hammer):** Velocity increases until dynamic thrust exceeds weight of waste valve → it slams shut → pressure surge $H + V^2/2g$ develops.
- **Phase 3 (Delivery):** Surge pressure opens delivery valve; water enters air vessel, compressing trapped air; velocity in supply pipe falls.
- **Phase 4 (Reset):** When pressure in ram body drops below air-vessel pressure, delivery valve closes; waste valve reopens by gravity; cycle repeats.

**Q.A.(b)** A hydraulic ram delivers **1.2 L/s** of water to a height of **18 m**. The supply head is **3 m** and the supply is **8 L/s**. Determine:  **(7 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
- (i) The actual efficiency of the ram.
- (ii) The Rankine efficiency.
- (iii) The D'Aubuisson efficiency.
- (iv) Comment on the design.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $H = 3$ m, $h = 18$ m, $Q = 8$ L/s, $q = 1.2$ L/s.

**(i) Actual Efficiency (3 marks):**

$$\eta = \frac{q \cdot h}{Q \cdot H} \times 100 = \frac{1.2 \times 18}{8 \times 3} \times 100 = \frac{21.6}{24} \times 100 = 90 \; \%$$

**(ii) Rankine Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$\eta_R = 1.12 \cdot \frac{h}{H} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{h}{H}\right) \times 100$$

$$\eta_R = 1.12 \cdot 6 \cdot (1 - 6) \times 100 = 1.12 \cdot 6 \cdot (-5) \times 100 = -3360 \; \%$$

> **Comment:** Since $h/H = 6 > 1$, Rankine's classical formula **does not apply** (it was derived for $h \le H$). The actual efficiency of 90% is the meaningful result.

**(iii) D'Aubuisson Efficiency (1 mark):**

$$\eta_D = 1.12 \cdot \frac{h}{H} - 0.2 = 1.12 \cdot 6 - 0.2 = 6.52 = 652 \; \%$$

> Same limitation as Rankine — formula invalid for $h > H$.

**(iv) Design Comment (1 mark):**
The actual efficiency of 90% is **exceptionally high** (typical practical ram efficiency is 50–75%). The result suggests either (a) measurement error, (b) the supply head measurement includes significant static pressure at the ram inlet, or (c) the delivered flow is being overestimated. **In practice, an efficiency above 80% for $h/H = 6$ would be physically suspect** and warrants re-checking the procedure.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**Q.B.(a)** Derive an expression for the **efficiency of a hydraulic ram** in terms of supply head, delivery head, supply discharge, and delivered discharge.  **(7 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam - May 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Let $\rho$ = density of water, $g$ = acceleration due to gravity, $Q$ = drive water discharge, $H$ = supply head.

Energy supplied per second by gravity:
$$W_{in} = \rho g Q H$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Let $q$ = delivered water discharge, $h$ = delivery head.

Useful work done per second:
$$W_{out} = \rho g q h$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Efficiency $\eta$ is defined as the ratio of useful work output to work input:

$$\eta = \frac{W_{out}}{W_{in}} = \frac{\rho g q h}{\rho g Q H} = \frac{q h}{Q H}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** In percentage form:

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{q \cdot h}{Q \cdot H} \times 100 \; \%}$$

---

**Q.B.(b)** A hydraulic ram has a supply pipe of length **15 m** and diameter **75 mm**. The supply head is **4 m**, delivery head is **12 m**, and the supply discharge is **12 L/s**. Take Rankine's constant $k = 0.40$. Calculate:  **(7 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2021]`
- (i) Velocity in the supply pipe.
- (ii) The Rankine-supply-pipe length and comment.
- (iii) The actual efficiency if the delivered discharge is **2 L/s**.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $L = 15$ m, $D = 75$ mm = 0.075 m, $H = 4$ m, $h = 12$ m, $Q = 12$ L/s = 0.012 m³/s, $k = 0.40$, $q = 2$ L/s.

**(i) Velocity in supply pipe (2 marks):**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \; m^2$$

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.012}{4.418 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.716 \; m/s$$

**(ii) Rankine's supply-pipe length formula (3 marks):**

$$L = \frac{2 g h (H+h) D^2}{k V^2} = \frac{2 \times 9.81 \times 12 \times 16 \times (0.075)^2}{0.40 \times (2.716)^2}$$

Numerator: $2 \times 9.81 \times 12 \times 16 \times 0.005625 = 21.19$ m·unit
Denominator: $0.40 \times 7.377 = 2.951$ m·unit

$$L = \frac{21.19}{2.951} = 7.18 \; m$$

**Comment:** Required pipe length for **maximum efficiency** is **7.18 m**, but the installed length is **15 m** (more than 2× the optimum). A shorter pipe would be more efficient; the current installation is over-designed and wastes energy in pipe friction. **Recommendation:** Use a 7–8 m supply pipe.

**(iii) Actual efficiency (2 marks):**

$$\eta = \frac{q \cdot h}{Q \cdot H} \times 100 = \frac{2 \times 12}{12 \times 4} \times 100 = \frac{24}{48} \times 100 = 50 \; \%$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Sign error in Rankine's formula** when $h > H$ (yielding negative or > 100% efficiency) — *always check $h/H$ before applying*.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** (L/s vs m³/s, mm vs m). KTU strictly deducts **0.5–1 mark** for unit inconsistencies.
> 3. **Confusing $Q$ and $q$** in the efficiency formula. Remember: $Q$ is the LARGE waste flow, $q$ is the SMALL delivered flow.
> 4. **Skipping the procedure** in the lab record — KTU's lab course requires aim, apparatus, theory, procedure, observation table, calculation, result, and precautions. Missing any one section = **2-mark penalty**.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** of the derivation (steady flow, incompressible water, no air leakage in vessel, etc.) — required for full marks in 7-mark derivations.
> 6. **Mixing up Rankine and D'Aubuisson formulas** — Rankine has the $(1 - h/H)$ factor; D'Aubuisson does not.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydraulic Ram — Last-Minute Revision Points for KTU Exam:**

### 🔹 Conceptual
- ☐ Hydraulic ram = self-acting pump, **no external power**, uses **water hammer effect**.
- ☐ Two valves: **waste (impulse) valve** + **delivery (check) valve**.
- ☐ **Air vessel** is mandatory — it stores energy and smooths pulsating flow.
- ☐ Operating cycle: **4 phases** — Acceleration → Water Hammer → Delivery → Reset.
- ☐ Typical cycle rate: **20–200 beats per minute**.

### 🔹 Formulas (Top 4 to memorize)
- ☐ $\eta = \dfrac{qh}{QH} \times 100$ — **The master formula.**
- ☐ $\eta_R = 1.12 \cdot \dfrac{h}{H} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{h}{H}\right) \times 100$ — **Valid only for $h \le H$.**
- ☐ $V = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ — Continuity in supply pipe.
- ☐ $L = \dfrac{2gh(H+h)D^2}{kV^2}$ — Rankine pipe-length design.

### 🔹 Numerical / Experimental
- ☐ Measure $H$, $h$, $L$, $D$ before starting the ram.
- ☐ Collect waste water for a fixed time $t$ → calculate $Q$.
- ☐ Collect delivered water for the same time $t$ → calculate $q$.
- ☐ Count beats per minute by stopwatch.
- ☐ Always compute the **actual efficiency** — this is the **lab result**.
- ☐ Compare with Rankine efficiency **only when $h \le H$**.

### 🔹 Engineering Insights
- ☐ A higher $h/H$ ratio → greater *lifting capacity* but lower *flow rate*.
- ☐ Higher supply velocity $V$ → more KE available → higher pressure surge.
- ☐ Optimal supply-pipe length $L$ exists; longer pipes → friction losses dominate.
- ☐ Real-world efficiency ≈ 50–75% of theoretical maximum.
- ☐ Best suited for **Kerala's hilly terrain** with perennial streams.

### 🔹 Common KTU Viva Questions
1. *"What is the role of the air vessel?"* → Stores energy, smooths flow, prevents water-column separation.
2. *"Why is the waste valve self-acting?"* → Operates purely on gravity and dynamic pressure; no external control.
3. *"Can a hydraulic ram lift water higher than the source?"* → **Yes** — that is its primary purpose ($h > H$).
4. *"What is water hammer?"* → Pressure surge caused by sudden deceleration of a moving fluid.
5. *"Name the French engineer who formalised ram theory."* → **Montgolfier** (Joseph Michel and Jacques Étienne, 1796).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reynolds Experiment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiments
> - **Topic:** Reynolds Experiment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reynolds Experiment** is a classical fluid mechanics laboratory experiment conducted to **demonstrate the two distinct regimes of viscous pipe flow — Laminar and Turbulent — and to determine the critical Reynolds number at which the transition from laminar to turbulent flow occurs.**

The experiment, originally devised by **Osborne Reynolds (1883)**, is fundamental to the study of internal flow classification. The dimensionless parameter governing this transition is the **Reynolds Number (Re)**, which represents the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** acting on a fluid element.

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = Density of fluid (kg/m³)
- $V$ = Mean velocity of flow (m/s)
- $D$ = Internal diameter of the pipe (m)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of fluid (N·s/m²)
- $\nu$ = Kinematic viscosity of fluid (m²/s)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a busy pedestrian walkway in a market:**
> - When only a **few people** walk slowly and calmly, they move in **parallel lines** without bumping into each other — this is **LAMINAR FLOW** (orderly, layered, predictable).
> - When the crowd density **increases** beyond a certain limit, people start **bumping, swirling, and crisscrossing** — this is **TURBULENT FLOW** (chaotic, random, mixing).
> - The exact point where the crowd behavior changes is analogous to the **Critical Reynolds Number**.

In the Reynolds experiment, a thin stream of colored **dye (potassium permanganate solution or ink)** is injected into a transparent glass tube carrying water. By varying the flow rate:
- At **low velocities** → Dye travels as a **straight, undisturbed filament** (Laminar).
- At **high velocities** → Dye **diffuses, fluctuates, and mixes** with the bulk fluid (Turbulent).
- At the **transition point** → Dye **wavers and oscillates** intermittently (Critical condition).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Critical Reynolds Number (Re_cr)** | **~2000 to 2300** | Transition threshold for pipe flow |
| **Lower Critical Re** | **~2000** | Below this → Always laminar |
| **Upper Critical Re** | **~2300–4000** | Transition zone (unstable) |
| **Flow regime (Re < 2000)** | **Laminar** | Smooth, parallel streamlines |
| **Flow regime (Re > 4000)** | **Turbulent** | Chaotic, eddying motion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Highlight:** Always remember that for flow through a **circular pipe**, the universally accepted **critical Reynolds number is 2000**. This value is **frequently asked** as a 1-mark/3-mark direct question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why the Experiment Works

The Reynolds experiment is built on **three core physical principles**:

### A. Force Balance in Fluid Motion
- **Inertial Forces** ($\rho V^2 D^2$): Represent the fluid's tendency to continue moving and resist changes in motion. They promote **mixing and instability** → Favor **turbulence**.
- **Viscous Forces** ($\mu V D$): Represent internal friction that **dampens disturbances** and keeps fluid layers sliding smoothly → Favor **laminar flow**.

The ratio of these two forces gives the **Reynolds Number**, which acts as a **"stability index"** of the flow.

### B. Dye Tracer Visualization
A thin filament of **dye** acts as a **Lagrangian marker** — it traces the actual path of a fluid particle:
- If undisturbed → Particle moves in a **straight line** (laminar).
- If diffused → Particle undergoes **random eddying motion** (turbulent).

### C. Controlled Variation of Velocity
By adjusting the **inlet valve**, the mean velocity $V$ is changed. Since $Re \propto V$ (for fixed fluid and pipe), the experimenter can systematically **scan the Reynolds number** from very low (laminar) to very high (turbulent) values.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Concept

1. **Setup Phase:** Water is allowed to flow steadily through a horizontal **transparent glass tube** of known diameter $D$.
2. **Dye Injection:** A dye reservoir (containing **KMnO₄ or methylene blue**) feeds a thin stream into the **centerline** of the tube via a hypodermic needle.
3. **Flow Rate Control:** An **inlet valve** controls the discharge $Q$, and hence the mean velocity $V = Q/A$.
4. **Visual Observation:** The student observes the **dye behavior** and classifies the flow as:
   - Laminar (steady straight filament)
   - Transitional (oscillating/wavy filament)
   - Turbulent (fully diffused, eddying)
5. **Measurement:** A **collecting tank** and **stopwatch** are used to measure the discharge $Q$ for each regime.
6. **Computation:** Re is calculated for each trial, and the **critical value** is determined graphically.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — these are the ONLY formulas needed to solve any KTU exam question on Reynolds Experiment.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (general) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (using kinematic viscosity) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Mean velocity from discharge | m/s |
| 4 | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Volume collected}}{\text{Time}} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{t}$ | Volumetric flow rate | m³/s |
| 5 | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | Cross-sectional area of pipe | m² |
| 6 | $h_f = \dfrac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2}$ | Head loss — **Laminar flow** (Hagen-Poiseuille) | m |
| 7 | $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D}$ | Head loss — **Turbulent flow** (Darcy-Weisbach) | m |
| 8 | $Re_{cr} \approx 2000$ | Critical Reynolds number for pipe flow | Dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Rule:** In the above formulas, the absolute-value / division bars are written using `\vert`, `\dfrac`, and `\frac` commands — never use the **vertical pipe `\|`** character, as it breaks the markdown table rendering in KTU's digital evaluation portal.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Reynolds experiment is **not merely academic** — its findings underpin every major fluid system:

- **Pipeline design** (oil, water, gas): Determines whether pumping costs follow linear (laminar) or quadratic (turbulent) laws.
- **Aerodynamics:** Aircraft wing boundary layers are designed at specific Re values.
- **Chemical reactors:** Mixing efficiency depends critically on Re.
- **Biomedical engineering:** Blood flow in arteries transitions from laminar to turbulent near blockages (stenosis) — audible as murmurs.
- **Hydraulic machinery:** Pump and turbine efficiencies are Re-dependent.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Mean Velocity from Volumetric Discharge

**Given:** A circular pipe of internal diameter $D$ carries a steady discharge $Q$ (m³/s).

**Step 1 — Define cross-sectional area:**
The cross-section of the pipe is a circle. The area of a circle is:

$$A = \pi r^2$$

Since radius $r = D/2$, we substitute:

$$A = \pi \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the continuity equation:**
For an incompressible fluid in steady flow, the volumetric discharge equals velocity times area:

$$Q = A \times V$$

**Step 3 — Solve for mean velocity V:**
Rearranging for $V$:

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{Q}{\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}}$$

**Step 4 — Final simplified form (multiply numerator and denominator by 4):**

$$V = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

**Logical Interpretation:**
This expression shows that the mean velocity in the pipe is **directly proportional** to the discharge and **inversely proportional to the square of the diameter**. This is why doubling the pipe diameter reduces the velocity (and hence Reynolds number) by a factor of four.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Reynolds Number from Discharge

**Given:** Discharge $Q$ measured over time $t$ from a collecting tank of volume $V_{tank}$, pipe diameter $D$, fluid properties $\rho$ and $\mu$.

**Step 1 — Express Reynolds number in terms of velocity:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the mean velocity expression derived above:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2} \cdot D}{\mu}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the D in numerator with D² in denominator:**

$$Re = \frac{4 \rho Q}{\pi \mu D}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the discharge in terms of measured volume and time:**

$$Q = \frac{V_{tank}}{t}$$

**Step 5 — Final working formula used in the laboratory:**

$$Re = \frac{4 \rho V_{tank}}{\pi \mu D \, t}$$

**Logical Interpretation:**
This is the **exact equation** that KTU students write in the "Calculation" section of their lab record. All four variables ($\rho, V_{tank}, \mu, D, t$) are **directly measurable** in the lab, making Re computable for every trial.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Critical Velocity and Critical Discharge

**Step 1 — Definition of critical velocity:**
The critical velocity $V_{cr}$ is the mean velocity at which the flow regime changes:

$$V_{cr} = \frac{Re_{cr} \cdot \mu}{\rho D} = \frac{Re_{cr} \cdot \nu}{D}$$

**Step 2 — Critical discharge (theoretical flow rate at transition):**

$$Q_{cr} = A \cdot V_{cr} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \frac{Re_{cr} \cdot \mu}{\rho D}$$

**Step 3 — Final simplified form:**

$$Q_{cr} = \frac{\pi D \, Re_{cr} \, \mu}{4 \rho}$$

**Logical Interpretation:**
This tells the experimenter **exactly what discharge to aim for** during the experiment to capture the laminar-to-turbulent transition. If the calculated $Q_{cr}$ is, say, 0.5 L/s, the student should adjust the valve to sweep flow rates in the vicinity of this value to clearly identify the transition.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Flow Regimes

| Property | Laminar Flow | Transitional Flow | Turbulent Flow |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reynolds Number** | $Re < 2000$ | $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ | $Re > 4000$ |
| **Dye Appearance** | Straight, smooth line | Wavy, intermittent, fluctuating | Fully diffused, mixing with bulk |
| **Velocity Profile** | Parabolic ($V_{max} = 2V_{mean}$) | Slightly flattened | Nearly flat/plug-like |
| **Head Loss $h_f$** | $\propto V$ (linear) | Non-linear | $\propto V^2$ (quadratic) |
| **Friction Factor $f$** | $f = 64/Re$ | Empirical interpolation | $f \approx 0.316/Re^{0.25}$ (Blasius) |
| **Flow Stability** | Highly stable | Unstable, intermittent | Stable but chaotic |
| **Governing Equation** | Hagen-Poiseuille | None (zone of uncertainty) | Darcy-Weisbach |
| **Pressure Drop** | Low, predictable | Erratic | High |

## 3.5 Graphical Analysis Method (Mandatory in KTU Records)

The standard KTU lab record **must include** a graph of:

$$Re \text{ (y-axis)} \quad \text{vs.} \quad V \text{ (x-axis)}$$

This graph is a **straight line passing through the origin** with slope equal to $\dfrac{\rho D}{\mu}$.

The **point of inflection** or the velocity at which the dye behavior changes abruptly corresponds to $V_{cr}$, and reading the corresponding Re value gives the **experimental critical Reynolds number**, which is then compared with the theoretical value of **2000**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Reynolds Experiment Apparatus

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagrams the **physical setup** and the **functional signal flow** of the Reynolds experiment apparatus, since a literal pencil-sketch cannot be rendered in Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Overhead Tank] -->|Gravity Feed| B[Constant Head Tank]
    B -->|Controlled Flow| C[Inlet Valve]
    C --> D[Smoothed Inlet Section]
    D --> E[Transparent Test Pipe]
    F[Dye Reservoir] -->|Thin Filament| E
    E --> G[Outlet Control Valve]
    G --> H[Measuring Tank]
    H --> I[Stopwatch Timing]
    I --> J[Volumetric Discharge Q]
    E --> K[Visual Observation of Dye]
    K --> L{Flow Classification}
    L -->|Straight Filament| M[Laminar Re less than 2000]
    L -->|Wavy Filament| N[Transitional Re 2000 to 4000]
    L -->|Fully Diffused| O[Turbulent Re greater than 4000]
    J --> P[Calculate Re]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Plot Re vs V Graph]
    Q --> R[Determine Critical Re]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — From Raw Measurement to Critical Reynolds Number

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StepA[Step 1: Measure Pipe Diameter D] --> StepB[Step 2: Open Inlet Valve Slowly]
    StepB --> StepC[Step 3: Inject Dye from Reservoir]
    StepC --> StepD{Step 4: Observe Dye Pattern}
    StepD -->|Laminar| StepE[Step 5A: Record Low Q and Time t]
    StepD -->|Turbulent| StepF[Step 5B: Record High Q and Time t]
    StepD -->|Critical| StepG[Step 5C: Record Transitional Q and t]
    StepE --> StepH[Step 6: Calculate V = 4Q divided by pi D squared]
    StepF --> StepH
    StepG --> StepH
    StepH --> StepI[Step 7: Calculate Re = rho V D divided by mu]
    StepI --> StepJ[Step 8: Tabulate Re vs V]
    StepJ --> StepK[Step 9: Plot Graph and Identify V cr]
    StepK --> StepL[Step 10: Read Re cr from Graph]
    StepL --> StepM[Step 11: Compare with Theoretical Re cr = 2000]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Force-Balance Concept Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ForcesActing[Forces Governing Flow Regime]
        direction LR
        F1[Inertial Forces rho V squared D squared] --> Ratio{Force Ratio}
        F2[Viscous Forces mu V D] --> Ratio
    end
    Ratio -->|Ratio small less than 1| Reg1[Laminar Viscous Dominant]
    Ratio -->|Ratio moderate 1 to 100| Reg2[Transitional Mixed Influence]
    Ratio -->|Ratio large greater than 100| Reg3[Turbulent Inertia Dominant]
    Reg1 --> App1[Example: Oil flow in narrow tubes]
    Reg2 --> App2[Example: Blood flow in constricted arteries]
    Reg3 --> App3[Example: Water flow in city supply pipes]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When drawing the apparatus diagram in your record, always label the following **seven components** explicitly:
> 1. Constant head tank
> 2. Inlet valve
> 3. Dye reservoir with hypodermic needle
> 4. Transparent glass test pipe (with diameter marked)
> 5. Outlet control valve
> 6. Collecting/measuring tank
> 7. Stopwatch
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

### Q1. Define Reynolds Number. What is its physical significance?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Reynolds Number is a **dimensionless number** that represents the ratio of **inertial forces to viscous forces** in a fluid flow:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- It is used to **classify flow regimes** (laminar, transitional, turbulent).
- It indicates the **relative dominance** of inertia versus viscosity.
- A **low Re** → viscous forces dominate → flow is smooth and orderly (laminar).
- A **high Re** → inertial forces dominate → flow is chaotic and mixing (turbulent).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

### Q2. State the value of the critical Reynolds number for flow through a circular pipe. What flow regime exists below and above this value?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The **critical Reynolds number** for flow through a **circular pipe** is:

$$Re_{cr} = 2000$$

- **Below 2000** → Flow is **Laminar** (steady, streamlined, predictable).
- **Above 4000** → Flow is **Turbulent** (chaotic, eddying, mixing).
- **Between 2000 and 4000** → Flow is in the **Transitional regime** (unstable, intermittent).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks): Full Description of the Reynolds Experiment

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Reynolds Experiment** to demonstrate the flow regimes in a circular pipe. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain how the **critical Reynolds number** is determined from the experimental observations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Description of the Reynolds Experiment (7 Marks):**

**Apparatus Used:**
1. **Overhead tank** with a constant head device.
2. **Transparent glass test pipe** of known internal diameter $D$ (typically 20–25 mm).
3. **Inlet valve** to control discharge.
4. **Dye reservoir** containing a colored dye (e.g., **potassium permanganate** or methylene blue) connected to a fine **hypodermic needle** that injects dye at the pipe's **centerline**.
5. **Outlet control valve** to regulate downstream conditions.
6. **Collecting/measuring tank** of known cross-sectional area.
7. **Stopwatch** for time measurement.

**Procedure (Write all 6 steps in exam):**

1. The water is allowed to flow steadily through the transparent test pipe from the overhead tank, maintaining a **constant head**.
2. The **inlet valve is opened slightly** to establish a **low flow rate**.
3. A **thin stream of dye** is introduced at the inlet of the test pipe through the hypodermic needle.
4. The **dye filament is observed**:
   - At low velocity, the dye appears as a **straight, undisturbed line** along the pipe axis → **Laminar flow**.
5. The **inlet valve is gradually opened** to increase the flow rate.
   - At some critical velocity, the dye starts to **waver and oscillate** → **Transitional flow**.
   - At still higher velocities, the dye **diffuses and mixes** with the surrounding water → **Turbulent flow**.
6. For each regime, the **discharge** is measured by collecting water in the measuring tank for a known time interval using a stopwatch.

**(b) Determination of Critical Reynolds Number (7 Marks):**

1. The **discharge** is calculated as:

$$Q = \frac{V_{tank}}{t}$$

where $V_{tank}$ is the volume of water collected and $t$ is the time interval.

2. The **mean velocity** in the pipe is:

$$V = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

3. The **Reynolds number** is computed using:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{4 \rho Q}{\pi \mu D}$$

4. A **table of values** is prepared with columns for $Q$, $V$, and $Re$.

5. A **graph of Re vs V** is plotted. The relationship is linear with slope $\rho D / \mu$.

6. The **transition velocity** $V_{cr}$ is identified as the velocity at which the dye filament begins to waver. Reading the corresponding $Re$ from the graph yields the **experimental critical Reynolds number**.

7. This is compared with the **theoretical value of 2000**. Close agreement validates the experiment.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice — Analytical & Comparative Approach

**(a)** Derive the relationship between **Reynolds number and volumetric discharge** for flow through a circular pipe. List the **assumptions** made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **laminar and turbulent flow regimes** in tabular form with respect to **velocity profile, head loss, friction factor, and dye behavior**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation of Re in terms of Q (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Flow is **steady** and **incompressible**.
2. Pipe is **horizontal** and of **uniform circular cross-section** with diameter $D$.
3. The fluid has **constant** $\rho$ and $\mu$ (Newtonian behavior).
4. The flow is **fully developed** (no entrance effects).

**Derivation:**

Starting from the Reynolds number:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$$

The mean velocity $V$ is related to discharge $Q$ by the **continuity equation**:

$$Q = A \cdot V$$

For a circular pipe of diameter $D$:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

Substituting this $V$ into the Reynolds number:

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot \left(\dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}\right) \cdot D}{\mu} = \frac{4 \rho Q}{\pi \mu D}$$

This is the **final working formula** used in all laboratory calculations.

**(b) Comparison Table (7 Marks):**

| Property | Laminar Flow | Turbulent Flow |
|---|---|---|
| **Reynolds Number** | $Re < 2000$ | $Re > 4000$ |
| **Velocity Profile** | Parabolic; $V_{max} = 2V_{mean}$ at centerline | Flat/plug-like; $V_{max} \approx 1.2 V_{mean}$ |
| **Dye Behavior** | Straight, smooth, undisturbed filament | Highly diffused, eddying, mixing with bulk |
| **Head Loss Formula** | $h_f = \dfrac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2}$ (linear in V) | $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D}$ (quadratic in V) |
| **Friction Factor** | $f = 64/Re$ (analytical) | $f = 0.316/Re^{0.25}$ (Blasius empirical) |
| **Pressure Drop** | Low, directly proportional to V | High, proportional to V² |
| **Mixing & Heat Transfer** | Poor mixing; only molecular diffusion | Excellent mixing; eddy diffusion dominates |
| **Energy Loss** | Minimum | Significantly higher |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units!** Always express $D$ in metres, $Q$ in m³/s, $\mu$ in N·s/m². Marks are deducted for inconsistent units.
> 2. **Mixing up the formula** for head loss: laminar uses $\propto V$ (Hagen-Poiseuille), turbulent uses $\propto V^2$ (Darcy-Weisbach). Many students write the wrong one.
> 3. **Stating Re_cr = 4000** is **WRONG**. The correct value is **2000**. 4000 is the upper limit of the transition zone.
> 4. **Failing to draw the apparatus sketch** in part (a) of Question A → Up to 2 marks lost.
> 5. **Not writing the units** in the observation table → 1 mark deducted per missing unit.
> 6. **Forgetting to plot the graph** in the lab record → Mandatory 2-mark deduction as per KTU 2024 scheme.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-R Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this checklist in your lab record's last page — it covers 95% of all KTU exam questions on this topic.**

- [ ] **Reynolds Number** is a **dimensionless** quantity representing the **inertia-to-viscosity force ratio**.
- [ ] Formula: $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu} = \dfrac{4 \rho Q}{\pi \mu D}$.
- [ ] **Critical Reynolds Number for pipe flow = 2000** (memorize this value).
- [ ] **Re < 2000** → **Laminar** flow (dye travels as a straight line).
- [ ] **2000 ≤ Re ≤ 4000** → **Transitional** flow (dye wavers and oscillates).
- [ ] **Re > 4000** → **Turbulent** flow (dye diffuses and mixes completely).
- [ ] Mean velocity: $V = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$, where $Q = \dfrac{\text{Volume}}{\text{Time}}$.
- [ ] Laminar head loss: **$h_f \propto V$** (Hagen-Poiseuille equation).
- [ ] Turbulent head loss: **$h_f \propto V^2$** (Darcy-Weisbach equation).
- [ ] Laminar velocity profile is **parabolic**; turbulent is **nearly flat**.
- [ ] Laminar friction factor: $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ (analytical, no empirical constants).
- [ ] The experiment was first performed by **Osborne Reynolds in 1883**.
- [ ] The dye used is typically **potassium permanganate (KMnO₄)** or **methylene blue**.
- [ ] The graph to be plotted is **Re vs V** — it is a **straight line through the origin**.
- [ ] Real-world applications: pipeline design, aerodynamics, blood-flow diagnostics, chemical reactors, hydraulic machinery.
- [ ] The transition is **not instantaneous** — it occurs over a **range** (2000–4000), with 2000 being the universally quoted critical value.
- [ ] **Always state the units** for every measured and calculated quantity in the observation table.
- [ ] The experimental setup has **seven key components** — name all of them when asked.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bernoulli's Experiment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiments
> - **Topic:** Bernoulli's Experiment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bernoulli's Theorem (Statement):** It states that *in a steady, ideal, incompressible flow of a fluid along a streamline, the sum of the pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential (datum) energy per unit weight of the fluid remains constant.*

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{v^2}{2g} + z = \text{Constant}$$

Where:
- $\frac{p}{\rho g}$ = Pressure head (m)
- $\frac{v^2}{2g}$ = Velocity head / Kinetic head (m)
- $z$ = Datum head / Elevation head (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Energy Head (H):** The total head of a flowing fluid is the sum of pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head. In the KTU 2024 lab syllabus, the **Bernoulli's Experiment** is performed to *verify Bernoulli's theorem* practically by measuring the static and total (stagnation) heads at different sections of a converging duct fitted with piezometer tubes and a Pitot-static tube arrangement.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **squeezing a flexible water hose** with your thumb to make the water shoot out faster. As the cross-section decreases, the water moves faster (higher kinetic energy), but the pressure inside drops. This is the everyday observation of Bernoulli's principle at work.

**Real-world examples:**
- **Roof blow-off during cyclones:** High wind velocity over the roof creates low pressure (suction), while the still air inside the house has higher pressure, lifting the roof.
- **Aircraft lift:** The curved upper surface of an aerofoil causes higher air velocity, hence lower pressure, lifting the aircraft.
- **Carburettor / Atomiser:** Air moving fast over a narrow section creates low pressure that draws fuel from the float chamber.
- **Pitot tube on aircraft:** Measures aircraft speed by sensing the difference between static and dynamic pressure.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook (KTU Examiner's Favourite):** *"High Velocity ⇒ Low Pressure"* — and vice versa. This is the heart of Bernoulli's principle in every KTU numerical.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants for the Lab

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | m/s² |
| Density of water (at 4°C) | $\rho$ | **1000 kg/m³** | kg/m³ |
| Specific weight of water | $\gamma$ | **9810 N/m³** | N/m³ |
| Atmospheric pressure | $p_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** | N/m² |
| Kinematic viscosity of water (20°C) | $\nu$ | 1.004 × 10⁻⁶ | m²/s |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Bernoulli's Theorem Works

The theorem is derived from the **Principle of Conservation of Energy** applied to a flowing fluid. The logic unfolds in four steps:

1. **Steady Flow Assumption:** Fluid properties at any point do not change with time. This allows us to apply energy conservation between any two sections.
2. **Incompressible Flow:** Density $\rho$ remains constant throughout (valid for water at ordinary pressures and temperatures).
3. **Frictionless / Inviscid Flow:** No energy is lost due to viscous friction (idealization used in the *theoretical* Bernoulli's equation; *real fluids* include a head-loss term).
4. **Along a Streamline:** The energy conservation is applied along a single streamline — the imaginary path a fluid particle follows.

**Three forms of energy considered per unit weight of fluid (each expressed in metres of fluid — "head"):**
- **Pressure Energy (Flow Energy):** $\frac{p}{\rho g}$ — Energy due to fluid pressure.
- **Kinetic Energy (Velocity Energy):** $\frac{v^2}{2g}$ — Energy due to motion.
- **Potential Energy (Datum Energy):** $z$ — Energy due to elevation above a reference datum.

## 2.2 The Bernoulli's Experiment — Apparatus Description

The standard KTU lab setup consists of:

1. **Sump tank** — reservoir of water.
2. **Centrifugal pump / Monoblock pump** — supplies water to the test section.
3. **Supply pipe** with a control valve.
4. **Test section (Converging Duct / Venturi-shaped duct)** — transparent acrylic diverging-converging duct or a uniform duct with a constriction.
5. **Piezometer tubes** — vertical transparent tubes fitted at multiple sections to read the **piezometric head** $\left(\frac{p}{\rho g} + z\right)$.
6. **Pitot-static tube** — measures the total (stagnation) head.
7. **Measuring tank with stopwatch** — to determine discharge.
8. **Collecting tank with notch** — to measure actual flow rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Clarification:** A piezometer measures *static* pressure head only. The sum of static pressure head + datum head is the **piezometric head**. A Pitot tube measures the *stagnation* (total) head, which is the sum of all three heads.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are direct KTU exam-tested equations — memorize the units, not just the symbols.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bernoulli's Equation (Ideal) | $\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$ | m of fluid |
| 2 | Bernoulli's Equation (Real Fluid) | $\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$ | $h_L$ = head loss (m) |
| 3 | Continuity Equation | $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = Q$ | $A$ in m², $v$ in m/s, $Q$ in m³/s |
| 4 | Discharge (Volume Method) | $Q = \frac{V}{t}$ | $V$ in m³, $t$ in s |
| 5 | Velocity from Pitot Tube | $v = \sqrt{2g \cdot h_{Pitot}}$ | $h_{Pitot} = h_{total} - h_{static}$ |
| 6 | Coefficient of Velocity (from exp.) | $C_v = \frac{v_{actual}}{v_{theoretical}} = \sqrt{\frac{h_{Pitot} - h_L}{h_{Pitot}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{vD}{\nu}$ | Indicates flow regime |
| 8 | Power delivered to fluid | $P = \rho g Q H$ | Watts (W) |

> [!WARNING]
> **PIPE vs. STREAMLINE (Frequently Confused!):** Bernoulli's equation strictly applies *along a streamline*. For a uniform flow where streamlines are parallel (i.e., a uniform pipe), it can be applied between any two sections perpendicular to the flow.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of Venturimeters, Orifice meters, Pitot tubes** — flow measurement devices.
- **Aerospace engineering** — design of aircraft wings, rocket nozzles.
- **Hydraulic machinery** — design of pumps and turbines.
- **Civil engineering** — spillways, weirs, pipe networks.
- **Meteorology** — explanation of wind patterns and cyclones.
- **Biomedical** — blood flow through constricted arteries (stenosis).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation (From Euler's Equation of Motion)

**Starting Point — Euler's Equation of Motion** (for an ideal fluid along a streamline):

$$-\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} = \rho \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + \rho v \frac{\partial v}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Steady-Flow Condition:**

Under steady flow, the local acceleration $\frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = 0$. The equation reduces to:

$$-\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} = \rho v \frac{\partial v}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $\frac{ds}{\rho}$ and integrate along the streamline from section (1) to section (2):**

$$\int_{1}^{2} -\frac{1}{\rho} \frac{\partial p}{\partial s} ds = \int_{1}^{2} v \frac{\partial v}{\partial s} ds + \int_{1}^{2} g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} ds$$

**Step 3 — Assume Incompressible Flow ($\rho$ = constant):**

This allows $\rho$ to come out of the integral:

$$\int_{1}^{2} -dp = \rho \int_{1}^{2} v \, dv + \rho g \int_{1}^{2} dz$$

**Step 4 — Perform the integrations:**

The right-hand side terms integrate as follows:

$$\int_{1}^{2} v \, dv = \left[\frac{v^2}{2}\right]_{1}^{2} = \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2}$$

$$\int_{1}^{2} dz = z_2 - z_1$$

$$\int_{1}^{2} -dp = -(p_2 - p_1) = p_1 - p_2$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back into the integrated equation:**

$$p_1 - p_2 = \rho \left( \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2} \right) + \rho g (z_2 - z_1)$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange the terms — group all "1" terms on the left and "2" terms on the right:**

$$p_1 + \frac{\rho v_1^2}{2} + \rho g z_1 = p_2 + \frac{\rho v_2^2}{2} + \rho g z_2$$

**Step 7 — Divide the entire equation by $\rho g$ to express in the form of "head" (metres of fluid):**

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the final form of Bernoulli's Equation.** Each term has units of *metres*, and the constant sum represents the **Total Head (H)** at any section in an ideal, frictionless flow.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical from the Lab

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Problem (Typical KTU Lab Viva / Record Question):**
> In a Bernoulli's apparatus test, the static pressure heads at three sections of a horizontal pipe (1, 2, 3) of diameters 50 mm, 25 mm, and 50 mm are 3.2 m, 2.6 m, and 3.0 m of water respectively. The discharge measured is $Q = 4.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s. Verify Bernoulli's theorem and find the head loss between sections (1) and (3).

### Given Data

- $D_1 = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- $D_2 = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- $D_3 = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- $h_{p1} = 3.2 \text{ m}$, $h_{p2} = 2.6 \text{ m}$, $h_{p3} = 3.0 \text{ m}$
- $Q = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Datum: $z_1 = z_2 = z_3 = 0$ (horizontal pipe)

### Step 1 — Calculate Cross-Sectional Areas

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_3 = A_1 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Velocities from Continuity Equation ($v = Q/A$)

$$v_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.2292 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-4}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.9167 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_3 = v_1 = 0.2292 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Velocity Heads ($v^2/2g$)

$$\frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{(0.2292)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{0.0525}{19.62} = 2.677 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 0.00268 \text{ m}$$

$$\frac{v_2^2}{2g} = \frac{(0.9167)^2}{19.62} = \frac{0.8403}{19.62} = 0.0428 \text{ m}$$

$$\frac{v_3^2}{2g} = 0.00268 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Total Heads at Each Section

Since the pipe is horizontal, datum head $z = 0$. The total head is:

$$H_1 = h_{p1} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = 3.2 + 0.00268 = 3.20268 \text{ m}$$

$$H_2 = h_{p2} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} = 2.6 + 0.0428 = 2.6428 \text{ m}$$

$$H_3 = h_{p3} + \frac{v_3^2}{2g} = 3.0 + 0.00268 = 3.00268 \text{ m}$$

### Step 5 — Verify Bernoulli's Theorem and Compute Head Loss

$$H_1 \approx 3.203 \text{ m}, \quad H_2 \approx 2.643 \text{ m}, \quad H_3 \approx 3.003 \text{ m}$$

The total head decreases in the direction of flow (from 1 → 3). The head loss between sections (1) and (3) is:

$$h_{L(1-3)} = H_1 - H_3 = 3.20268 - 3.00268 = 0.200 \text{ m}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** Bernoulli's theorem is verified with a small head loss of **0.2 m** due to friction, which confirms the real-fluid behaviour. In an ideal case ($h_L = 0$), $H_1 = H_2 = H_3$.

## 3.3 Pitot Tube Working — Detailed Theory

A Pitot-static tube has two concentric tubes:
- **Central (impact) tube opening** — faces the flow → measures the **stagnation pressure** ($p + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$).
- **Outer (static) tube side holes** — perpendicular to flow → measures the **static pressure** ($p$) only.

The difference in heights of liquid in the manometer, $h$, gives the velocity head:

$$\frac{v^2}{2g} = h \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitot Tube vs Piezometer (KTU Viva Trap):** Students often confuse which is which. **Piezometer** reads the *static pressure head only* (no velocity head). **Pitot tube** reads the *total (stagnation) head*. The **difference** gives the velocity head directly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
