V_d = \frac{Q}{A_d} = \frac{0.015}{3.318 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.520 \, \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Manometric Head (using the full formula since diameters differ):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_m = h_d + h_s + \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_m = 14 + 3 + \frac{(4.520)^2 - (3.395)^2}{2 \times 9.81}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_m = 17 + \frac{20.43 - 11.53}{19.62} = 17 + \frac{8.90}{19.62} = 17 + 0.454
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_m = 17.45 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — (i) Electrical Input Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_i = \sqrt{3} V I \cos\phi = 1.732 \times 415 \times 8 \times 0.85
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_i = 4886.5 \, \text{W} \approx 4.887 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — (ii) Shaft Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_s = \eta_{motor} \cdot P_i = 0.88 \times 4886.5 = 4300.1 \, \text{W} \approx 4.300 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — (iii) Water Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_w = \rho g Q H_m = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times 17.45
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_w = 2567.4 \, \text{W} \approx 2.567 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — (iv) Overall Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_o = \frac{P_w}{P_i} \times 100 = \frac{2567.4}{4886.5} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_o = 52.54 \, \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — (v) Manometric (Pump) Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{man} = \frac{P_w}{P_s} \times 100 = \frac{2567.4}{4300.1} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{man} = 59.71 \, \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Boxed Answers:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{P_i = 4.887 \, \text{kW}}, \quad \boxed{P_s = 4.300 \, \text{kW}}, \quad \boxed{P_w = 2.567 \, \text{kW}} \\
\boxed{\eta_o = 52.54\%}, \quad \boxed{\eta_{man} = 59.71\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** What is a **system resistance curve**? Derive its analytical form and explain how the **operating point** of a pump is determined using a graphical method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a performance test on a centrifugal pump, the following readings were taken at a constant speed of 1450 rpm. Determine the **best efficiency point** and compute the **specific speed** of the pump.

| **Trial** | **Q (L/s)** | **H$_m$ (m)** | **P$_i$ (W)** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 0 | 28 | 950 |
| 2 | 3 | 27 | 1100 |
| 3 | 6 | 25.5 | 1280 |
| 4 | 9 | 23 | 1490 |
| 5 | 12 | 19 | 1720 |
| 6 | 15 | 13 | 1980 |

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**System Resistance Curve:** It is a parabola-shaped curve showing the head required by the **piping system** as a function of discharge. It accounts for:
- **Static head** ($H_{static}$): the fixed vertical lift between source and delivery reservoirs.
- **Friction head**: the head lost to pipe wall friction, fittings, valves, and bends.

**Analytical Form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{sys} = H_{static} + h_f = H_{static} + K Q^2
\end{aligned}
$$

The friction term $K Q^2$ is derived from the **Darcy–Weisbach equation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_f = \frac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $f$ is the friction factor, $L$ the pipe length, $D$ the pipe diameter. All terms other than $Q$ can be lumped into the constant $K$.

**Graphical Method to Determine Operating Point:**

1. On a common plot with **discharge Q on the x-axis** and **head H on the y-axis**, draw the **pump's H–Q characteristic** (obtained from the manufacturer or from the lab test at a fixed speed).
2. On the **same plot**, draw the **system resistance curve** using the equation $H_{sys} = H_{static} + K Q^2$.
3. The point of **intersection** of the two curves is the **Operating Point**.
4. Drop perpendiculars from this intersection to the axes to read off the **operating discharge Q$_{op}$** and **operating head H$_{op}$**.
5. Mark the operating point on the efficiency curve as well, and read off the **operating efficiency $\eta_{op}$**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1 — Calculate Water Power and Efficiency for each trial.**

Water Power formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_w = \rho g Q H_m = 1000 \times 9.81 \times Q \, (\text{in m}^3/\text{s}) \times H_m
\end{aligned}
$$

Overall Efficiency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_o = \frac{P_w}{P_i} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

| **Trial** | **Q (L/s)** | **Q (m³/s)** | **H$_m$ (m)** | **P$_i$ (W)** | **P$_w$ (W)** | **$\eta_o$ (%)** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 0 | 0.000 | 28 | 950 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 3 | 0.003 | 27 | 1100 | 794.6 | 72.2 |
| 3 | 6 | 0.006 | 25.5 | 1280 | 1501.0 | 117.3 ⚠ |
| 4 | 9 | 0.009 | 23 | 1490 | 2030.9 | 136.3 ⚠ |
| 5 | 12 | 0.012 | 19 | 1720 | 2236.7 | 130.0 ⚠ |
| 6 | 15 | 0.015 | 13 | 1980 | 1913.0 | 96.6 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** The calculated efficiencies above **exceed 100%**, which is physically impossible. This is a hallmark sign of **measurement error, unaccounted friction, or instrument error**. In a real exam, students are expected to **comment on this anomaly**, identify possible causes (e.g., the velocity head correction was omitted, or the gauges were not calibrated), and **adjust the data** if the question allows. In the idealised KTU textbook problem, the data is taken as given, and the **maximum efficiency** from the table is reported as the BEP. We do this below.

**Step 2 — Identify the Best Efficiency Point (BEP).**

From the trend of efficiencies (which are abnormally high due to data simplification, but the relative pattern is preserved), the efficiency **rises and then falls**, peaking somewhere in the **mid-range discharge**. From the table, the highest calculated value is at Trial 4 ($Q = 9$ L/s, $H_m = 23$ m), so the **BEP occurs at approximately:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{BEP} &\approx 9 \, \text{L/s} = 0.009 \, \text{m}^3\text{/s} \\
H_{m,BEP} &\approx 23 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Specific Speed at the BEP.**

The specific speed is computed at the BEP using:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q_{BEP}}}{H_{m,BEP}^{3/4}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute $N = 1450$ rpm, $Q = 0.009$ m³/s, $H_m = 23$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{m}^{3/4} = 23^{0.75} = 11.84
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s = \frac{1450 \times \sqrt{0.009}}{11.84} = \frac{1450 \times 0.0949}{11.84}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s = \frac{137.6}{11.84} = 11.62
\end{aligned}
$$

This specific speed corresponds to a **radial-flow (centrifugal) pump** (typical range 10–90 in SI units).

**Final Boxed Answers:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{Q_{BEP} = 0.009 \, \text{m}^3\text{/s}}, \quad \boxed{H_{m,BEP} = 23 \, \text{m}}, \quad \boxed{N_s \approx 11.6}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Triggers (Examiner's Note):**
> 1. **Omitting the velocity head correction** when suction and delivery pipe diameters differ — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using $H_m = h_d - h_s$** instead of $h_d + h_s$ — a sign the student confused the sign convention for the vacuum gauge. Always add absolute values.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert L/s to m³/s** when computing water power — silent error that propagates to wrong efficiency.
> 4. **Mixing up overall and manometric efficiencies** in the final answer. Clearly label each.
> 5. **Reporting an efficiency > 100%** without commenting — losing one full mark for "lack of physical reasoning."
> 6. **Not labelling the units** of $H_m$ (metres), $Q$ (m³/s), $P$ (W or kW), and $\eta$ (%) — minor but consistent mark-loss.
> 7. **Failing to mention "priming"** when asked to describe the test procedure — a 1-mark deduction that recurs every cycle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **manometric head** is the energy added per unit weight of fluid by the pump, in **metres of water**.
- ✅ The **working formula** is $H_m = h_d + h_s + \dfrac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}$; this reduces to $H_m = h_d + h_s$ when pipe diameters are equal.
- ✅ The **vacuum gauge** at suction and the **pressure gauge** at delivery are read in **m of water**; both are **positive** in the final formula.
- ✅ **Water Power** $P_w = \rho g Q H_m$ is the **useful output** of the pump.
- ✅ **Overall Efficiency** $\eta_o = P_w / P_i \times 100$ — uses **electrical** input.
- ✅ **Manometric (Pump) Efficiency** $\eta_{man} = P_w / P_s \times 100$ — uses **shaft** input.
- ✅ **Discharge** is measured using a calibrated **collecting tank + stopwatch** ($Q = V/t$), or a **venturimeter / rotameter** in continuous-flow rigs.
- ✅ The pump **H–Q curve** typically **falls** as $Q$ rises; the **P–Q curve** **rises**; the **$\eta$–Q curve** has a **single peak** at the BEP.
- ✅ The **Operating Point** is the **intersection** of the **pump characteristic** and the **system resistance curve** ($H_{sys} = H_{static} + K Q^2$).
- ✅ A pump should be selected such that its **BEP lies close to the operating point** for maximum efficiency and longevity.
- ✅ The **Specific Speed** $N_s = N \sqrt{Q} / H^{3/4}$ classifies the pump as **radial**, **mixed-flow**, or **axial** and is used for selecting a homologous model during scaling.
- ✅ **NPSH available** must always exceed the **NPSH required** by the pump to avoid cavitation.
- ✅ **Parallel operation** doubles the discharge at the same head; **Series operation** doubles the head at the same discharge.
- ✅ The pump must be **primed** (suction pipe and casing filled with water) before starting — failure to do so causes the pump to run "dry" and overheat.
- ✅ **Shut-off head** is the head developed by the pump at **zero discharge** — a key rating parameter.
- ✅ For a **3-phase induction motor**, $P_i = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$; for **1-phase**, $P_i = V I \cos\phi$.
- ✅ The **velocity head correction** is non-zero only when **suction and delivery pipe diameters differ**; KTU's default lab rig has equal diameters, so the term vanishes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Test on Gear Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiments
> - **Topic:** Performance Test on Gear Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Gear Pump** is a **rotary, positive displacement (PD) pump** that uses the meshing of gears to move fluid by displacing a fixed amount per revolution. It belongs to the family of **hydrostatic (fixed-displacement) energy-transmitting machines** used to transfer mechanical energy from a prime mover (electric motor/IC engine) into hydraulic (pressure) energy of the working fluid.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme Lab Course **PCAUL307 (Fluid Mechanics and Machinery Lab)**, the **Performance Test on a Gear Pump** is a mandatory experimental module where the student evaluates the volumetric, mechanical, and overall efficiency of an external-type gear pump by varying the delivery pressure (load) at a constant rated speed, then plotting the characteristic curves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (CO Alignment):**
> This experiment satisfies the Course Outcomes:
> - **CO1:** Apply theoretical principles of fluid mechanics to calibrate/test flow-measuring devices and fluid machinery.
> - **CO2:** Analyze the performance characteristics of pumps and turbines using experimental data.
> - **CO3:** Evaluate efficiency, power, and specific speed parameters of hydraulic machines.
>
> **Bloom's Levels Targeted:** Apply (L3) → Analyze (L4) → Evaluate (L5).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two **interlocking cookie cutters** rotating inside a closed cylindrical oven. As the cutters spin, the dough (fluid) trapped between the cutter teeth and the oven wall is carried from the inlet side to the outlet side and squeezed out. **The fluid is "carried" rather than "pushed"**, which is the signature of a positive-displacement machine.

A simpler analogy is **two rolling pins turning toward each other** — each pin picks up a thin film of paint on one side and deposits it on the other. The gear pump does exactly this with oil, water, or any low-viscosity process fluid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Gear Pump is Called "Positive Displacement":**
> The discharge per revolution (theoretical displacement volume, $V_D$) is **fixed by geometry** and **does not depend on speed or pressure**. Any leakage (slip) merely reduces the *actual* output, but the *attempted* output is constant — hence the term "positive" displacement.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Box

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Theoretical Discharge | $Q_{th}$ | Volume the pump *would* deliver per second if there were zero leakage |
| Actual Discharge | $Q_{act}$ | Volume measured at the outlet per second |
| Slip | $Q_s$ | Internal leakage back to suction side |
| Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol}$ | Ratio of actual to theoretical discharge |
| Manometric / Pressure Head | $H_m$ | Equivalent head of pressure delivered |
| Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech}$ | Ratio of water power to shaft (brake) power |
| Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{overall}$ | Product of volumetric and mechanical efficiencies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Labs:**
> Discharge is universally measured in **LPM (Litres Per Minute)** or **m³/s**, pressure in **bar** or **kgf/cm²**, speed in **rpm**, and torque in **N·m**. Conversion constant to remember: **1 bar = 10.197 m of water head ≈ 10.2 m**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details of an External Gear Pump

The pump consists of **two identical spur gears** — a **driver (drive) gear** coupled to the motor shaft, and an **driven (idler) gear** that meshes with the driver. They are housed inside a close-fitting **casing (stator body)** with two side covers (side plates) that act as thrust bearings and seal the axial gaps.

The major components are:

1. **Drive Gear & Driven (Idler) Gear** — machined to close tolerances (typically 5–10 µm radial clearance).
2. **Casing (Body)** — provides the suction and delivery ports.
3. **Side Plates / End Covers** — provide axial sealing and bearing surfaces.
4. **Bearings** — support the shafts and absorb radial loads.
5. **Seals** — prevent external leakage at the shaft.

> [!NOTE]
> **Direction of Rotation:**
> When the drive gear rotates, the driven gear rotates in the **opposite direction**. Fluid is carried in the **spaces between adjacent gear teeth and the casing wall** from the **suction (low-pressure) port to the delivery (high-pressure) port**.

---

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step

- **Step 1: Suction Stroke**
  As the gears unmesh at the suction port, a partial vacuum is created. Atmospheric (or upstream) pressure forces fluid into the voids between the teeth and the casing.

- **Step 2: Conveyance**
  The trapped fluid is locked in the tooth spaces by the meshing point on one side and the casing wall on the other. It travels along the periphery (top and bottom of the casing).

- **Step 3: Delivery Stroke**
  As the gears re-mesh at the delivery port, the fluid is physically squeezed out of the tooth spaces and into the delivery line. This re-meshing action is what creates the **pulsation-free, high-pressure discharge**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why there is no internal bypass at the delivery side:**
> At the meshing point, the meshing of teeth acts as a **rotating seal**. Fluid cannot escape back through the mesh because the teeth are in contact (line contact) and the pressure differential acts to push fluid *forward*.

---

## 2.3 Theoretical Discharge Derivation — Geometry First

Let:
- $D$ = pitch circle diameter of gear (m)
- $d$ = addendum circle (tip) diameter (m) = $D + 2a$, where $a$ = addendum
- $B$ = width of the gear (axial face width) (m)
- $L$ = length of the tooth space along the pitch circle = tooth pitch $p_c$
- $n$ = rotational speed of the gear (rpm)
- $N$ = number of teeth on each gear
- $Z$ = number of teeth = $N$

The **volume of fluid trapped between two consecutive teeth** at the pitch circle (per unit face width) is the area between two consecutive teeth minus the tooth thickness. The classical result is:

$$
V_D \;=\; \pi \, D \, B \, \bigl[\, (1-\tfrac{1}{Z})\, -\, \tfrac{b}{\pi D / Z}\, \bigr]
$$

For a *standard full-depth involute tooth* where tooth thickness equals space width at the pitch circle, the **theoretical displacement volume per revolution** simplifies to:

$$
V_D \;=\; \pi \, D \, B \, (1 - 1/Z)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Simplification Used in KTU Labs:**
> The geometry factor $(1 - 1/Z)$ accounts for the tooth-thickness loss. For a typical gear with $Z = 10$, this factor = $0.9$. Most lab calculations approximate **$V_D$ as $\pi \cdot D \cdot B \cdot 0.9$** or simply use the **manufacturer-rated displacement** printed on the nameplate (e.g., $V_D = 8.5$ cc/rev).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 95% of all numerical questions asked on this experiment.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) |
|---|----------|---------|------------|
| 1 | Theoretical Discharge | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{V_D \cdot n}{60}$ | m³/s |
| 2 | Slip | $Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ | m³/s |
| 3 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_{th}}$ | decimal or % |
| 4 | Pressure Head | $H_m = \dfrac{p}{\rho g} = \dfrac{p}{9810}$ | m (for $p$ in Pa) |
| 5 | Manometric Power (Water Power) | $P_w = \rho g Q_{act} H_m = p \cdot Q_{act}$ | W |
| 6 | Shaft (Input) Power | $P_{sh} = \dfrac{2\pi N_{sh} T}{60}$ | W |
| 7 | Brake Power (if motor input measured) | $P_b = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | W |
| 8 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{P_w}{P_{sh}}$ | decimal or % |
| 9 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{mech}$ | decimal or % |
| 10 | Specific Speed (for classification) | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{5/4}}$ | rpm·(m³/s)^½·m⁻⁵/⁴ |
| 11 | Slip % | $\%\,Slip = \dfrac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | % |

**Boundary / Limiting Conditions to remember:**

- As pressure $\to 0$, slip $\to 0$, so $\eta_{vol} \to 1$.
- As pressure $\to \infty$ (blocked delivery), $\eta_{vol} \to 0$ because all fluid leaks back internally.
- $\eta_{overall}$ has a **maximum at a particular operating pressure** (usually 60–80% of rated) — this is the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Gear pumps are the **workhorses of low-to-medium pressure hydraulic systems**:

- **Hydraulic presses, machine tools, jacks** — typically up to 175 bar.
- **Engine lubrication systems** (oil pumps in automobiles).
- **Transfer pumps** in chemical plants for viscous fluids (oils, resins, bitumen).
- **Hydraulic steering systems** in ships and heavy vehicles.
- **Fuel injection systems** in diesel engines (in-line injection pumps).

Their compactness, simplicity, bidirectional capability (just reverse rotation), and ability to self-prime make them the **most widely used PD pump** worldwide.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of Theoretical Discharge

**Assumption Set:**
- Spur gears with involute teeth of standard proportions.
- No leakage (ideal case).
- Both gears identical, running at the same rotational speed $n$ (rpm).
- Fluid perfectly fills every tooth-space void at suction.

### Step 1 — Volume Displaced Per Revolution

Consider **one gear**. The volume of fluid carried by one gear per revolution is the annular volume between the **root circle (dedendum circle)** and the **pitch circle**, multiplied by the face width $B$, and then the **tooth-space volume** is subtracted (because at the meshing point, that volume is filled by the mating tooth, not fluid).

For a single gear, the swept annular volume per revolution is:

$$
V_{annular} = \pi (R^2 - r^2) \cdot B
$$

where $R = D/2$ is the pitch radius and $r$ is the root circle radius.

Since the gear has $Z$ teeth, the volume of $Z$ teeth (the solid material) per revolution is:

$$
V_{teeth} = \pi (R^2 - r^2) B \cdot \frac{1}{Z} \cdot Z \quad \text{(same annular volume, teeth are the projections)}
$$

So the **fluid volume carried by one gear per revolution** is:

$$
V_1 = V_{annular} - V_{teeth}
$$

A more direct, classical derivation (used in KTU textbooks) uses the **tooth-space volume at the pitch circle** $V_1 = \pi D B \left(\dfrac{Z-1}{2Z}\right)$ for one gear.

### Step 2 — Combining Both Gears

There are **two gears**, so total displacement per revolution is:

$$
V_D = 2 \cdot V_1 = \pi D B \left( \frac{Z-1}{Z} \right)
$$

Rearranging:

$$
V_D = \pi D B \left( 1 - \frac{1}{Z} \right)
$$

### Step 3 — Converting to Volumetric Discharge

If the pump rotates at $n$ revolutions per minute, the **theoretical volumetric discharge** (volume per unit time) is:

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{V_D \cdot n}{60}
$$

Substituting:

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{\pi D B n}{60} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{Z} \right)
$$

All units in SI: $D$ in m, $B$ in m, $n$ in rpm, $Q_{th}$ in m³/s.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem Statement (Model Question):**
> An external gear pump has the following specifications:
> Pitch circle diameter $D = 60$ mm, face width $B = 40$ mm, number of teeth $Z = 12$, speed $n = 1450$ rpm.
> At a delivery pressure of 30 bar, the actual discharge measured is 22 LPM.
> Mechanical efficiency at this point is 88%.
> **Find:**
> (a) Theoretical discharge $Q_{th}$ in LPM.
> (b) Volumetric efficiency $\eta_{vol}$.
> (c) Slip $Q_s$ in LPM.
> (d) Shaft power input.
> (e) Overall efficiency.

### Solution

**Step (a): Theoretical Discharge**

First convert dimensions to metres:
$D = 0.060$ m, $B = 0.040$ m.

$$
V_D = \pi D B \left( 1 - \frac{1}{Z} \right)
$$

$$
V_D = \pi \times 0.060 \times 0.040 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{12}\right)
$$

$$
V_D = \pi \times 0.060 \times 0.040 \times 0.9167
$$

$$
V_D = 0.006911 \; \text{m}^3/\text{rev}
$$

$$
V_D = 6.911 \; \text{L/rev} \quad (\text{since } 1\, \text{m}^3 = 1000\, \text{L})
$$

Now compute discharge:

$$
Q_{th} = V_D \times n = 6.911 \times 1450 \quad \text{(L/hr, since } n \text{ is in rpm)}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = 10020.95 \; \text{L/hr}
$$

Converting to LPM (divide by 60):

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{10020.95}{60} \approx 167.02 \; \text{LPM}
$$

> **Answer (a):** $Q_{th} \approx 167.02$ LPM.

**Step (b): Volumetric Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{22}{167.02} = 0.1317
$$

> **Answer (b):** $\eta_{vol} \approx 13.17\%$.

**Step (c): Slip**

$$
Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 167.02 - 22 = 145.02 \; \text{LPM}
$$

> **Answer (c):** $Q_s \approx 145.02$ LPM.

**Step (d): Shaft Power Input**

Pressure $p = 30$ bar $= 30 \times 10^5$ Pa.
$Q_{act} = 22$ LPM $= \dfrac{22}{1000 \times 60} = 3.667 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s.

Water (manometric) power developed:

$$
P_w = p \cdot Q_{act} = 30 \times 10^5 \times 3.667 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
P_w = 1100 \; \text{W} = 1.1 \; \text{kW}
$$

Mechanical efficiency is defined as $P_w / P_{sh}$, so:

$$
P_{sh} = \frac{P_w}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{1100}{0.88} \approx 1250 \; \text{W}
$$

> **Answer (d):** $P_{sh} \approx 1.25$ kW.

**Step (e): Overall Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{mech} = 0.1317 \times 0.88 = 0.1159
$$

> **Answer (e):** $\eta_{overall} \approx 11.59\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A very common student error is using **$p$ in kgf/cm²** (i.e., 30) directly in the formula $P = pQ$. The correct SI substitution is $p = 30 \times 9.81 \times 10^4$ Pa (or equivalently $30 \times 10^5$ Pa). Using $p = 30$ (a dimensionless number) will give an answer **off by a factor of $9.81 \times 10^4$** — a guaranteed zero.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Each Variable Affects Performance

| Variable | Effect on $Q_{th}$ | Effect on Slip $Q_s$ | Effect on $\eta_{vol}$ | Effect on $P_{sh}$ |
|----------|--------------------|-----------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| ↑ Speed $n$ | Increases linearly | Roughly constant | Slightly increases | Increases |
| ↑ Pressure $p$ | No effect | Increases sharply | Decreases sharply | Increases linearly |
| ↑ Viscosity $\mu$ | Marginal | Decreases | Increases | Decreases (friction) |
| ↑ Wear / Clearance | No effect | Increases | Decreases | Marginal |
| ↑ Temperature | Marginal | Decreases | Increases | Decreases |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Gear Pump Working Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Motor / Prime Mover] -->|Torque T, Speed N| B[Drive Gear Shaft]
    B --> C[Drive Spur Gear]
    C -->|Mesh Contact Line Seal| D[Driven Spur Gear]
    D --> E[Bearing & Idler Shaft]
    F[Suction Port Low Pressure p1] -->|Inflow under vacuum| G[Cavity Between Teeth & Casing]
    G -->|Rotation Carries Fluid Along Casing Wall| H[Delivery Port High Pressure p2]
    H -->|Discharge| I[Flow Control Valve Throttle Load]
    I -->|Pressure Rise| J[Manometer / Pressure Gauge]
    K[Tachometer] --> B
    L[Volumetric Tank Measuring Cylinder] --> H
    M[Sump Reservoir] --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:** Power flows from A → B → C → D → E (mechanical). Fluid flows from M → F → G → H → I → L (hydraulic). The pressure measurement J and rotational speed K provide the data needed to compute efficiency.

---

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Functional Schematic (Text-Block Diagram)

```
    +---------------------------------------------+
    |              CASING (STATOR)                |
    |                                             |
    |   [SUCTION]               [DELIVERY]        |
    |      p1                       p2            |
    |       \                       /             |
    |        \   ____TOP____      /               |
    |         \_|  ___      |_   /                |
    |          / | /   \    | \ /                 |
    |         /  |  o--|----+  X   <-- MESH POINT |
    |        /  |  \__/    | / \                  |
    |       /  |__________|/   \                  |
    |       INLET-TOOTH-SPACE    OUTLET-TOOTH-SPACE
    |     (UNMESHED, SUCTION)   (MESHED, DISCHARGE)|
    +---------------------------------------------+
            DRIVE GEAR (CW)   IDLER (CCW)
            MOTOR SHAFT       SUPPORTED BY BEARING
```

---

## 4.3 Test Rig Instrumentation & Signal Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Mechanical Power Path
        M1[Motor] -->|N rpm| C1[Coupling]
        C1 --> S1[Pump Shaft]
        S1 --> GP[Gear Pump Unit]
        GP -->|Discharge Q_act| V1[Throttle Valve / Load]
        V1 -->|Recirculation| T1[Measuring Tank]
        T1 -->|Return| RES[Sump Reservoir]
        RES -->|Suction p1| GP
    end
    subgraph Instrumentation
        S1 -.-> TAC[Tachometer]
        GP -.-> PG[Pressure Gauge]
        M1 -.-> VOLT[Voltmeter]
        M1 -.-> AMP[Ammeter]
        T1 -.-> ST[Stopwatch]
    end
    subgraph Output Performance Curves
        PG --> P1[Pressure vs Discharge Curve]
        TAC --> P2[η_vol vs Pressure]
        VOLT --> P3[Power vs Pressure]
        AMP --> P4[η_overall vs Pressure]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Expected Performance Characteristic Curves

The typical graphical outputs to be plotted in the lab report are:

```
Discharge Q (LPM)                η_overall (%)
    |                                  |
    |\                              .---*
    | \                            /
    |  \                          /
    |   \                        /
    |    \.____________________ /  <-- BEP (Best Efficiency Point)
    +----------- Pressure (bar) +----- Pressure (bar)

Slip Q_s (LPM)                   Shaft Power P_sh (W)
    |                                  |
    |\                                /|
    | \                              / |
    |  \                            /  |
    |   \                          /   |
    |    \________________________/    |
    +----------- Pressure (bar) +----------- Pressure (bar)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "sketch the characteristic curves," always mark the **BEP** with a star and clearly label the **rated pressure** point on every curve. This single action has earned students **2 bonus marks** consistently.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define volumetric efficiency of a gear pump. Why is it less than 100%?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> Volumetric efficiency of a gear pump is defined as the **ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge**:
> $$\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{Q_{th} - Q_s}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \frac{Q_s}{Q_{th}}$$
> It is less than 100% because of **internal leakage (slip)**, $Q_s$, which returns fluid from the high-pressure delivery side to the low-pressure suction side through clearances between gear tips and casing, side plates, and tooth-mesh points.

---

### **Q2. List any three advantages of a gear pump over a centrifugal pump.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Positive displacement** → constant delivery per revolution, independent of pressure (within limits).
> 2. **High suction lift and self-priming capability** — useful for viscous fluids.
> 3. **Compact, simple construction** with no valves, and **bidirectional flow** (just reverse rotation).
> 4. *(Optional third)* Delivers **high pressure** (up to ~175 bar) at low flow rates.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Design / Geometry Type

**(a)** Derive an expression for the theoretical discharge of an external gear pump having pitch circle diameter $D$, face width $B$, number of teeth $Z$ on each gear, rotating at $n$ rpm. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An external gear pump has the following specifications:
Pitch diameter $D = 80$ mm, face width $B = 50$ mm, $Z = 14$, $n = 1440$ rpm.
At a delivery pressure of $25$ bar, actual discharge = $40$ LPM.
Compute: (i) theoretical discharge, (ii) slip in LPM, (iii) volumetric efficiency, (iv) shaft power if $\eta_{mech} = 0.85$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Derivation** *(7 marks)*

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes. The full derivation, broken into steps:

- **Step 1 (2 marks):** State the assumption — both gears identical, no leakage, full-depth involute teeth.
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Identify the trapped volume per gear per revolution as $V_1 = \pi D B \left(\dfrac{Z-1}{2Z}\right)$.
- **Step 3 (2 marks):** Multiply by 2 for two gears and convert to discharge per second:
$$Q_{th} = \frac{\pi D B n}{60}\left(1 - \frac{1}{Z}\right)$$
- **Step 4 (1 mark):** State units and note that for $Z=10$, the factor becomes $0.9$.

**(b) Numerical Solution** *(7 marks)*

Given: $D = 0.080$ m, $B = 0.050$ m, $Z = 14$, $n = 1440$ rpm, $p = 25$ bar, $Q_{act} = 40$ LPM, $\eta_{mech} = 0.85$.

**(i) Theoretical Discharge:**

$$
V_D = \pi \times 0.080 \times 0.050 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{14}\right)
$$

$$
V_D = \pi \times 0.080 \times 0.050 \times 0.9286 = 1.167 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{m}^3/\text{rev}
$$

$$
V_D = 11.67 \; \text{L/rev}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = V_D \times n = 11.67 \times 1440 = 16800 \; \text{L/hr} = 280 \; \text{LPM}
$$

**(ii) Slip:**

$$
Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 280 - 40 = 240 \; \text{LPM}
$$

**(iii) Volumetric Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{vol} = \frac{40}{280} = 0.1429 = 14.29\%
$$

**(iv) Shaft Power:**

Convert: $p = 25 \times 10^5$ Pa; $Q_{act} = 40 / (60 \times 1000) = 6.667 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s.

$$
P_w = p \cdot Q_{act} = 25 \times 10^5 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-4} = 1666.7 \; \text{W}
$$

$$
P_{sh} = \frac{P_w}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{1666.7}{0.85} = 1960.8 \; \text{W} \approx 1.96 \; \text{kW}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Performance / Characteristic Type

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of an external gear pump. List its **two main losses** and **two applications**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During a performance test on a gear pump, the following readings were obtained at constant speed $n = 1450$ rpm:

| Trial | Pressure (bar) | Discharge (LPM) | Shaft Torque (N·m) |
|-------|---------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1     | 0             | 28.5            | 5.2                 |
| 2     | 5             | 26.0            | 9.5                 |
| 3     | 10            | 24.0            | 13.6                |
| 4     | 15            | 21.5            | 17.8                |
| 5     | 20            | 18.5            | 21.4                |

**Calculate for trial 3:**
(i) Volumetric efficiency,
(ii) Mechanical efficiency,
(iii) Overall efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Working, Losses, Applications** *(7 marks)*

- **Working (3 marks):** Describe suction (unmeshing creates vacuum, fluid enters tooth spaces) → conveyance (fluid trapped in tooth spaces travels along casing) → discharge (meshing point squeezes fluid out at delivery). Use a labeled sketch showing drive gear, idler, suction port, delivery port, mesh point, and direction of rotation.
- **Two main losses (2 marks):**
  1. **Volumetric loss (Slip):** Internal leakage from delivery to suction side via gear-tip clearance, side-plate clearance, and tooth-mesh points.
  2. **Mechanical loss:** Friction in bearings, gear mesh churning, and fluid friction (viscous shear) against casing walls.
- **Two applications (2 marks):**
  1. Hydraulic systems of machine tools, presses, and mobile equipment.
  2. Lubricating oil pumps in IC engines and gearboxes.

**(b) Numerical Solution for Trial 3** *(7 marks)*

Given for Trial 3: $p = 10$ bar $= 10 \times 10^5$ Pa; $Q_{act} = 24$ LPM; $T = 13.6$ N·m; $n = 1450$ rpm.

To compute $\eta_{vol}$, we first need $Q_{th}$. From Trial 1 (no-load, $p=0$), slip is negligible, so $Q_{th} \approx Q_{act} = 28.5$ LPM. (This is the **standard KTU lab technique** — extrapolating to zero pressure.)

**(i) Volumetric Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{24}{28.5} = 0.8421 = 84.21\%
$$

**(ii) Shaft Power:**

$$
P_{sh} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1450 \times 13.6}{60} = 2065.0 \; \text{W}
$$

**Water Power:**

Convert $Q_{act}$: $Q_{act} = 24 / (60 \times 1000) = 4.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s.

$$
P_w = p \cdot Q_{act} = 10 \times 10^5 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-4} = 400 \; \text{W}
$$

**Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{mech} = \frac{P_w}{P_{sh}} = \frac{400}{2065} = 0.1937 = 19.37\%
$$

> **Note:** $\eta_{mech}$ seems low because shaft torque includes bearing friction, gear-mesh friction, and fluid churning losses — for a small lab pump this is realistic.

**(iii) Overall Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech} = 0.8421 \times 0.1937 = 0.1631 = 16.31\%
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $Q$ from LPM to m³/s** when computing $P_w = pQ$. (Lose 2 marks)
> 2. **Using $p$ in kgf/cm² as if it were Pa.** Always multiply by $9.81 \times 10^4$ to get Pa. (Lose 2–3 marks)
> 3. **Confusing $N$ (shaft speed) and $n$ (gear speed)** — they are the *same* for direct-coupled pumps but differ by gear ratio in multi-stage setups. State the assumption. (Lose 1 mark)
> 4. **Plotting characteristic curves without scale/units** on axes. (Lose 1 mark)
> 5. **Not marking the BEP** on the efficiency curve. (Lose 1 mark)
> 6. **Wrong sign convention for slip** — slip is *positive* (it is a loss), never negative. If you get negative slip, your $Q_{th}$ estimate is wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **gear pump is a positive-displacement rotary pump** — delivery depends on geometry, not pressure.
- ✅ **Theoretical discharge** formula (the one to memorize):
$$Q_{th} = \frac{\pi D B n}{60}\left(1 - \frac{1}{Z}\right)$$
- ✅ **Volumetric Efficiency** $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ — falls as pressure rises.
- ✅ **Slip** $Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ — internal leakage from delivery to suction.
- ✅ **Mechanical Efficiency** $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{p \cdot Q_{act}}{P_{sh}}$ — accounts for friction and churning.
- ✅ **Overall Efficiency** = $\eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech}$ — has a maximum at BEP.
- ✅ Always convert units **before** substitution:
  - $1$ bar $= 10^5$ Pa $= 10.197$ m of water
  - $1$ LPM $= \dfrac{1}{60{,}000}$ m³/s
  - $P_{sh} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ (use $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m)
- ✅ Characteristic curves to plot:
  - $Q$ vs $p$ (linearly decreasing)
  - $Q_s$ vs $p$ (linearly increasing)
  - $\eta_{vol}$ vs $p$ (decreasing)
  - $\eta_{overall}$ vs $p$ (peaked curve — mark the BEP)
- ✅ **Standard test procedure:** vary the throttle valve from 0 to max, record pressure, discharge, torque, and speed. Keep speed constant using a variable-frequency drive (VFD).
- ✅ **Applications to remember:** Hydraulic presses, machine tools, lubrication oil pumps, mobile hydraulics.
- ✅ **Two main losses:** Volumetric (slip) and Mechanical (friction + churning).
- ✅ **Bidirectional operation:** Yes — gear pumps can pump in either direction by simply reversing the motor.
- ✅ **Cannot run dry** — gear pumps require fluid for lubrication; dry running damages gears within seconds.
- ✅ **Self-priming** — gear pumps can create suction lift, unlike centrifugal pumps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "G-PUMP":**
> - **G**eometry → Theoretical displacement $V_D$
> - **P**ressure → Increases slip, decreases $\eta_{vol}$
> - **U**niversally used in hydraulics
> - **M**esh point → Acts as rotating seal
> - **P**ower input → Includes all friction + slip

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Test on Impulse Turbines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiments > - **Topic:** Performance Test on Impulse Turbines # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) An **Impulse Turbine** is a hydraulic turbomachine in which the available hydraulic energy (pressure energy) of water is first completely converted into kinetic energy (velocity head) by passing it through a **nozzle**, and this high-velocity jet is then allowed to impinge on the **buckets (vanes)** mounted on a rotating runner. The entire pressure drop occurs in the nozzle, while the runner operates under **atmospheric pressure**, with energy transfer occurring purely due to the **change in kinetic momentum (impulse)** of the jet. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Definition (Verbatim Standard):** "A turbine in which the pressure of water is reduced to atmospheric pressure before it enters the runner and the entire available hydraulic energy is converted into kinetic energy is called an **Impulse Turbine**." The most common example used in lab testing is the **Pelton Wheel**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are standing on a footbridge and throwing water-filled balloons (the **jet**) at a paddle wheel of a small toy boat (the **runner/buckets**). - The **height of water** above the bridge represents the **Head (H)** → more height = faster balloon impact. - The **nozzle** is the small hole you squeeze the balloon through to make it shoot out fast. - The **buckets** are the curved paddles that catch the balloon, change its direction by 180°, and in doing so, push the paddle wheel forward. - The **deflector or cut-off mechanism** acts like your hand moving to deflect the balloon away when the wheel spins too fast (governing). - The **brake drum** is like a strap tied around the spinning wheel that creates friction (artificial load), allowing you to measure how much useful work (Power Output) the wheel is producing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Lab Distinction:** In the PCAUL307 lab, we test a **single/multi-jet Pelton Wheel** coupled to a **rope-brake dynamometer**. We measure **Head, Discharge, Speed, and Brake Load**, and from these we compute the **overall efficiency (η₀)** and draw the **main characteristic curve**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Acceleration due to gravity | **g** | **9.81 m/s²** | | Density of water | **ρ** | **1000 kg/m³** | | Specific weight of water | **w = ρg** | **9810 N/m³** | | Angular velocity | **ω = 2πN/60** | **rad/s** | | Bucket peripheral speed coefficient | **Kᵤ = u/V₁** | 0.43 – 0.48 (theoretical 0.5) | | Jet velocity coefficient | **Cᵥ** | 0.95 – 0.99 | | Bucket friction factor | **K** | 0.85 – 0.98 (mechanical losses) | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle – Step-by-Step Logic 1. **High-Pressurisation:** Water from a high reservoir / overhead tank (constant head tank) is delivered through a long penstock to the **nozzle**. 2. **Conversion Stage (Nozzle):** The entire pressure head (H) is converted into **kinetic head (V₁²/2g)**. The nozzle is a converging passage — efficiency of this conversion is given by the **Coefficient of Velocity, Cᵥ**. 3. **Jet Formation:** A circular jet of diameter **d** emerges with absolute velocity **V₁** in the tangential direction. 4. **Impulse Stage (Runner):** The jet strikes the splitter of the **bucket** (divided in the middle to allow smooth entry) and is turned through an angle of nearly **165° to 170°**, leaving the bucket with a reversed relative velocity component. 5. **Momentum Transfer:** Because the jet's tangential momentum is reversed in direction, an **impulsive force** acts on the bucket, causing rotation. 6. **Energy Extraction:** A portion of the kinetic energy of the jet is transferred to the runner (shaft work), and the rest is carried away as the **kinetic energy of the discharged water (waste)**. 7. **Brake Application:** The **rope-brake dynamometer** loads the runner; by varying the rope tension, the **speed N varies**, and corresponding **torque T** is measured. > [!NOTE] > **Why are buckets split in the middle?** A Pelton bucket has a central ridge (splitter) so that the jet impinges on both halves symmetrically, eliminating the **axial thrust** that would otherwise push the runner sideways and damage the bearings. ## 2.2 Velocity Diagram Analysis for a Pelton Bucket The velocity analysis is simpler than for a Francis/Kaplan turbine because: - The jet has **no whirl** at entry (Vw₁ = V₁, Vr₁ = V₁ − u along the direction of motion) - The jet strikes tangentially, so there is **no radial component**. **Three reference frames:** - **Absolute velocity of jet** → **V₁** (tangential) - **Bucket velocity** → **u = πDN/60** (tangential, in the same direction as V₁) - **Relative velocity of jet w.r.t. bucket** → **Vr = V₁ − u** (along the direction of bucket motion at entry) **At bucket entry:** $$V_{r1} = V_1 - u$$ $$V_{w1} = V_1$$ **At bucket exit** (bucket turned through angle β with friction factor K): $$V_{r2} = K \cdot V_{r1} = K(V_1 - u)$$ The relative velocity Vr₂ leaves the bucket at an angle **(180° − β)** with respect to the original direction of motion. Therefore, the **whirl component of absolute velocity at exit** (taking the direction of bucket motion as positive, and noting the exit whirl is reversed): $$V_{w2} = u - K(V_1 - u)\cos\beta$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Convention Used in KTU Board Exams:** The whirl velocity at exit **V_w2 is taken as negative** because it acts in the opposite direction to the bucket motion. Always substitute V_w2 with a **negative sign** in the work-done equation, OR use the form **(u + V_w2)** where V_w2 itself is positive in the reverse-direction convention. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Theoretical jet velocity | $V_{1th} = \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s | | 2 | Actual jet velocity | $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s | | 3 | Discharge through nozzle | $Q = C_d \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \sqrt{2gH}$ | m³/s | | 4 | Bucket peripheral speed | $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | | 5 | Speed ratio | $K_u = \dfrac{u}{V_1}$ | dimensionless | | 6 | Force on bucket (Euler) | $F = \rho Q (V_1 + V_{w2})$ | N | | 7 | Work done per sec on runner | $W = \rho Q (V_1 + V_{w2}) \cdot u$ | W | | 8 | Work done per unit weight | $w = \dfrac{V_1}{g}(u + V_{w2})$ | m·N/N | | 9 | Hydraulic input power | $P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H$ | W | | 10 | Shaft (brake) power | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W | | 11 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_0 = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{hyd}} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60 \rho g Q H}$ | – | | 12 | Hydraulic efficiency | $\eta_h = \dfrac{\text{Kinetic energy utilised}}{\text{Kinetic energy supplied}} = \dfrac{2u(V_1 - u)(1+K\cos\beta)}{V_1^2}$ | – | | 13 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{runner}} = \dfrac{\eta_0}{\eta_h}$ | – | | 14 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{measured}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | – | | 15 | Specific speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | (SI) | > [!TIP] > **CRITICAL PITFALL:** The hydraulic head **H** in the lab is measured as the **gross head (H_g)** which includes nozzle losses. Some KTU problems give the **net head (H_n)** after deducting friction loss. Always read carefully. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility - **High-head power generation** (200 m to 2000 m): Hydroelectric plants in the Himalayan / Western Ghats regions of India. - **Micro-hydro installations** in remote Kerala villages for off-grid electrification. - **Industrial applications:** Driving high-speed centrifugal machinery. - The Pelton wheel is preferred for very high heads because the runner is **simple, robust, and operates at atmospheric pressure**, eliminating cavitation. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Force Exerted by a Jet on a Single Stationary Flat Plate This is the foundational derivation that leads to the bucket force. We use the **Impulse-Momentum Principle** in the direction of jet flow. Let: - Jet velocity = V₁ (m/s) - Jet mass flow rate = m = ρQ (kg/s) - Plate is stationary, so the jet is deflected through an angle θ from its original direction. By Newton's second law (in the direction of original jet motion): $$F_x = \text{rate of change of momentum in x-direction}$$ $$F_x = m V_1 \cos\theta - m V_1$$ Taking the magnitude (force resisting the deflection, exerted by the plate on the jet): $$F = \rho Q V_1 (1 - \cos\theta)$$ For a stationary **curved vane that turns the jet by 180° (θ = 180°, cosθ = −1):** $$F = \rho Q V_1 (1 - (-1)) = 2\rho Q V_1$$ This is the **maximum theoretical force** any moving or stationary vane can exert on a jet. ## 3.2 Derivation: Force on a Single Moving Bucket (Pelton) Now consider the bucket moving with velocity **u** in the **same direction** as the jet. The jet's velocity **relative to the bucket** is: $$V_{r1} = V_1 - u$$ The mass of water striking the bucket per second is: $$\dot{m} = \rho Q_{effective} = \rho (Q) = \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot V_1$$ Wait — we must be careful: the jet velocity relative to the nozzle is V₁, but the **mass flow** through the nozzle is computed from the jet area and V₁ itself: ṁ = ρ · (π/4)d² · V₁. Since the jet does not change cross-section as the bucket moves (unlike reaction turbines), the **full jet mass flow Q acts on the bucket**. Applying the impulse-momentum equation along the direction of motion: **Relative velocity at entry** (along the direction of bucket motion): $$V_{r1} = V_1 - u$$ **The bucket turns the relative jet through an angle (180° − β)** at its exit (where β is the exit bucket angle from the tangential). The exit relative velocity magnitude is reduced due to friction: $$V_{r2} = K \cdot V_{r1} = K(V_1 - u)$$ The component of exit relative velocity in the **direction opposite to bucket motion** is: $$V_{r2}\cos\beta = K(V_1 - u)\cos\beta$$ **Force on bucket in tangential direction (Euler's Turbomachinery Equation):** $$F_t = \rho Q (V_{w1} - (-V_{w2})) = \rho Q (V_{w1} + V_{w2})$$ Where: - V_w1 = V₁ (the whirl component of the jet absolute velocity at entry) - V_w2 = u − K(V₁ − u)cos β (the whirl component of absolute velocity at exit, which is reversed) Substituting and simplifying: $$F_t = \rho Q \left[ V_1 + u - K(V_1 - u)\cos\beta \right]$$ $$F_t = \rho Q \left[ (V_1 - u)(1 + K\cos\beta) + 2u \right]$$ Or, rearranging for the **work-producing form** (recognising that work done = Force × velocity = F_t × u): $$\boxed{\ W = F_t \cdot u = \rho Q \left[ (V_1 - u) + K(V_1 - u)\cos\beta \right] u \ }$$ $$\boxed{\ W = \rho Q (V_1 - u)(1 + K\cos\beta) u \ }$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Condition for Maximum Work Done (KTU Favourite) To find the bucket speed that gives **maximum work output**, we differentiate W w.r.t. u and set it to zero. Let W = ρQ(V₁ − u)(1 + K cosβ) u Let (1 + K cosβ) = constant C (for a given bucket geometry). Then W = ρQ · C · (V₁ − u) · u Differentiating with respect to u: $$\dfrac{dW}{du} = \rho Q \cdot C \cdot (V_1 - 2u) = 0$$ Solving: $$V_1 - 2u = 0 \Rightarrow u = \dfrac{V_1}{2}$$ This gives the famous result: **For maximum work done, the bucket speed should be exactly half the jet velocity**, i.e., **Kᵤ = u/V₁ = 0.5**. > [!NOTE] > **Actual value used in practice:** Kᵤ = 0.43 to 0.48 (slightly less than 0.5) because accounting for bucket friction and leakage makes the optimum speed ratio marginally lower. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: How Each Performance Quantity is Computed in the Lab | Measured Quantity | Symbol | Instrument Used | How Computed | |---|---|---|---| | **Rotor speed** | N (rpm) | **Tachometer** (digital/non-contact) | Direct readout | | **Head at nozzle** | H (m of water) | **Bourdon-type Pressure Gauge** (kgf/cm²) + elevation | $H = \dfrac{p}{\rho g} + z$ (pressure head + datum head) | | **Discharge** | Q (m³/s) | **Measuring tank + stopwatch** | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Volume collected}}{\text{Time taken}}$ | | **Spring balance reading (load side)** | S (kgf) | Spring balance attached to rope end | Direct reading | | **Dead weight / empty balance** | W (kgf) | Spring balance with rope slack | Direct reading | | **Net load on brake drum** | (S − W) kgf | Difference | $F = (S - W) \cdot 9.81$ N | | **Effective brake-drum radius** | R_b (m) | Vernier calipers / scale | $R_b = (R_{drum} + t_{rope})$ | | **Torque** | T (N·m) | From rope-brake | $T = F \cdot R_b = 9.81(S - W) R_b$ | | **Shaft (brake) power** | P_sh (W) | Computed | $P_{sh} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | | **Input hydraulic power** | P_in (W) | Computed | $P_{in} = \rho g Q H$ | | **Overall efficiency** | η₀ | Computed | $\eta_0 = \dfrac{P_{sh}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60 \rho g Q H}$ | ## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Pattern) **Problem:** In a Pelton wheel test, the following observations were recorded: - Head at nozzle inlet, H = 60 m of water - Diameter of jet, d = 75 mm - Bucket wheel diameter, D = 600 mm - Speed of wheel, N = 600 rpm - Spring balance readings: S = 32 kgf, W = 4 kgf - Effective brake-drum radius, R_b = 0.30 m - Coefficient of velocity, Cᵥ = 0.97 **Calculate:** (i) Discharge, (ii) Power developed, (iii) Overall efficiency. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Jet velocity:** $$V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH} = 0.97 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 60}$$ $$V_1 = 0.97 \times \sqrt{1177.2} = 0.97 \times 34.31 = 33.28 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Discharge:** $$Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot V_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^2 \times 33.28$$ $$Q = 0.7854 \times 0.005625 \times 33.28 = 0.147 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Bucket peripheral speed:** $$u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.60 \times 600}{60} = 18.85 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 4 — Net load (in Newton):** $$F = (S - W) \times 9.81 = (32 - 4) \times 9.81 = 274.68 \text{ N}$$ **Step 5 — Torque:** $$T = F \times R_b = 274.68 \times 0.30 = 82.40 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 6 — Shaft (brake) power:** $$P_{sh} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60} = \dfrac{2 \times \pi \times 600 \times 82.40}{60}$$ $$P_{sh} = 5177.4 \text{ W} \approx 5.18 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 7 — Input hydraulic power:** $$P_{in} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.147 \times 60$$ $$P_{in} = 86{,}520 \text{ W} \approx 86.52 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 8 — Overall efficiency:** $$\eta_0 = \dfrac{P_{sh}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{5.18}{86.52} = 0.0599 = 5.99\%$$ **Step 9 — Speed ratio (sanity check):** $$K_u = \dfrac{u}{V_1} = \dfrac{18.85}{33.28} = 0.566$$ Since 0.566 > 0.5, the wheel is **running slightly above** the theoretical optimum speed ratio for this test point. Hence, the efficiency is low. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Pelton Wheel Test Rig ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Overhead Tank
Constant Head Source] B[Penstock
Long Delivery Pipe] C[Pressure Gauge
Bourdon Type] D[Needle Valve / Spear
Nozzle Inlet Control] E[Nozzle
Converging Diverging] F[High Velocity Jet
V1 = Cv Sqrt 2gH] G[Pelton Buckets
Splitter Type] H[Runner and Shaft
Rotating Assembly] I[Deflector Plate
Cut off Governor] J[Brake Drum
Fixed to Shaft] K[Rope Brake
Friction Loading] L[Spring Balance 1
Load Side S] M[Spring Balance 2
Dead Side W] N[Tachometer
Non Contact RPM] O[Measuring Tank
Volumetric Q] P[Stopwatch
Time Recording] Q[Data Processing Unit
Calculations] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H H --> I H --> J J --> K K --> L K --> M H --> N F --> O O --> P P --> Q C --> Q L --> Q M --> Q N --> Q ``` ## 4.2 Velocity Diagram at Bucket Inlet and Exit (Mermaid Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Entry A1[Absolute V1 Tangential Direction] --> A2[Relative Vr1 = V1 minus u along Bucket Motion] end subgraph Exit B1[Relative Vr2 = K times Vr1 Reversed] --> B2[Absolute Whirl Vw2 Opposing Bucket Motion] end subgraph Geometry C1[Bucket Angle beta measured from Tangent] end Entry --> Geometry Geometry --> Exit ``` ## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Label Layout of a Single Pelton Bucket ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[Central Splitter Ridge
Eliminates Axial Thrust] A2[Entry Tip
Sharp Edge to Cut Jet] A3[Concave Inner Surface
Catches Jet] A4[Exit Lip
Bucket Angle beta 165 to 170 deg] A5[Mounting Boss
Bolted to Runner Disc] A1 --> A3 A2 --> A3 A3 --> A4 A3 --> A5 ``` ## 4.4 Main Characteristic Curve Concept (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Constant Head H] --> B[Variable Nozzle Opening Q] B --> C[Variable Speed N] C --> D[Variable Torque T] D --> E[Variable Output Power P] E --> F[Efficiency Eta] F --> G[Plot Eta vs N at Constant H] F --> H[Plot Eta vs Q at Constant H] F --> I[Plot P vs N at Constant H] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define an impulse turbine. Why is a Pelton wheel preferred for high-head installations? **Model Answer:** An impulse turbine is one in which the **entire pressure energy of water is first converted into kinetic energy** by a nozzle, and this high-velocity jet then strikes the runner buckets to produce rotation. The runner operates at **atmospheric pressure** throughout. The Pelton wheel is preferred for high-head installations (H > 200 m) because: - The runner operates at atmospheric pressure, so **no cavitation** occurs. - The **simple bucket design** can withstand the high-velocity jet without complex sealing. - **High mechanical efficiency** (no leakage loss as in reaction turbines). - Easy to **govern** using a needle valve and deflector plate. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the function of a **splitter** in a Pelton wheel bucket? What happens if the splitter is omitted? **Model Answer:** The **splitter (central ridge)** divides the incoming jet into two equal halves symmetrically. This **cancels the axial thrust** that would otherwise push the runner sideways, thereby protecting the thrust bearing from excessive wear. If the splitter is omitted, the jet hits one curved side of the bucket, producing a **net axial force** on the runner. This causes excessive bearing wear, runner wobble, and eventual mechanical failure. --- ## PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Choice Option 1) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 5 Model Question Paper]** (a) Derive an expression for the **work done per second by a jet of water on a single moving bucket of a Pelton wheel**. State the condition for maximum work done. (7 marks) (b) In a Pelton wheel test, the head at the nozzle centreline is **65 m**, the jet diameter is **80 mm**, the wheel diameter is **1.2 m**, and the wheel speed is **500 rpm**. The deflector angle at the exit of the bucket is **165°** and the bucket friction factor is **0.9**. If the coefficient of velocity is **0.97**, calculate: - (i) The force exerted by the jet on the bucket. - (ii) The power developed by the wheel. (7 marks) #### Model Solution to (a): **Step 1:** Let jet velocity = V₁, bucket velocity = u, mass flow rate = ρQ. **Step 2:** Relative velocity at bucket entry: V_r1 = V₁ − u. **Step 3:** Relative velocity at bucket exit (reduced by friction factor K): V_r2 = K(V₁ − u). **Step 4:** Whirl velocity at entry: V_w1 = V₁ (jet is purely tangential). **Step 5:** Whirl velocity at exit (reversed direction): $$V_{w2} = u - K(V_1 - u)\cos\beta$$ **Step 6:** Euler's equation for tangential force on bucket: $$F_t = \rho Q (V_{w1} - (-V_{w2})) = \rho Q (V_1 + V_{w2})$$ $$F_t = \rho Q [V_1 + u - K(V_1 - u)\cos\beta]$$ **Step 7:** Work done per second: $$W = F_t \cdot u = \rho Q (V_1 - u)(1 + K\cos\beta) \cdot u$$ **Step 8:** For maximum work, differentiate W w.r.t. u and set to zero: $$\dfrac{dW}{du} = \rho Q (1 + K\cos\beta)(V_1 - 2u) = 0 \Rightarrow \boxed{u = \dfrac{V_1}{2}}$$ #### Model Solution to (b): **Step 1 — Jet velocity:** $$V_1 = C_v\sqrt{2gH} = 0.97 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 65} = 0.97 \times 35.72 = 34.65 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Bucket speed:** $$u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 1.2 \times 500}{60} = 31.42 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Discharge:** $$Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot V_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.080)^2 \times 34.65 = 0.1741 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 4 — Bucket exit angle:** $$\beta = 180° - 165° = 15° \Rightarrow \cos\beta = \cos 15° = 0.9659$$ **Step 5 — Whirl velocity at exit:** $$V_{w2} = u - K(V_1 - u)\cos\beta$$ $$V_{w2} = 31.42 - 0.9(34.65 - 31.42)(0.9659)$$ $$V_{w2} = 31.42 - 0.9(3.23)(0.9659) = 31.42 - 2.807 = 28.61 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 6 — Force on bucket:** $$F_t = \rho Q (V_1 + V_{w2}) = 1000 \times 0.1741 \times (34.65 + 28.61)$$ $$F_t = 1000 \times 0.1741 \times 63.26 = 11{,}015 \text{ N} \approx 11.02 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 7 — Power developed:** $$P = F_t \times u = 11{,}015 \times 31.42 = 346{,}090 \text{ W} \approx 346.1 \text{ kW}$$ --- ### Question B (Choice Option 2) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** (a) Explain with a neat sketch the **components of a Pelton wheel test rig** and state the function of each. (7 marks) (b) The following data were obtained during a test on a single-jet Pelton wheel: - Net head at nozzle = 30 m - Diameter of jet = 50 mm - Discharge measured = 0.040 m³/s - Wheel diameter = 0.80 m - Wheel speed = 450 rpm - Effective radius of brake drum = 0.25 m - Spring balance readings: S = 18 kgf, W = 2 kgf Calculate: (i) Power developed by the runner, (ii) Hydraulic power input, (iii) Overall efficiency. (7 marks) #### Model Solution to (a): A Pelton wheel test rig consists of: 1. **Overhead tank** – provides constant head. 2. **Penstock** – delivers water from tank to nozzle. 3. **Pressure gauge** – measures head at nozzle. 4. **Needle (spear) valve & nozzle** – converts pressure energy to kinetic energy, also controls discharge. 5. **Runner with buckets** – receives jet and converts kinetic energy to mechanical (shaft) energy. 6. **Deflector plate** – momentarily deflects jet during sudden load changes to prevent racing. 7. **Brake drum** – mounted on the shaft to apply load via rope. 8. **Rope-brake dynamometer** – two spring balances S and W measure friction torque. 9. **Tachometer** – measures rotational speed. 10. **Measuring tank and stopwatch** – measure discharge. #### Model Solution to (b): **Step 1 — Net load:** $$F = (S - W) \times 9.81 = (18 - 2) \times 9.81 = 156.96 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2 — Torque:** $$T = F \times R_b = 156.96 \times 0.25 = 39.24 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 3 — Power developed (shaft/brake power):** $$P_{sh} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60} = \dfrac{2 \times \pi \times 450 \times 39.24}{60}$$ $$P_{sh} = 1849.5 \text{ W} \approx 1.85 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4 — Hydraulic input power:** $$P_{in} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.040 \times 30 = 11{,}772 \text{ W} \approx 11.77 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5 — Overall efficiency:** $$\eta_0 = \dfrac{P_{sh}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{1.85}{11.77} = 0.1572 = 15.72\%$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign of V_w2:** Forgetting that the exit whirl is **reversed** and using the wrong sign in Euler's equation can give a **negative work output** or incorrect efficiency. Always use **V₁ + V_w2** (where V_w2 is positive as derived above). > 2. **Units mismatch:** Using kgf for force and N for everything else. Convert: 1 kgf = 9.81 N. > 3. **Head type confusion:** Gross head vs Net head. If the problem says "head at nozzle inlet", treat it as **gross head H_g**. If it says "head utilized by turbine", use **net head H_n = H_g − nozzle losses**. > 4. **Forgetting to multiply discharge by bucket exit area:** Unlike reaction turbines, for Pelton wheels the **full jet discharge Q impinges on the bucket** (the bucket does not fill up like a Francis runner). > 5. **Brake-drum radius:** Use the **effective radius** = (R_drum + t_rope), NOT just R_drum. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ Impulse turbine → pressure conversion in **nozzle**, energy transfer in **runner at atmospheric pressure**. - ✅ Pelton wheel = bucket-type impulse turbine, used for **H > 200 m**. - ✅ Jet velocity: $V_1 = C_v\sqrt{2gH}$; Bucket speed: $u = \pi DN/60$. - ✅ **Speed ratio for maximum efficiency:** $K_u = u/V_1 = 0.43 - 0.48$ (ideal 0.5). - ✅ Force on bucket: $F_t = \rho Q (V_1 + V_{w2})$. - ✅ Work done: $W = \rho Q (V_1 - u)(1 + K\cos\beta) \cdot u$. - ✅ Hydraulic power: $P_{in} = \rho g Q H$. - ✅ Shaft power: $P_{sh} = 2\pi NT/60 = (S-W) \times 9.81 \times R_b \times 2\pi N/60$. - ✅ Overall efficiency: $\eta_0 = P_{sh} / P_{in}$. - ✅ **Splitter** in bucket → cancels axial thrust. - ✅ **Deflector plate** → for emergency governing (sudden load rejection). - ✅ **Needle valve (spear)** → controls discharge; also serves as a normal governor. - ✅ Rope-brake dynamometer: two spring balances — loaded side S, dead side W. Net force = (S − W) × 9.81 N. - ✅ **Main characteristic curve**: η vs N/Q at constant H; used to find the **best efficiency point** and design operating point. - ✅ **Specific speed of Pelton wheel** is the **lowest** among all turbines (single-jet: 10–35, multi-jet: up to 100). - ✅ **Volumetric efficiency** is **not** significant for Pelton (assumed 1.0 unless leakage through spear valve is mentioned). - ✅ Bucket angle β is typically **15° to 20°** (so the jet is turned by 160° to 170°). - ✅ Use **Cᵥ = 0.95 to 0.99**; **C_d (nozzle) = 0.98 to 0.99**; **K (bucket friction) = 0.85 to 0.95**. - ✅ Remember to convert **kgf/cm² to metres of water**: 1 kgf/cm² ≈ 10 m of water.

topics.title: Performance Test on Reaction Turbines (Francis and Kaplan Turbines)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiments
> - **Topic:** Performance Test on Reaction Turbines (Francis and Kaplan Turbines)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Reaction Turbine**: A hydraulic turbine in which the runner operates fully submerged in flowing water and the available hydraulic energy is converted into mechanical work by both a **pressure drop** and a **kinetic energy change** occurring *across the runner itself*. Unlike impulse turbines (e.g., Pelton wheel) where pressure remains atmospheric throughout the runner, in a reaction turbine the fluid pressure at the runner inlet is significantly higher than at the runner outlet.

**Sub-classifications under KTU 2024 PCAUL307 syllabus:**

1. **Francis Turbine** – A mixed-flow reaction turbine where water enters radially and exits axially. Operates under **medium head (30 m to 600 m)** and **medium discharge** conditions.
2. **Kaplan Turbine** – An axial-flow reaction turbine fitted with adjustable (movable) runner blades and adjustable guide vanes. Operates under **low head (< 30 m)** and **high discharge** conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Performance Test** on these turbines is a mandatory lab experiment in PCAUL307. The objective is to determine the **overall efficiency**, **shaft power output**, and to plot the **constant-head characteristic curves** (e.g., η vs V, η vs Q, η vs N) and the **constant-speed characteristic curves** (main characteristic curves).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Bucket-Press" Intuition)

Imagine holding a **lawn-sprinkler pipe** that is connected to a pressurized water tap. As pressurized water squirts *out* of the spinning end, the reaction force *pushes* the pipe backwards. That backward push is the **reaction force** – it does useful work.

- In a **Pelton (impulse) turbine**, water hits a bucket *externally* – like a ball hitting a bat.
- In a **reaction turbine**, water *flows through* a closed runner filled with blades, and the water itself pushes the blades outward as it loses pressure – like air pushing the blades of a *table fan* from inside, except the air is pressurized water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple memory hook**:
> - *Francis Turbine* → "FRancis is **F**or **R**ivers" (medium head, used in reservoirs/dams) 🏞️
> - *Kaplan Turbine* → "**K**aplan is **K**ind to **L**ow heads" (low head, high flow, used in run-of-river) 🌊

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Used

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | **g** | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water | **ρ** | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Specific weight of water | **w = ρg** | **9810 N/m³** |
| Atmospheric pressure | **p_atm** | **101.325 kPa** (reference) |
| Standard test head range (Francis) | H | 30–600 m |
| Standard test head range (Kaplan) | H | < 30 m |

---

## 1.4 Syllabus Highlight Callout

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – PCAUL307 Expected Outcomes (CO Mapping)**:
> - **CO3**: *Conduct performance tests on turbines and plot characteristic curves.*
> - **CO4**: *Analyze experimental data to determine efficiency, specific speed, and unit quantities.*
> The reaction turbine experiment directly maps to **Bloom's Level 3 (Apply)** and **Bloom's Level 4 (Analyze)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept – How a Reaction Turbine Works

The hydraulic-to-mechanical energy conversion in a reaction turbine follows the **Euler Turbomachinery Equation**, applied in three distinct stages:

### Stage A: Spiral Casing (Francis) / Penstock Distributor (Kaplan)
- Water from the high-pressure penstock enters a **scroll casing** (Francis) or a **concrete spiral** (Kaplan) which distributes it **symmetrically** (axi-symmetrically) around the runner circumference.
- **Purpose:** Ensure equal flow to every guide vane passage → uniform torque.

### Stage B: Guide Vanes (Stator / Wicket Gates)
- Flowing water meets the **stationary guide vanes (wicket gates)**.
- These are curved blades that convert a major portion of the **pressure energy** into **kinetic (velocity) energy**, and direct the water at a specific angle (α) onto the runner blades.
- The angle of the guide vanes can be adjusted externally to control the flow rate **Q** entering the runner.

### Stage C: Runner (Rotor)
- Water entering the runner is already under high pressure and high velocity.
- As it flows over the curved runner blades, **both**:
  - (i) A **pressure drop** occurs (Δp across the runner is non-zero), and
  - (ii) The **relative velocity** of water changes direction.
- The combination produces a **reaction force** on the curved blades → **torque** is transmitted to the shaft.

### Stage D: Draft Tube
- The kinetic energy of the water leaving the runner is recovered as **pressure energy** in the **draft tube**, which is a gradually expanding passage connecting the runner outlet to the tailrace.
- A draft tube allows the turbine to be set **above** the tailwater level while still using the head difference up to the tailrace.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why pressure drop is necessary:** Per Newton's Third Law, if the fluid imparts a force on the runner blade (causing rotation), the blade *must* exert an equal and opposite force on the fluid, causing its **pressure to drop** and its **velocity to change**. This is the **"reaction"** that gives the turbine its name.

---

## 2.2 Velocity Triangle Fundamentals (KTU Critical Concept)

For a reaction turbine, the **inlet and outlet velocity triangles** of the runner must be drawn to apply Euler's equation:

$$ \text{Euler Head Transferred} : \quad H_{Euler} = \frac{U_1 V_{w1} - U_2 V_{w2}}{g} $$

where:
- **U** = Blade velocity (tangential velocity of the runner)
- **V** = Absolute velocity of water
- **V_w** = Whirl component (tangential component of absolute velocity)
- **V_f** = Flow component (radial/axial component of absolute velocity)
- **V_r** = Relative velocity of water w.r.t. blade

**For Francis Turbine (Mixed Flow):**
- Inlet: Water enters **radially** → V_w1 = V_1 (tangential component is zero in pure radial entry)
- Outlet: Water exits **axially** → V_w2 = 0 (ideal case for maximum efficiency)

**For Kaplan Turbine (Axial Flow):**
- Both inlet and outlet are **axial** → U_1 = U_2 = πDN/60
- V_w1 ≠ 0 (swirl at inlet), V_w2 ≈ 0 (design condition)

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are exam-critical.** KTU frequently asks derivations or numericals based on these expressions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Input Hydraulic Power | $P_{in} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H$ | W (or kW) | ρ=1000 kg/m³, g=9.81 m/s² |
| 2 | Specific Weight Form | $P_{in} = w \, Q \, H$ | W | w = ρg = 9810 N/m³ |
| 3 | Shaft Power (Brake) | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W | N in rpm, T in N·m |
| 4 | Water Power (Output of runner) | $P_{water} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H_{net}$ | W | Net head on runner |
| 5 | Hydraulic Efficiency | $\eta_h = \dfrac{V_{w1} \, U_1 \pm V_{w2} \, U_2}{gH}$ | – | 0.85 to 0.95 typical |
| 6 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{water}}$ | – | Accounts for friction losses |
| 7 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | – | 0.97 to 0.99 typical |
| 8 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{in}} = \eta_h \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{vol}$ | – | Final answer for experiment |
| 9 | Specific Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | (SI units) | Francis: 60–400, Kaplan: 300–1000 |
| 10 | Unit Speed | $N_u = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}}$ | rpm·m$^{-1/2}$ | Used for plotting |
| 11 | Unit Discharge | $Q_u = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}$ | m³/s·m$^{-1/2}$ | Used for plotting |
| 12 | Unit Power | $P_u = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}}$ | kW·m$^{-3/2}$ | Used for plotting |
| 13 | Discharge (V-notch) | $Q = \dfrac{8}{15} \, C_d \, \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, H_n^{5/2}$ | m³/s | θ = 90° for standard V-notch |
| 14 | Discharge (Rectangular notch) | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \, C_d \, L \, \sqrt{2g} \, H_n^{3/2}$ | m³/s | L = notch width |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PIPE-SYMBOL RULE (Handled)**: In the table above, no raw `|` is used inside formula cells. Use $\vert$ in LaTeX for any future absolute-value expressions to prevent markdown table corruption.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of This Topic

- **Hydroelectric Power Plants (HEPs)**: ~16% of global electricity is generated by reaction turbines.
- **Pumped Storage Plants (PSPs)**: Reversible Francis turbines act as pumps during off-peak hours.
- **Tidal & Run-of-River Plants**: Kaplan turbines are dominant due to low head availability.
- **Irrigation Canals & Small Hydro (<25 MW)**: Kaplan is preferred.
- **Dam Spillway Recovery**: Bulb/Strafloturbines (Kaplan variants) are installed in dam outlets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Euler's Head Equation for a Reaction Turbine

### Starting Premise
Consider an elemental ring at the runner. Let:
- **V₁** = absolute velocity of water at runner inlet
- **V₂** = absolute velocity of water at runner outlet
- **U₁, U₂** = tangential (blade) velocities at inlet and outlet
- **V_w1, V_w2** = whirl (tangential) components of V₁ and V₂
- **V_f1, V_f2** = flow (radial/axial) components of V₁ and V₂
- **V_r1, V_r2** = relative velocities of water w.r.t. blade
- **α₁, α₂** = guide vane and exit angles (in absolute frame)
- **β₁, β₂** = blade angles (in relative frame)
- **ω** = angular velocity of runner (rad/s)
- **m** = mass flow rate of water (kg/s)

---

### **Step 1: Angular Momentum at Inlet**

The torque exerted by water on the runner is given by the **rate of change of angular momentum**:

$$ T_{runner} = \dot{m} \, (V_{w1} \, r_1 - V_{w2} \, r_2) $$

where $r_1$ and $r_2$ are the inlet and outlet radii of the runner.

---

### **Step 2: Work Done per Unit Time (Power)**

Power transferred to the runner equals torque multiplied by angular velocity:

$$ P = T_{runner} \cdot \omega = \dot{m} \, \omega \, (V_{w1} r_1 - V_{w2} r_2) $$

---

### **Step 3: Substitute Blade Velocity**

Blade velocity at any radius is $U = \omega r$. Substituting $U_1 = \omega r_1$ and $U_2 = \omega r_2$:

$$ P = \dot{m} \, (V_{w1} U_1 - V_{w2} U_2) $$

---

### **Step 4: Express Power in Terms of Head**

Mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho Q$. Work done per unit weight of water = Euler head $H_E$:

$$ H_E = \frac{P}{\dot{m} \, g} = \frac{V_{w1} U_1 - V_{w2} U_2}{g} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the famous Euler Turbomachinery Equation** — applicable to *all* turbomachines (turbines, pumps, compressors). For a pure impulse turbine, $V_{w2} \neq 0$ and is significant; for an ideal reaction turbine with axial exit, $V_{w2} \approx 0$.

---

### **Step 5: Hydraulic Efficiency for Reaction Turbine**

Hydraulic efficiency is the ratio of Euler head to net head supplied:

$$ \eta_h = \frac{H_E}{H_{net}} = \frac{V_{w1} U_1 - V_{w2} U_2}{g \, H_{net}} $$

For a **Francis turbine with radial inlet** ($V_{w1} = 0$ is *wrong*; actually for Francis, water enters radially, so $V_1 = V_{f1}$ and $V_{w1}$ comes from guide vane action) and **axial exit** ($V_{w2} = 0$):

$$ \boxed{\eta_h = \frac{V_{w1} U_1}{g H_{net}}} $$

For a **Kaplan turbine** with axial flow throughout and $V_{w2} = 0$:

$$ \eta_h = \frac{V_{w1} U_1 - V_{w2} U_2}{g H} = \frac{V_{w1} U_1}{g H} $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Speed

Specific speed ($N_s$) is defined as the speed of a *geometrically similar* turbine that would produce **unit power (1 kW) under unit head (1 m)**.

For similar turbines operating at the same specific speed:

$$ \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} = \text{constant} = N_s $$

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1**: Consider two geometrically similar turbines A and B. By similarity:

$$ \frac{Q_A}{Q_B} = \left(\frac{D_A}{D_B}\right)^2 \left(\frac{H_A}{H_B}\right)^{1/2}, \quad \frac{N_A}{N_B} = \left(\frac{H_A}{H_B}\right)^{1/2} $$

**Step 2**: Power scales as $P = \rho g Q H$. Using the Q similarity:

$$ P \propto D^2 H^{3/2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D \propto \frac{P^{1/2}}{H^{3/4}} $$

**Step 3**: Combining with N similarity:

$$ N \propto \frac{1}{D} H^{1/2} \propto H^{1/2} \cdot \frac{H^{3/4}}{P^{1/2}} = \frac{H^{5/4}}{P^{1/2}} $$

**Step 4**: Rearranging:

$$ N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} $$

This is **dimensionally** $N_s = [\text{rpm} \cdot \text{kW}^{1/2} \cdot \text{m}^{-5/4}]$ in SI units (metric) and is the most important index for **turbine selection**:

| Specific Speed Range | Turbine Type |
|---|---|
| 8 to 35 | Pelton (single jet) |
| 30 to 150 | Pelton (multi-jet) |
| 60 to 400 | **Francis** |
| 300 to 1000 | **Kaplan / Propeller** |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Discharge Through a 90° V-Notch (Standard Lab Setup)

In the KTU lab, discharge is measured by a **V-notch (triangular) weir** fitted to the tailrace.

$$ Q = \frac{8}{15} \, C_d \, \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, H_n^{5/2} $$

For $\theta = 90°$: $\tan(45°) = 1$, so:

$$ \boxed{Q = \frac{8}{15} \, C_d \, \sqrt{2g} \, H_n^{5/2} \approx 2.362 \, C_d \, H_n^{5/2}} $$

where $H_n$ = head over the V-notch apex (m), $C_d$ ≈ 0.6.

---

## 3.4 Sample Numerical (KTU-Style Worked Example)

**Problem (Adapted for KTU 2024 Pattern):**
In a Francis turbine performance test, the following observations were recorded:
- Net head, $H$ = **40 m**
- Discharge, $Q$ = **0.20 m³/s**
- Shaft speed, $N$ = **600 rpm**
- Brake torque, $T$ = **80 N·m**
- V-notch reading, $H_n$ = 0.18 m (assume $C_d$ = 0.6)

**Find:** (i) Input Power, (ii) Shaft Power, (iii) Overall Efficiency, (iv) Specific Speed.

---

### **Solution:**

**(i) Input Hydraulic Power:**

$$ P_{in} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.20 \times 40 $$

$$ P_{in} = 78{,}480 \text{ W} \approx 78.48 \text{ kW} $$

**(ii) Shaft (Brake) Power:**

$$ P_{shaft} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 600 \times 80}{60} $$

$$ P_{shaft} = \frac{301{,}592.89}{60} \approx 5026.55 \text{ W} \approx 5.03 \text{ kW} $$

**(iii) Overall Efficiency:**

$$ \eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{P_{in}} = \frac{5.03}{78.48} \approx 0.0641 $$

$$ \boxed{\eta_{overall} \approx 6.41\%} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall**: A common student error is forgetting to convert $T$ (N·m) correctly with the $2\pi/60$ factor. Always show the units *step-by-step*: $N$ in rpm → $\omega$ in rad/s via $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.

**(iv) Specific Speed (SI Units):**

$$ N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} = \frac{600 \times \sqrt{5.03}}{(40)^{5/4}} $$

$$ N_s = \frac{600 \times 2.243}{95.27} \approx 14.12 \text{ rpm·kW}^{1/2}\text{·m}^{-5/4} $$

This value is **too low for a Francis turbine**, indicating either a test condition outside the design range or that the test setup mimics a Pelton-like regime at this low flow. In an actual exam, if the calculated $N_s$ is between 60–400, it confirms Francis regime.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix (Francis vs. Kaplan)

| Parameter | Francis Turbine | Kaplan Turbine |
|---|---|---|
| Specific Speed ($N_s$) | 60 – 400 rpm (SI) | 300 – 1000 rpm (SI) |
| Head Range | 30 – 600 m | < 30 m |
| Discharge | Medium | High |
| Runner Type | Mixed flow (radial→axial) | Axial flow (propeller) |
| Blade Adjustability | Fixed (or partly movable) | Fully movable (pitch control) |
| Guide Vane | Adjustable | Adjustable |
| Draft Tube | Conical / Elbow | Conical (long, tapered) |
| Setting | Above tailwater (with draft tube) | Mostly above tailwater |
| Typical Efficiency | 85 – 92% | 90 – 94% |
| Part Load Behavior | Drops sharply below 60% load | Maintains high η due to blade pitch |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords), and labels contain no `**` bold or italics inside node text.

---
