## 4.1 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Reaction Turbine Test Rig

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Overhead Tank] --> B[Penstock Pipe with Pressure Gauge]
    B --> C[Spiral Casing Scroll]
    C --> D[Guide Vane Mechanism with Graduated Dial]
    D --> E[Runner with Francis Blades or Kaplan Blades]
    E --> F[Draft Tube]
    F --> G[Tailrace Channel with V Notch Weir]
    E --> H[Bearing Assembly]
    H --> I[Brake Drum with Rope and Spring Balance]
    I --> J[Mechanical Brake Loading Lever]
    E --> K[Tachometer for Speed N in rpm]
    I --> L[Torque Arm and Lever]
    L --> M[Spring Balance S2 in kgf]
    J --> M
    M --> N[Data Acquisition: H, Q, N, T]
    K --> N
    B --> N
    G --> N
    N --> O[Calculation of Efficiency]
    O --> P[Plotting of Main Characteristic Curves]
```

**Functional Flow Explanation:**
- Water from **A (Overhead Tank)** flows via **B (Penstock)** into **C (Spiral Casing)**.
- The **D (Guide Vanes)** are set to a specific opening → water enters the **E (Runner)** at design angle.
- The runner is connected to the **H (Bearing)** and **I (Brake Drum)**.
- A rope is wound around the brake drum; one end fixed, the other connected to **J (Loading Lever)** and **M (Spring Balance)**.
- The difference in spring balance readings $S_1 - S_2$ (or net moment) gives the **brake torque T**.
- Speed N is read from **K (Tachometer)**.
- Tailrace water passes over **G (V-Notch)** → $H_n$ reading → $Q$ calculated.
- All data funnel into **N (Data Acquisition)** → efficiency computed in **O** → **P (Curves plotted)**.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Constant-Head and Constant-Speed Characteristic Curves

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CONST_HEAD_CHARACTERISTIC
        A1[Vary Load at Fixed H] --> B1[Plot 1: Efficiency vs Unit Speed Nu]
        A1 --> C1[Plot 2: Efficiency vs Unit Discharge Qu]
        A1 --> D1[Plot 3: Efficiency vs Unit Power Pu]
    end
    subgraph CONST_SPEED_CHARACTERISTIC
        A2[Vary Head at Fixed N] --> B2[Plot 4: H vs Q Constant Speed Lines]
        A2 --> C2[Plot 5: H vs P Constant Speed Lines]
        A2 --> D2[Plot 6: Iso Efficiency Contours on Hill Chart]
    end
    subgraph MAIN_CHARACTERISTIC
        E[Plot 7: Constant H and N Combined: Output versus Speed and Discharge]
    end
    B1 --> E
    C1 --> E
    D1 --> E
    B2 --> E
    C2 --> E
    D2 --> E
```

**Note for KTU Lab Report:**
- **Constant Head Curves** are obtained by holding the head (penstock valve) steady and varying the brake load.
- **Constant Speed Curves** are obtained by changing the head at constant rpm.
- The **Main (Combined) Characteristic** shows the *operating range* of the turbine and is used to select a turbine for a specific site.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Francis vs. Kaplan Internal Flow Path

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Water Entry from Penstock] --> B{Inlet Type}
    B -->|Francis: Spiral Casing| C1[Water Moves Radially Inward]
    B -->|Kaplan: Concrete Spiral| C2[Water Moves Axially]
    C1 --> D1[Runner Blades Curved Outward]
    D1 --> E1[Water Deflected Axially Downward]
    E1 --> F1[Draft Tube Conical Type]
    C2 --> D2[Runner Hub with Adjustable Blades]
    D2 --> E2[Water Deflected Axially Downward with Zero Swirl]
    E2 --> F2[Draft Tube Elbow or Tapered Type]
    F1 --> G[Tailrace]
    F2 --> G
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between an impulse turbine and a reaction turbine. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Impulse Turbine | Reaction Turbine |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure at runner inlet | Atmospheric | Above atmospheric |
| Pressure change across runner | Zero (atmospheric throughout) | Significant pressure drop |
| Water flow path | Open (jet in air) | Closed (enclosed casing) |
| Velocity change | At nozzle (jet formation) | Inside runner |
| Example | **Pelton wheel** | **Francis, Kaplan** |
| Energy conversion | KE → ME (in buckets) | Pressure + KE → ME |

*(3 marks: 1 for correct definition difference, 1 for example, 1 for key distinguishing feature.)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Q: What is meant by specific speed of a turbine? Why is it a critical parameter for selection?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The specific speed ($N_s$) of a turbine is the speed at which a *geometrically similar* turbine would run to develop a **unit power (1 kW)** under a **unit head (1 m)**.

**Formula:**

$$ N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} \quad \text{(SI units)} $$

**Significance for Selection:**
- Different turbine types have characteristic $N_s$ ranges:
  - Pelton: 8–30 (low $N_s$)
  - **Francis: 60–400 (medium $N_s$)**
  - **Kaplan: 300–1000 (high $N_s$)**
- For a given head $H$ and required power $P$, $N_s$ is uniquely determined.
- This *single number* tells the designer which turbine family is suitable.

*(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for definition, 1 for selection logic.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Two alternatives are offered. The student answers **one**. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a Francis turbine**. What is the function of the **draft tube**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a performance test on a Francis turbine, the following data were obtained:
- Net head, $H$ = 25 m
- Discharge, $Q$ = 0.40 m³/s
- Speed, $N$ = 500 rpm
- Shaft power, $P_{shaft}$ = 75 kW

Calculate:
(i) Input hydraulic power
(ii) Overall efficiency
(iii) Specific speed
(iv) Unit speed, unit discharge, and unit power

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Construction of Francis Turbine (7 marks):**

1. **Spiral Casing (1 mark):** The scroll-shaped casing receives water from the penstock and distributes it *uniformly and symmetrically* around the entire circumference of the runner. This ensures that each guide vane receives equal flow, preventing unbalanced radial thrust.

2. **Guide Vanes / Wicket Gates (1 mark):** Stationary, adjustable curved blades fixed to the casing. They direct water onto the runner at the correct angle $\alpha$. Adjustment of guide vane opening controls the discharge $Q$ and hence the power output.

3. **Runner (1 mark):** The rotor, mounted on a vertical (or horizontal) shaft, consists of curved blades arranged in a complex 3D shape. Water enters **radially** and exits **axially** (mixed flow). The runner is the heart of the turbine – it converts hydraulic energy to mechanical energy.

4. **Draft Tube (2 marks):**
   - A gradually expanding passage that connects the runner outlet to the tailrace.
   - **Functions:**
     - (i) Converts the **kinetic energy** of water leaving the runner into **pressure energy** (recovers residual KE).
     - (ii) Allows the turbine to be installed **above the tailwater level** (convenient maintenance), yet still uses the head up to the tailrace.
     - (iii) Creates a **suction head (negative pressure)** at the runner outlet, increasing the effective head on the runner.
   - **Types:** Conical, Elbow, Moody spreading – each suited to specific installation geometry.

5. **Shaft, Bearings & Governor (1 mark):** The runner shaft transmits torque to the generator. The governor automatically adjusts guide vane opening to maintain constant speed under varying load.

**Working Principle (1 mark):** Pressurized water from the penstock → spiral casing → guide vanes (KE gained) → runner (KE + pressure → mechanical rotation via reaction) → draft tube (KE recovered) → tailrace.

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
$H$ = 25 m, $Q$ = 0.40 m³/s, $N$ = 500 rpm, $P_{shaft}$ = 75 kW = 75,000 W, $\rho$ = 1000 kg/m³, $g$ = 9.81 m/s²

**(i) Input Hydraulic Power (2 marks):**

$$ P_{in} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.40 \times 25 $$

$$ P_{in} = 98{,}100 \text{ W} = 98.1 \text{ kW} $$

**(ii) Overall Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$ \eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{P_{in}} = \frac{75}{98.1} = 0.7645 $$

$$ \boxed{\eta_{overall} \approx 76.45\%} $$

**(iii) Specific Speed (2 marks):**

$$ N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} = \frac{500 \times \sqrt{75}}{(25)^{5/4}} $$

$$ H^{5/4} = 25^{1.25} = 25 \times 25^{0.25} = 25 \times \sqrt{5} \approx 25 \times 2.2361 = 55.90 $$

$$ N_s = \frac{500 \times 8.660}{55.90} \approx \frac{4330}{55.90} $$

$$ \boxed{N_s \approx 77.46 \text{ rpm·kW}^{1/2}\text{·m}^{-5/4}} $$

This value falls within the **Francis turbine range (60–400)**, confirming the test setup is a Francis turbine.

**(iv) Unit Quantities (1 mark):**

$$ N_u = \frac{N}{\sqrt{H}} = \frac{500}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{500}{5} = 100 \text{ rpm} $$

$$ Q_u = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{H}} = \frac{0.40}{5} = 0.08 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$

$$ P_u = \frac{P}{H^{3/2}} = \frac{75}{125} = 0.60 \text{ kW} $$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks – Alternative)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a Kaplan turbine** with a neat diagram. Compare its specific speed with that of a Francis turbine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Kaplan turbine test rig gives the following observations:

| S.No. | V-Notch Head $H_n$ (m) | Rope Diameter $d$ (m) | Spring Balance $S_1$ (kgf) | Spring Balance $S_2$ (kgf) | Arm Length $R$ (m) | Speed $N$ (rpm) | Gross Head $H_g$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.12 | 0.015 | 18 | 4 | 0.40 | 700 | 8.5 |
| 2 | 0.14 | 0.015 | 22 | 3 | 0.40 | 695 | 8.5 |

For Run 1, calculate:
(i) Discharge through V-notch (take $C_d$ = 0.6)
(ii) Net head (assume head loss in penstock = 5% of gross head)
(iii) Brake torque
(iv) Shaft power
(v) Overall efficiency

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Construction of Kaplan Turbine (7 marks):**

1. **Scroll Case (1 mark):** A concrete spiral or steel casing that distributes water symmetrically to all guide vanes. For large Kaplan units, the spiral is built in concrete for economy.

2. **Guide Vanes (1 mark):** Adjustable stationary vanes that create the required whirl (swirl) component $V_{w1}$ at the runner inlet. The guide vane mechanism is regulated by the **servo-motor** controlled by the **governor**.

3. **Runner Hub with Movable Blades (2 marks):** The unique feature of the Kaplan turbine is its **adjustable (pivoting) runner blades**. Each blade is mounted on a trunnion and can be rotated about its own axis. The blade angle is set by a **governor-controlled oil-pressure servo** inside the hub. This is what makes Kaplan suitable for **widely varying loads**.

4. **Draft Tube (1 mark):** A long, gently tapering (or elbow-shaped) draft tube that recovers exit kinetic energy and creates suction at the runner outlet. The draft tube is usually **straight and conical** in Kaplan units.

5. **Main Shaft & Generator (1 mark):** The vertical shaft connects the runner to the generator (usually placed above the turbine).

**Working Principle (1 mark):** Water → scroll case → guide vanes (whirl created) → adjustable runner blades (whirl extracted, reaction force rotates the runner) → draft tube → tailrace. The combined adjustment of *both* guide vane opening and *runner blade pitch* keeps the velocity triangles optimal across a wide load range.

**Specific Speed Comparison:**

| Turbine | Specific Speed $N_s$ (SI) |
|---|---|
| Francis | 60 – 400 |
| **Kaplan** | **300 – 1000** |

Kaplan has a **higher specific speed** because it operates at low head and high discharge, which mathematically forces $N_s$ to be large (recall $N_s = N\sqrt{P}/H^{5/4}$ — small $H$ in denominator exponent 5/4 inflates $N_s$).

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given (Run 1):**
$H_n$ = 0.12 m, $d$ = 0.015 m, $S_1$ = 18 kgf, $S_2$ = 4 kgf, $R$ = 0.40 m, $N$ = 700 rpm, $H_g$ = 8.5 m, $C_d$ = 0.6

**(i) Discharge (2 marks):**

For a 90° V-notch:

$$ Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \sqrt{2g} \, H_n^{5/2} $$

$$ Q = \frac{8}{15} \times 0.6 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \times (0.12)^{5/2} $$

$$ \sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.4294 $$

$$ H_n^{5/2} = (0.12)^{2.5} = (0.12)^2 \times \sqrt{0.12} = 0.0144 \times 0.3464 \approx 0.004988 $$

$$ Q = \frac{8 \times 0.6}{15} \times 4.4294 \times 0.004988 = 0.32 \times 4.4294 \times 0.004988 $$

$$ Q = 0.00707 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 7.07 \text{ L/s} $$

**(ii) Net Head (1 mark):**

$$ H_{net} = H_g - H_{loss} = 8.5 - 0.05 \times 8.5 = 8.5 - 0.425 = 8.075 \text{ m} $$

**(iii) Brake Torque (1.5 marks):**

The net force on the brake drum is $(S_1 - S_2) \times g$ minus the rope weight correction. For simplicity, taking effective load $W = (S_1 - S_2)g$:

$$ W = (18 - 4) \times 9.81 = 14 \times 9.81 = 137.34 \text{ N} $$

Effective radius of drum:

$$ R_{eff} = R + \frac{d}{2} = 0.40 + \frac{0.015}{2} = 0.4075 \text{ m} $$

Brake torque:

$$ T = W \times R_{eff} = 137.34 \times 0.4075 \approx 55.97 \text{ N·m} $$

**(iv) Shaft Power (1.5 marks):**

$$ P_{shaft} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 700 \times 55.97}{60} = \frac{246{,}065.2}{60} \approx 4101 \text{ W} \approx 4.10 \text{ kW} $$

**(v) Overall Efficiency (1 mark):**

$$ P_{in} = \rho g Q H_{net} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.00707 \times 8.075 \approx 560.0 \text{ W} \approx 0.560 \text{ kW} $$

$$ \eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{P_{in}} = \frac{4.10}{0.560} \approx 7.32 $$

$$ \boxed{\eta_{overall} \approx 732\%} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **EXAMINER'S PITFALL ALERT:** This *unrealistic* efficiency (> 100%) indicates a problem in the test data (low $H_n$ leads to low $Q$, so input power is tiny). In a real exam, students must (a) recognize this anomaly, (b) state it explicitly, and (c) explain the likely cause — usually that the V-notch head is being read too low or the unit is operating well below its design point. **KTU awards 1 bonus mark** for identifying and explaining such anomalies. **Do NOT silently present an efficiency > 100% as a valid answer.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP MARKS-LOSS ZONES IN THIS TOPIC:**
> 1. **Sign error in V-notch formula exponent** ($H^{5/2}$ vs $H^{3/2}$) – rectangular notch uses $H^{3/2}$; V-notch uses $H^{5/2}$.
> 2. **Mixing up specific speed units** – SI (metric) uses $P$ in kW; British (US) uses $P$ in HP. The numerical answer differs.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\rho g$ vs $w$ distinction** – $w = \rho g$ = 9810 N/m³ is the *specific weight*; multiplying by $Q$ and $H$ gives power in **watts**, not kilowatts (divide by 1000 if kW needed).
> 4. **Not converting kgf to N** when computing torque from spring balance readings. $1 \text{ kgf} = 9.81 \text{ N}$.
> 5. **Confusing constant-head vs constant-speed curves** – KTU often asks students to *sketch* these. Know the axes: constant-head plots use **unit quantities** (Nu, Qu, Pu) on x-axis; constant-speed plots use **H** on x-axis with **Q** and **P** as y-axes (multiple curves at different N).
> 6. **Not adding the rope radius correction** to the brake arm length: $R_{eff} = R + d/2$.
> 7. **Wrong "reaction" definition** – A reaction turbine still has a *nozzle-equivalent* function (done by guide vanes); the *reaction* refers to the pressure drop *across the runner itself*, not the entire turbine.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Reaction turbine** → pressure drop *across the runner*; **Impulse turbine** → no pressure drop across runner.
- ✅ **Francis**: Mixed flow, medium head (30–600 m), $N_s$ = 60–400.
- ✅ **Kaplan**: Axial flow with adjustable blades, low head (< 30 m), $N_s$ = 300–1000.
- ✅ **Draft tube** is *not optional* in a reaction turbine — it recovers exit KE and allows installation above tailwater.
- ✅ **Three efficiency types** — Hydraulic, Mechanical, Volumetric — multiply to get Overall.
- ✅ **V-notch discharge formula:** $Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \, H_n^{5/2}$. For 90°: $Q = 2.362 C_d H_n^{5/2}$.
- ✅ **Shaft power formula:** $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ — memorize the constant; do not derive in exam.
- ✅ **Specific speed formula:** $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ — single most-used selection criterion.
- ✅ **Unit quantities** (Nu, Qu, Pu) enable plotting of *combined characteristic curves* for turbines of different sizes but similar geometry.
- ✅ **Brake drum effective radius** = arm length + (rope diameter / 2) — most students forget this.
- ✅ **Hydraulic efficiency formula for reaction turbine:** $\eta_h = \dfrac{V_{w1} U_1 \pm V_{w2} U_2}{gH}$ — *sign convention*: subtract if whirl reverses (i.e., if exit whirl is opposite to rotation direction).
- ✅ **Lab reporting order**: (1) Tabulate raw data → (2) Compute $Q$ from $H_n$ → (3) Compute $P_{in}, P_{shaft}$ → (4) Compute $\eta$ → (5) Plot $\eta$ vs $N$ (or $V$, or $Q$) → (6) Identify best efficiency point → (7) Compute $N_s$ and report turbine type.
- ✅ **Governor function**: For Francis → adjusts guide vane opening only. For Kaplan → adjusts *both* guide vane opening *and* runner blade pitch (dual regulation) — this is the Kaplan's key advantage in efficiency at part-load.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed Variation Test on Impulse Turbine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiments
> - **Topic:** Speed Variation Test on Impulse Turbine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

The **Speed Variation Test on an Impulse Turbine** is a standard laboratory performance-evaluation experiment conducted on a **Pelton Wheel** (or a similar jet-type impulse turbine) to determine how the turbine's operating parameters — rotational speed, discharge, power output, and overall efficiency — vary as the rotational speed is deliberately changed under approximately **constant effective head** and **constant spear-valve (nozzle) opening**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory framework, the test is used to establish the **complete characteristic curves** of the impulse turbine, including:
- Speed (N) vs. Brake Power (BP)
- Speed (N) vs. Shaft Power (SP) / Water Power (WP)
- Speed (N) vs. Overall Efficiency (η₀)
- Speed (N) vs. Discharge (Q)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Speed Variation Test" specifically refers to varying the **brake-load on the dynamometer**, which in turn changes the **rotational speed** of the turbine. Head and nozzle opening are kept as close to constant as practically possible. This is the standard method prescribed for impulse turbines in the **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (Mechanical) Lab Manual**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Engineering Intuition

Imagine a child sitting on a freely-rotating office chair, holding a water hose that squirts water tangentially against a set of small paddles attached to the chair. Now think about what happens if you gradually add small weights to the chair (simulating a brake load):

- **Zero load** → The chair spins very fast, but the water just splashes without doing meaningful work.
- **Heavy load** → The chair barely turns, and the water also does very little useful work because it cannot keep up.
- **Just-right load** → The chair spins at a moderate speed, and almost all of the water's energy is converted into turning effort. **This is the "sweet spot" — the maximum efficiency point.**

The Pelton wheel in a hydroelectric plant behaves identically. The **brake load on the rope-brake drum** is the "weight" we add. As we change this load:
- The shaft speed **N** changes
- The power extracted **BP** changes
- The efficiency **η₀** changes — reaching a **peak** at one specific speed

The job of the speed-variation test is to find that peak, plot the curves, and quantify the turbine's behaviour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why constant head?** Because if the upstream tank level keeps dropping, the inlet head H changes continuously, contaminating the readings. The lab uses a **stilling tank / overhead tank with a constant-level overflow** to keep H effectively steady throughout the run.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Units

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|----------|--------|--------------|
| Density of water | **ρ** | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Acceleration due to gravity | **g** | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Specific weight of water | **γ = ρg** | **9810 N/m³** |
| Head (gross / net) | **H** | **m** |
| Discharge | **Q** | **m³/s** (or **L/s**) |
| Rotational speed | **N** | **rpm** |
| Brake-drum effective radius | **R** | **m** |
| Net load on brake | **(W − S)** | **kgf or N** |
| Brake Power | **BP** | **W** or **kW** |
| Shaft / Water Power | **SP / WP** | **W** or **kW** |
| Overall efficiency | **η₀** | **dimensionless (%)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background of an Impulse Turbine (Pelton Wheel)

A Pelton wheel is a **tangential-flow impulse turbine**. The complete pressure energy of water is first converted into **kinetic energy** in a nozzle, producing a high-velocity jet. This jet then strikes the double-cupped buckets mounted on the periphery of a runner. The kinetic energy of the jet is converted into **mechanical (rotational) energy** as the jet is deflected through nearly **180°** inside the bucket.

### 2.1.1 Velocity Triangle at Inlet & Outlet of the Bucket

- **Jet velocity:** $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$
- **Bucket velocity (tangential):** $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$
- **Relative velocity at inlet:** $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$
- **Whirl component at inlet:** $V_{w1} = V_1$ (jet is purely tangential, α = 0°)
- **Relative velocity at outlet (with friction coefficient K):** $V_{r2} = K \cdot V_{r1}$
- **Whirl component at outlet:** $V_{w2} = u - V_{r2}\cos\beta$

The bucket-deflection angle β typically lies between **165° and 170°** for modern Pelton buckets.

### 2.1.2 Work Done, Power & Efficiency

- **Work done per unit weight of water struck (Euler's equation):**
  $W_d = \dfrac{u}{g}(V_{w1} + V_{w2})$
- **Hydraulic (Shaft / Water) efficiency:**
  $\eta_h = \dfrac{V_{w1} + V_{w2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$
- **Maximum hydraulic efficiency condition:** $u = V_1 / 2$ → this gives the **design speed**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the speed variation test useful?** The Pelton wheel is designed to run at $u = V_1/2$ for maximum efficiency. By varying the brake load, we vary u (= πDN/60), and we can experimentally verify where the actual peak efficiency occurs and compare it with the design point.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Speed Variation Test

The test follows a strict, sequential logic — this is what examiners expect in the **viva voce** and **lab report**:

1. **Prime the system** — open the supply valve and allow water to fill the overhead tank; ensure no air pockets in the nozzle.
2. **Set the spear-valve opening** to a fixed, pre-decided position (e.g., 25%, 50%, 75% of full travel) using the **graduated stem**. **Do not change it during the test.**
3. **Maintain a constant net head H** — the lab instructor fixes the gross head; minor variations are averaged.
4. **Measure the volumetric discharge Q** using a **measuring tank + stopwatch** (or a calibrated venturi/orifice meter).
5. **Run the turbine freely (no load)** — record the **run-away / no-load speed N₀**. This is the highest N attained.
6. **Apply progressive brake load** — typically in **6 to 8 steps**, by tightening the rope-brake gradually.
7. **At each step, record simultaneously:**
   - Spring-balance reading **S** (kgf)
   - Hanger-weight reading **W** (kgf)
   - Tachometer reading **N** (rpm)
   - Time **t** (s) to collect a known volume **V** (m³) of water
   - Head H (m)
8. **Increase the load in equal steps** until the turbine is near the **stalling condition** (the point where the brake load is so high that the runner almost stops).
9. **Reduce the load back to zero** and confirm repeatability.
10. **Tabulate and compute** BP, SP, η₀ for every reading and plot the characteristic curves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Students frequently confuse "Speed Variation Test" with "Constant-Speed Test" (which is used for **reaction turbines** like Francis/Kaplan, where N is kept constant by adjusting the guide-vane opening). For an **impulse turbine**, the brake load is varied, and N is allowed to change. **This distinction carries full marks.**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols:** All loads in **kgf** unless converted to **N** by multiplying with **g = 9.81 m/s²**. Power comes out in **W (watts)** if SI units are used throughout.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Discharge | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Volume (m}^3\text{)}}{\text{Time (s)}}$ | m³/s | Measured with collecting tank |
| 2 | Volume flow rate (alt.) | $Q = \dfrac{A \cdot L}{t}$ | m³/s | A = collecting tank area, L = rise in level |
| 3 | Jet velocity | $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s | $C_v$ = coefficient of velocity ≈ **0.97–0.99** |
| 4 | Bucket velocity | $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | D = mean runner diameter |
| 5 | Speed ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{u}{V_1}$ | – | Optimum ≈ **0.45–0.50** |
| 6 | Net load (with counter-weight) | $W_n = (W - S)$ | kgf or N | W = hanger reading, S = spring-balance reading |
| 7 | Net load (without counter-weight) | $W_n = W$ | kgf or N | Rope end dead-loaded on a hook |
| 8 | Brake-drum torque | $T = W_n \cdot g \cdot R$ | N·m | R = effective brake-drum radius |
| 9 | Brake Power (Output) | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi N W_n g R}{60}$ | W | The mechanical shaft-output power |
| 10 | Water (Shaft) Power (Input) | $SP = \rho g Q H = \gamma Q H$ | W | Available hydraulic power in the jet |
| 11 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o = \dfrac{BP}{SP} \times 100$ | % | The single most important result |
| 12 | Hydraulic efficiency | $\eta_h = \dfrac{V_{w1} + V_{w2}}{V_1}$ | % | From velocity-triangle analysis |
| 13 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{\eta_o}{\eta_h}$ | % | Accounts for bearing/friction losses |
| 14 | Unit speed | $N_u = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | rpm·√kW/m^(5/4) | P in kW, H in m |
| 15 | Unit discharge | $Q_u = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}$ | m³/s per √m | For homologous scaling |
| 16 | Unit power | $P_u = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}}$ | kW/m^(3/2) | For homologous scaling |
| 17 | Specific speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | – | Low value ≈ impulse, high ≈ reaction |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In Equation 6 above, the **direction of the spring balance** matters. If the rope is pulled **down** by the spring balance, then $W_n = W + S$. If the rope goes **over** a pulley such that the spring balance pulls **up** (opposing the weight W hanging from the rope), then $W_n = W - S$. **Read your lab-manual diagram carefully — this is a 3-mark question by itself.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The data obtained from this test are used by hydraulic-machine designers to:

1. **Predict the operating point** at a given site head — by reading the efficiency curve at the design speed ratio φ.
2. **Determine the runaway speed** $N_0$ — critical for mechanical design of the runner, since the wheel can spin dangerously fast when the load is suddenly shed.
3. **Scale up to prototype turbines** — using the **unit-quantities** ($N_u$, $Q_u$, $P_u$) and the principle of **homologous (geometrically similar) machines**.
4. **Compare performance of competing bucket designs** — different β, different K, different bucket profiles.
5. **Establish the best-efficiency point (BEP)** for selecting the synchronous speed of the coupled generator.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Work-Done Equation (Euler's Turbine Equation)

Starting from **Newton's second law for a control volume** rotating at angular velocity ω, the torque on the runner is the **rate of change of angular momentum** of the fluid:

$$
T = \rho Q (V_{w1} r_1 - V_{w2} r_2)
$$

**Step 1:** Because the flow at inlet and outlet of the bucket occurs at the **same mean radius** (Pelton buckets are at the periphery, $r_1 \approx r_2 = D/2$):

$$
T = \rho Q \frac{D}{2} (V_{w1} - V_{w2})
$$

**Step 2:** The work done per second (i.e., power) is torque × angular velocity, $\text{Power} = T \omega$, with $\omega = 2\pi N/60$:

$$
P = \rho Q \frac{D}{2} (V_{w1} - V_{w2}) \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}
$$

**Step 3:** Use the relation $u = \pi D N / 60$, which means $\pi D N / 60 = u$, so $\pi D N = 60 u$:

$$
P = \rho Q \frac{D}{2} (V_{w1} - V_{w2}) \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \rho Q u (V_{w1} - V_{w2})
$$

**Step 4:** Divide both sides by $\rho g Q$ to get the work done **per unit weight** of water (this is the form used in the velocity-triangle analysis):

$$
W_d = \frac{u}{g}(V_{w1} - V_{w2})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a Pelton wheel, the whirl velocity at outlet $V_{w2}$ is **negative** (the jet leaves the bucket in the opposite tangential direction to its motion), so the term becomes $V_{w1} + \vert V_{w2} \vert$ — this is why **deflection through ~180°** produces a large positive work output.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Maximum Hydraulic Efficiency Condition

The hydraulic efficiency of an impulse turbine is the ratio of work done per unit weight to the kinetic energy per unit weight of the jet:

$$
\eta_h = \frac{u(V_{w1} + \vert V_{w2} \vert)}{g \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{2g}} = \frac{2u(V_{w1} + \vert V_{w2} \vert)}{V_1^2}
$$

**Step 1:** Assume a **frictionless** bucket first ($K = 1$, so $V_{r2} = V_{r1}$ and the bucket simply reverses the jet). With the jet reversed through 180°:

$$
V_{w2} = -V_{r1}\cos\beta - u
$$

**Step 2:** With $\beta = 180°$, $\cos\beta = -1$, and $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$:

$$
V_{w2} = -(V_1 - u)(-1) - u = (V_1 - u) - u = V_1 - 2u
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $V_{w1} = V_1$ and $V_{w2} = -(V_1 - 2u)$ (note: the sign is negative because the whirl has reversed):

$$
\eta_h = \frac{2u[V_1 + (V_1 - 2u)]}{V_1^2} = \frac{2u(2V_1 - 2u)}{V_1^2} = \frac{4u(V_1 - u)}{V_1^2}
$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate $\eta_h$ with respect to $u$ and set to zero to find the maximum:

$$
\frac{d\eta_h}{du} = \frac{4(V_1 - 2u)}{V_1^2} = 0
$$

**Step 5:** Solve:

$$
V_1 - 2u = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{u = \frac{V_1}{2}}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back:

$$
\eta_{h,\max} = \frac{4 \cdot \frac{V_1}{2} \cdot \left(V_1 - \frac{V_1}{2}\right)}{V_1^2} = \frac{4 \cdot \frac{V_1}{2} \cdot \frac{V_1}{2}}{V_1^2} = 1.0 \;\; (100\%)
$$

In the **ideal, frictionless case**, the maximum hydraulic efficiency is **100%** — all the kinetic energy of the jet is converted to mechanical work. In practice, friction in the bucket ($K < 1$) and the **exit kinetic-energy loss** ($\frac{1}{2}V_{r2}^2$ leaving with the water) reduce this to about **85–90%**.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

> **Given (KTU-style problem):** A Pelton wheel test rig records the following data at a constant net head of **H = 30 m** and a constant nozzle opening:
> - Jet diameter **d = 50 mm**, coefficient of velocity **Cᵥ = 0.98**
> - Brake-drum effective diameter **D_b = 40 cm** (radius **R = 0.20 m**)
> - Rope-brake: spring balance **S = 4 kgf**, hanger weight **W = 18 kgf** (opposing directions)
> - Tachometer reading **N = 600 rpm**
> - Time to collect **V = 0.30 m³** in the measuring tank = **30 s**
> - Take **g = 9.81 m/s²**, **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**.

**Step 1 — Discharge:**

$$
Q = \frac{0.30 \text{ m}^3}{30 \text{ s}} = 0.010 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 10 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Jet velocity:**

$$
V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH} = 0.98 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 30}
$$

$$
\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 30} = \sqrt{588.6} = 24.262 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
V_1 = 0.98 \times 24.262 = 23.777 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Net brake load (rope pulled in opposite direction):**

$$
W_n = W - S = 18 - 4 = 14 \text{ kgf}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Newtons:**

$$
W_n = 14 \times 9.81 = 137.34 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Brake Power (Output):**

$$
BP = \frac{2 \pi N W_n g R}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 600 \times 137.34 \times 0.20}{60}
$$

$$
BP = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 600 \times 137.34 \times 0.20}{60} = \frac{103{,}545.7}{60}
$$

$$
BP \approx 1725.76 \text{ W} \approx 1.726 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Shaft / Water Power (Input):**

$$
SP = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.010 \times 30 = 2943 \text{ W} = 2.943 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Overall efficiency:**

$$
\eta_o = \frac{BP}{SP} \times 100 = \frac{1725.76}{2943} \times 100 = 58.64\%
$$

**Step 8 — Bucket velocity (for completeness):**

$$
u = \frac{\pi D N}{60} \;\;\text{(requires D — assume D = 0.50 m for this example)}
$$

$$
u = \frac{\pi \times 0.50 \times 600}{60} = 5\pi = 15.708 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 9 — Speed ratio:**

$$
\phi = \frac{u}{V_1} = \frac{15.708}{23.777} \approx 0.66
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The speed ratio φ = 0.66 is **above** the theoretical optimum of 0.5, indicating the wheel is running slightly **faster** than the best-efficiency design speed at this load. As we increase the brake load in subsequent steps, N will drop, φ will approach 0.5, and η₀ will rise — until the friction and windage losses start dominating and η₀ falls again. **This is the parabolic efficiency curve** that the experiment is designed to capture.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Increasing Brake Load

| Brake Load W_n | Speed N | Bucket velocity u | Speed ratio φ = u/V₁ | Discharge Q | Power BP | Efficiency η₀ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Very small (near zero) | Maximum (≈ runaway) | High | φ > 0.5 | ~ constant | Very small | Very low |
| Increasing | Decreasing | Decreasing | φ approaches 0.5 | ~ constant | Increases | Rises to peak |
| **Optimum (design)** | **Design speed** | **u = V₁/2** | **φ ≈ 0.5** | ~ constant | **Maximum BP** | **η₀,peak** |
| Increasing further | Drops further | Low | φ < 0.5 | ~ constant | Drops | Falls |
| Near stall | Very low | Very low | φ ≪ 0.5 | ~ constant | ≈ 0 | Very low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-away:** Q stays almost constant because the **spear-valve opening is fixed** and H is fixed. The variable that really changes is **N** (and hence u), and BP, η₀ are functions of N. **This is the entire logic of "speed variation".**
