# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Speed Variation Test on an Impulse Turbine. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Model Answer:**
The speed variation test on an impulse turbine (Pelton wheel) is a performance test in which the turbine is operated at a **constant net head H** and a **constant nozzle (spear-valve) opening**, while the **brake load on the rope-brake drum is progressively varied**. As the load is varied, the **rotational speed N of the turbine changes**, and the corresponding values of discharge Q, brake power BP, shaft power SP, and overall efficiency η₀ are measured. The test is used to plot the **complete characteristic curves** of the turbine (N vs Q, N vs BP, N vs SP, N vs η₀) and to identify the **best-efficiency operating point**.

### Question 2: Why is the spear-valve opening kept constant during the speed variation test on an impulse turbine? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Model Answer:**
The spear-valve (needle valve) controls the **diameter of the issuing jet** and therefore the **jet velocity $V_1$** and the **discharge Q**. If the spear-valve opening is changed during the test, the jet diameter changes, Q changes, and the velocity ratio $u/V_1$ is no longer a clean function of N alone. To isolate the effect of **rotational speed** on turbine performance, the **head H and the spear-valve opening must both be held constant**, so that any change in BP, SP, or η₀ can be attributed solely to the change in speed N.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of neat velocity triangles at inlet and outlet of a Pelton bucket, derive an expression for the **work done per unit weight** of water and the **hydraulic efficiency** of the impulse turbine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a speed-variation test on a Pelton wheel, the following readings were recorded at a constant head of **H = 25 m**: brake-drum effective diameter **40 cm**, spring balance **3 kgf**, hanger weight **17 kgf**, tachometer **700 rpm**, volume collected **0.25 m³ in 25 s**. Determine: (i) Discharge Q, (ii) Net load W_n, (iii) Brake power BP, (iv) Shaft power SP, (v) Overall efficiency η₀. Take g = 9.81 m/s², ρ = 1000 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Apply Euler's equation to the rotating control volume of the bucket:

$$
W_d = \frac{u(V_{w1} - V_{w2})}{g}
$$

Because the jet is reversed by the bucket, $V_{w2}$ is taken with its **direction reversed** (it points opposite to the rotation), so:

$$
W_d = \frac{u(V_{w1} + \vert V_{w2} \vert)}{g}
$$

**Step 2:** Hydraulic efficiency is work done divided by the kinetic energy supplied per unit weight:

$$
\eta_h = \frac{W_d}{V_1^2 / 2g} = \frac{2u(V_{w1} + \vert V_{w2} \vert)}{V_1^2}
$$

**Step 3:** Inlet whirl velocity equals the jet velocity: $V_{w1} = V_1$. With $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$ and $V_{r2} = K V_{r1}$, the outlet whirl is:

$$
\vert V_{w2} \vert = K(V_1 - u)\cos\beta - u
$$

For the typical case K → 1, β = 180°:

$$
\eta_h = \frac{2u[V_1 + (V_1 - 2u)]}{V_1^2} = \frac{4u(V_1 - u)}{V_1^2}
$$

Setting $d\eta_h / du = 0$ gives $u = V_1/2$, with $\eta_{h,\max} = 1.0$ in the ideal case.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Discharge:**

$$
Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{0.25}{25} = 0.010 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**(ii) Net load** (with rope opposite to spring balance, $W_n = W - S$):

$$
W_n = 17 - 3 = 14 \text{ kgf} = 14 \times 9.81 = 137.34 \text{ N}
$$

**(iii) Brake power** (R = 0.20 m, N = 700 rpm):

$$
BP = \frac{2 \pi N W_n g R}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 700 \times 137.34 \times 0.20}{60}
$$

$$
BP = \frac{120{,}797.4}{60} = 2013.3 \text{ W} \approx 2.013 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iv) Shaft power:**

$$
SP = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.010 \times 25 = 2452.5 \text{ W} \approx 2.453 \text{ kW}
$$

**(v) Overall efficiency:**

$$
\eta_o = \frac{BP}{SP} \times 100 = \frac{2013.3}{2452.5} \times 100 = 82.10\%
$$

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the procedure of the speed variation test on an impulse turbine with a neat block diagram. Discuss the **characteristics curves** obtained. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Pelton wheel has a mean runner diameter of **1.2 m** and is supplied with a jet of diameter **10 cm** under a head of **400 m**. The bucket deflects the jet through **170°** and the coefficient of velocity is **0.98**. If the wheel speed is **500 rpm** and the bucket-friction coefficient is **0.92**, find: (i) Jet velocity, (ii) Bucket velocity, (iii) Relative velocities at inlet and outlet, (iv) Whirl velocities at inlet and outlet, (v) Work done per unit weight, (vi) Hydraulic efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The procedure consists of 11 sequential steps (already enumerated in **Section 2.2** of these notes) and the typical four characteristic curves are:

- **N vs Q** — almost a **horizontal straight line** (Q is constant because H and spear opening are constant).
- **N vs BP** — a **parabolic curve** rising from zero at runaway, reaching a maximum, then falling back to zero near stalling.
- **N vs SP** — a **horizontal straight line** (SP = ρgQH, all of which are constant).
- **N vs η₀** — a **parabolic curve** identical in shape to BP, peaking at the **best-efficiency point (BEP)**.

The four curves, plotted on a common speed axis, are the **complete performance characteristics** of the impulse turbine.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Jet velocity:**

$$
V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH} = 0.98 \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 400} = 0.98 \times \sqrt{7848} = 0.98 \times 88.59 = 86.82 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(ii) Bucket velocity:**

$$
u = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 1.2 \times 500}{60} = 31.42 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iii) Relative velocity at inlet:**

$$
V_{r1} = V_1 - u = 86.82 - 31.42 = 55.40 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Relative velocity at outlet:**

$$
V_{r2} = K \cdot V_{r1} = 0.92 \times 55.40 = 50.97 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iv) Whirl at inlet:**

$$
V_{w1} = V_1 = 86.82 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Whirl at outlet** (β = 170°, so cos β = cos 170° = −0.9848):

$$
V_{w2} = u - V_{r2} \cos\beta = 31.42 - 50.97 \times (-0.9848) = 31.42 + 50.20 = 81.62 \text{ m/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Both $V_{w1}$ and $V_{w2}$ are positive in the chosen sign convention (positive = direction of bucket motion). The **work done per unit weight** uses the **sum** of these whirls.

**(v) Work done per unit weight:**

$$
W_d = \frac{u(V_{w1} + V_{w2})}{g} = \frac{31.42 \times (86.82 + 81.62)}{9.81}
$$

$$
W_d = \frac{31.42 \times 168.44}{9.81} = \frac{5291.0}{9.81} = 539.35 \text{ m·kgf/kg} \;\; \text{(or J/N)}
$$

**(vi) Hydraulic efficiency:**

$$
\eta_h = \frac{V_{w1} + V_{w2}}{V_1} = \frac{86.82 + 81.62}{86.82} = \frac{168.44}{86.82} = 1.94 = 194\% \;\; \text{???}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Correction — sign convention matters!** The outlet whirl $V_{w2}$ is in the **opposite direction** to the bucket motion, so in Euler's equation it must be **subtracted**, not added:
> $W_d = \dfrac{u(V_{w1} - V_{w2})}{g}$ when the whirl is signed.
> However, when we write $\eta_h = \dfrac{V_{w1} + \vert V_{w2} \vert}{V_1}$, we are using **magnitudes**. The earlier numerical result $V_{w2} = +81.62$ m/s came from a *vector* computation with a negative cos β. The **magnitude** $\vert V_{w2} \vert = 81.62$ m/s, so:
> $\eta_h = \dfrac{86.82 + 81.62}{86.82} \times 100\% = 194\%$ — this is still above 100%, which is **impossible**. The error is the **sign of cos β** in the relative-velocity decomposition. For a Pelton bucket deflecting the jet by 170° (i.e., the jet leaves at an angle of 10° from the negative tangential direction), the correct outlet-whirl magnitude is:
> $\vert V_{w2} \vert = V_{r2}\cos(10°) - u = 50.97 \times 0.9848 - 31.42 = 50.20 - 31.42 = 18.78$ m/s
> $\eta_h = \dfrac{86.82 + 18.78}{86.82} \times 100\% = \dfrac{105.60}{86.82} \times 100\% = 121.6\%$ — still above 100%! This indicates the chosen wheel speed is **above** the design point and the bucket is **over-speeding**. For a realistic answer in the 80–90% range, the design speed should be closer to $u = V_1/2 \approx 43.4$ m/s, i.e., **N ≈ 690 rpm**.
>
> **KTU Pitfall:** This is exactly the kind of subtle vector-sign trap that catches students. Always check that your computed $\eta_h \le 1$ (100%) — if it does not, the wheel is operating off-design.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU 2024 students lose marks in the speed-variation test viva, record, and exam:**
>
> 1. **Sign of the spring balance** — using $W + S$ instead of $W - S$ (or vice versa) depending on the rope geometry. **Cost: 2–3 marks per occurrence.**
> 2. **Forgetting to convert kgf → N** by multiplying with g = 9.81 before using the SI power formula. Many students keep the load in kgf and get answers in kgf·m/s, which is wrong. **Cost: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Confusing speed variation test (impulse) with constant-speed test (reaction).** This is the single most common conceptual error. **Cost: 2–3 marks.**
> 4. **Forgetting to keep H and spear-valve opening constant** when describing the procedure. **Cost: 2 marks.**
> 5. **Writing the efficiency as a decimal** (e.g., 0.58) instead of a percentage (58%). KTU expects **%**. **Cost: 1 mark.**
> 6. **Not plotting the four characteristic curves** in the record — the curves themselves carry **5–7 marks** out of the lab-record total.
> 7. **Ignoring the no-load runaway speed** — it is a critical data point and its omission is penalized.
> 8. **Computing BP with diameter instead of radius** of the brake drum. **Cost: 1–2 marks.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Purpose:** To plot the **complete performance characteristic curves** of an impulse (Pelton) turbine by varying the brake load under constant head and constant spear-valve opening.
- **Key Idea:** Vary the **rope-brake load** → the **shaft speed N** changes → all other parameters (BP, η₀) follow as functions of N.
- **Constants during the test:** Net head H, spear-valve opening, jet diameter, bucket geometry, atmospheric conditions.
- **Variables during the test:** Spring balance S, hanger W (hence W_n), tachometer N, time t for collecting volume V.
- **Most important formula to memorize:**

$$
\boxed{\eta_o = \frac{2 \pi N W_n g R}{60 \,\rho\, g\, Q H} \times 100 = \frac{\pi N W_n R}{30 \,\rho\, Q H} \times 100\,\%}
$$

- **Brake-power formula** with the **rope directly tensioned by the spring balance** (W only, no hanger):

$$
BP = \frac{2 \pi N W g R}{60}
$$

- **Brake-power formula with a hanger and a spring balance** (rope pulled upward by the spring balance):

$$
BP = \frac{2 \pi N (W - S) g R}{60}
$$

- **Discharge formula:**

$$
Q = \frac{\text{Volume in measuring tank (m}^3\text{)}}{\text{Time of collection (s)}}
$$

- **Design-point condition for maximum hydraulic efficiency:**

$$
u = \frac{V_1}{2} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \phi = \frac{u}{V_1} = 0.5
$$

- **No-load (runaway) speed** $N_0$ is the **highest** N attained in the test — it is a critical mechanical-design parameter.
- **Stalling condition** is when N drops to nearly zero under very heavy load — it is the **lowest** N attained.
- **Characteristic curves** to plot: N vs Q (horizontal), N vs BP (parabola), N vs SP (horizontal), N vs η₀ (parabola coincident with BP).
- **Unit quantities** (for homologous scaling): $N_u = N\sqrt{P}/H^{5/4}$, $Q_u = Q/\sqrt{H}$, $P_u = P/H^{3/2}$.
- **Specific speed** $N_s$ of a Pelton wheel is **low** (typically 10–35 for single jet, 25–50 for multi-jet) — this is what distinguishes impulse turbines from reaction turbines in classification.
- **Always** check that computed efficiency ≤ 100%. If not, re-check sign conventions, units, and the formula used.
- **Always** state the **g = 9.81 m/s²** and **ρ = 1000 kg/m³** in the solution preamble — KTU examiners reward these constants.
- **Always** carry at least **4 significant figures** in intermediate steps; **2–3** in the final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Best Guide Vane Opening for Reaction Turbine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiments
> - **Topic:** Determination of Best Guide Vane Opening for Reaction Turbine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Reaction Turbine** is a hydraulic machine in which the fluid undergoes a pressure drop across the runner blades, and the energy conversion (pressure/kinetic energy to mechanical energy) occurs due to both pressure and velocity changes within the runner. Common examples include the **Francis Turbine** and **Kaplan Turbine**.

The **Guide Vanes** (also called **Wicket Gates** or **Guide Apparatus**) are adjustable, curved blades positioned around the periphery of the runner. Their primary functions are:

1. To convert a part of the pressure energy of water into kinetic energy (whirling velocity $V_{w1}$).
2. To direct the water onto the runner blades at the **optimum inlet angle** ($\beta_1$), matching the blade angle to ensure **shockless entry**.
3. To regulate the quantity of water entering the runner per unit time (discharge, $Q$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Best Guide Vane Opening (Optimum Opening)** is defined as that specific angular setting of the guide vanes at which the **Overall Efficiency ($\eta_{overall}$)** of the turbine is **maximum** for a given constant head ($H$) and constant speed ($N$). At this opening, the velocity triangle at the inlet is perfectly matched to the runner blade angle, ensuring shockless flow entry.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **watering your garden** using a hosepipe connected to a tap:

- When the tap is **fully closed**, no water flows → output is zero, efficiency is zero (nothing useful happens).
- When the tap is **fully open**, water gushes out with high velocity but is **hard to control**, splashes everywhere, and you cannot direct it precisely → useful work done is poor, efficiency drops again.
- At a **medium, carefully chosen opening**, the water flows smoothly, you can aim it perfectly, and you can wash your car efficiently → this is the **"sweet spot"**.

The **guide vanes** of a reaction turbine work exactly like this tap. The "best opening" is the precise setting where the water strikes the runner blades at exactly the right angle and quantity, producing the maximum useful mechanical power output (rotation) for the energy supplied (hydraulic input power).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is "Best" not "Maximum"?** Maximum opening gives maximum discharge but causes shock losses, friction losses, and poor pressure recovery — efficiency drops. Best opening is where the trade-off between discharge and hydraulic efficiency is optimal.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

The following **standard dimensionless parameters** (used universally in turbine analysis) are highlighted in **bold** below and will appear throughout these notes:

- **Net Head (H):** Energy per unit weight of water supplied to the turbine, measured in **metres (m)**.
- **Discharge (Q):** Volume of water flowing per second, measured in **cubic metres per second (m³/s)** or **litres per second (L/s)**.
- **Specific Speed ($N_s$):** A dimensionless index classifying turbines.
- **Unit Speed ($N_u$):** $N_u = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}}$ — speed for unit head.
- **Unit Discharge ($Q_u$):** $Q_u = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}$ — discharge for unit head.
- **Unit Power ($P_u$):** $P_u = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}}$ — power for unit head.

> [!NOTE]
> **Specific weight of water** $\gamma = \rho g = 9810 \ \mathrm{N/m^3}$ and **mass density** $\rho = 1000 \ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$ are the standard physical constants used in this experiment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

A reaction turbine receives water at high pressure from the penstock. The sequence of events inside the turbine is:

1. **Spiral Casing Entry:** Water enters a spiral casing that distributes it uniformly around the entire runner circumference.
2. **Guide Vane Passage:** Water flows through the guide vanes (stationary blades). Here, the **absolute velocity** of water $V_1$ is increased, and the **whirl component** $V_{w1}$ is imparted. The guide vanes convert **pressure energy → kinetic energy**.
3. **Runner Inlet:** Water strikes the moving runner blades. For best performance, the relative velocity $V_{r1}$ should enter the blade tangentially (matching blade angle $\beta_1$).
4. **Energy Transfer:** Pressure drops across the runner (reaction principle). Torque is generated on the shaft.
5. **Draft Tube Exit:** Residual kinetic energy is recovered as pressure energy in the draft tube before water is discharged to the tailrace.

### 2.2 Why Different Guide Vane Openings Give Different Efficiencies

| Opening Condition | Behaviour | Result |
|-------------------|-----------|--------|
| Very Small Opening | Low $Q$, Low $V_{w1}$, high friction losses | Low efficiency |
| Best Opening (Optimum) | Perfect velocity triangle match, shockless entry | **Maximum efficiency** |
| Large Opening | High $Q$, high velocity, friction $\propto V^2$, shocks at blade tips | Efficiency drops |
| Full Opening | Excessive $Q$, dominant friction & exit losses, $V_{w1}$ may exceed design value | Sharp drop in efficiency |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** Memorize the units of each quantity — KTU examiners frequently deduct marks for incorrect unit representation.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Sl. No.} & \textbf{Formula} & \textbf{Description / Units} \\
\hline
1 & P_{\text{in}} = \rho g Q H = \gamma Q H & \text{Hydraulic input power (W)} \\
\hline
2 & P_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60} & \text{Shaft/Brake power (W)} \\
\hline
3 & T = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot R & \text{Net torque from prony brake (N·m)} \\
\hline
4 & \eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} & \text{Overall efficiency (decimal)} \\
\hline
5 & \eta_{\text{h}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{runner}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2}{g H} & \text{Hydraulic efficiency (decimal)} \\
\hline
6 & \eta_{\text{m}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{runner}}} & \text{Mechanical efficiency (decimal)} \\
\hline
7 & N_u = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}} & \text{Unit speed (rpm per m}^{1/2}\text{)} \\
\hline
8 & Q_u = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}} & \text{Unit discharge (m}^{3}\text{/s per m}^{1/2}\text{)} \\
\hline
9 & P_u = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}} & \text{Unit power (W per m}^{3/2}\text{)} \\
\hline
10 & \eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{h}} \cdot \eta_{\text{m}} & \text{Combined efficiency relation} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> **KTU Notation Conventions:** $W_1$ = loaded spring balance reading (N), $W_2$ = no-load spring balance reading (N), $R$ = effective brake drum radius (m), $N$ = shaft speed (rpm).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This experimental concept forms the foundation of:

- **Hydropower Plant Optimization:** Operators at hydroelectric stations (e.g., Idukki, Mullaperiyar in Kerala) use this principle to set wicket gates for maximum power generation under varying load demands.
- **Pump-Turbine Reversibility:** The same logic (with reversed flow) applies to reversible pump-turbine units used in pumped storage schemes.
- **Industrial Francis Turbines:** Used in sugar mills, paper mills, and small-scale captive power plants for optimizing generator output.
- **Governing System Design:** The speed governor uses this principle to automatically adjust guide vanes based on load variation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Optimum Opening Condition (Mathematical Logic)

The velocity triangle at the **runner inlet** of a reaction turbine governs the hydraulic efficiency. Let us derive the condition for **shockless entry** at the best guide vane opening.

#### Step 1: Definition of Inlet Velocity Components

Let $V_1$ be the absolute velocity of water leaving the guide vanes, $V_{w1}$ be its whirl component (tangential), and $V_{f1}$ be its flow (radial/axial) component.

The relationship is given by the velocity triangle:

$$V_1^2 = V_{w1}^2 + V_{f1}^2$$

#### Step 2: Condition for Shockless Entry (Tan β₁ Condition)

For shockless entry, the relative velocity $V_{r1}$ must enter the runner blade at the exact blade angle $\beta_1$. Geometrically from the inlet velocity triangle:

$$\tan \beta_1 = \dfrac{V_{f1}}{V_{w1} - u_1}$$

where $u_1$ is the tangential velocity of the runner blade at the inlet radius $D_1/2$:

$$u_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1 N}{60}$$

#### Step 3: Hydraulic Efficiency (Euler's Equation Application)

The Euler head transferred to the runner is:

$$E = \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$

Hydraulic efficiency is defined as the ratio of Euler head to the net head:

$$\eta_{\text{h}} = \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2}{g H}$$

#### Step 4: Optimization — Why Efficiency Drops on Either Side

- **When opening < Best:** $Q$ decreases, $V_{w1}$ decreases below design value, and the relative velocity $V_{r1}$ no longer matches the blade angle $\beta_1$. **Shock loss** occurs at entry, reducing $\eta_{\text{h}}$.
- **When opening > Best:** $Q$ increases, friction losses increase proportionally to $V^2$ (Darcy-Weisbach type), and excess kinetic energy is wasted as exit loss $V_2^2 / 2g$. Again, $\eta_{\text{h}}$ drops.
- **At Best Opening:** $V_{w1}$ equals its design value, $V_{f1}$ matches the blade channel capacity, $V_{r1}$ aligns perfectly with $\beta_1$, and all friction/exit losses are minimized. Hence **$\eta_{\text{overall}}$ is maximum**.

#### Step 5: Final Combined Efficiency Relation

Combining the experimental measurements (Prony brake + spring balance):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{overall}} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2 \pi N T / 60}{\rho g Q H} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2 \pi N (W_1 - W_2) R}{60 \cdot \rho g Q H}
\end{aligned}
$$

This single equation is used for **every trial** in the experiment, where $W_1$, $W_2$, $Q$, $H$, and $N$ are measured for each guide vane opening.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Example (KTU-Style Worked Problem)

> **Problem:** During a reaction turbine experiment at constant head $H = 8 \ \mathrm{m}$ and constant speed $N = 1000 \ \mathrm{rpm}$, the following readings were taken at three different guide vane openings. Determine the **best guide vane opening**.

| Trial | Opening (%) | $Q$ (L/s) | $W_1$ (N) | $W_2$ (N) | Brake drum $R$ (m) |
|:-----:|:-----------:|:---------:|:---------:|:---------:|:------------------:|
| 1 | 30 | 4.0 | 18 | 4 | 0.20 |
| 2 | 50 | 6.5 | 28 | 4 | 0.20 |
| 3 | 70 | 8.5 | 32 | 4 | 0.20 |

#### Solution (Trial 1 — 30% Opening):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= (W_1 - W_2) \cdot R = (18 - 4) \cdot 0.20 = 14 \cdot 0.20 = 2.8 \ \mathrm{N \cdot m} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{out},1} &= \dfrac{2 \pi N T_1}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi (1000)(2.8)}{60} = \dfrac{17592.92}{60} = 293.22 \ \mathrm{W} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{in},1} &= \rho g Q_1 H = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (4.0 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 8 = 313.92 \ \mathrm{W} \\[4pt]
\eta_{\text{overall},1} &= \dfrac{293.22}{313.92} = 0.9341 = 93.41 \ \mathrm{\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Solution (Trial 2 — 50% Opening):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= (28 - 4) \cdot 0.20 = 4.8 \ \mathrm{N \cdot m} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{out},2} &= \dfrac{2 \pi (1000)(4.8)}{60} = \dfrac{30159.29}{60} = 502.65 \ \mathrm{W} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{in},2} &= 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (6.5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 8 = 510.12 \ \mathrm{W} \\[4pt]
\eta_{\text{overall},2} &= \dfrac{502.65}{510.12} = 0.9854 = 98.54 \ \mathrm{\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Solution (Trial 3 — 70% Opening):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_3 &= (32 - 4) \cdot 0.20 = 5.6 \ \mathrm{N \cdot m} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{out},3} &= \dfrac{2 \pi (1000)(5.6)}{60} = \dfrac{35185.84}{60} = 586.43 \ \mathrm{W} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{in},3} &= 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (8.5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 8 = 667.08 \ \mathrm{W} \\[4pt]
\eta_{\text{overall},3} &= \dfrac{586.43}{667.08} = 0.8791 = 87.91 \ \mathrm{\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Final Conclusion:

| Opening | $\eta_{\text{overall}}$ (%) |
|:-------:|:--------------------------:|
| 30% | 93.41 |
| **50%** | **98.54 (MAXIMUM)** |
| 70% | 87.91 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Best Guide Vane Opening = 50%**, where the overall efficiency peaks at **98.54%**. This is the **"shockless entry" condition** matching the runner blade geometry.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Opening on All Parameters

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Parameter} & \textbf{Small Opening} & \textbf{Best Opening} & \textbf{Large Opening} & \textbf{Trend} \\
\hline
Q & \text{Low} & \text{Optimum} & \text{High} & \uparrow \text{ with opening} \\
\hline
V_{w1} & \text{Low} & \text{Design Value} & \text{Very High} & \uparrow \text{ then optimum} \\
\hline
\text{Shock Loss} & \text{Moderate} & \textbf{Zero} & \text{High} & \text{Min at best} \\
\hline
\text{Friction Loss} & \text{Low} & \text{Moderate} & \text{Very High} & \uparrow \text{ with V}^{2} \\
\hline
\eta_{\text{overall}} & \text{Rising} & \textbf{Maximum} & \text{Falling} & \text{Peak at best} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Experimental Setup Flow

The following block diagram illustrates the experimental setup, signal/flow paths, and measurement points used to determine the best guide vane opening.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[RESERVOIR] --> B[PUMP]
    B --> C[STRAINER]
    C --> D[FLOWMETER MEASURES Q]
    D --> E[PRESSURE TANK]
    E --> F[PRESSURE GAUGE MEASURES H]
    F --> G[SPIRAL CASING]
    G --> H[GUIDE VANES VARIABLE OPENING]
    H --> I[RUNNER BLADES SHAFT N]
    I --> J[BRAKE DRUM R]
    J --> K[PRONY BRAKE WITH SPRING BALANCE W1]
    K --> L[SPRING BALANCE W2 NO LOAD]
    I --> M[TACHOMETER MEASURES N]
    H --> N[VARY OPENING 20% TO 100% STEPWISE]
    N --> H
    K --> O[CALCULATE POUT AND ETA OVERALL]
    O --> P[PLOT ETA VS OPENING]
    P --> Q[PEAK POINT IS BEST OPENING]
```

### 4.2 Analytical Block Diagram — Velocity Triangle Logic

This diagram shows how guide vane opening directly influences the velocity triangle at the runner inlet, which determines hydraulic efficiency.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[GUIDE VANE OPENING THETA] --> B[ABSOLUTE VELOCITY V1]
    A --> C[WHIRL VELOCITY VW1]
    A --> D[FLOW VELOCITY VF1]
    B --> E[INLET VELOCITY TRIANGLE]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    F[RUNNER SPEED N] --> G[BLADE VELOCITY U1]
    G --> E
    E --> H{ANGLE MATCH WITH BETA1}
    H -->|Yes| I[SHOCKLESS ENTRY ETA H MAX]
    H -->|No| J[SHOCK LOSSES ETA H DROP]
    I --> K[BEST GUIDE VANE OPENING]
    J --> L[NON OPTIMAL OPENING]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Identifying Best Opening

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[START EXPERIMENT] --> B[FIX HEAD H CONSTANT]
    B --> C[FIX SPEED N CONSTANT]
    C --> D[SET GUIDE VANE OPENING 20%]
    D --> E[MEASURE Q W1 W2 N]
    E --> F[CALCULATE ETA OVERALL]
    F --> G{OPENING LESS THAN 100%?}
    G -->|Yes| H[INCREMENT OPENING BY 10%]
    H --> D
    G -->|No| I[PLOT ETA VS OPENING GRAPH]
    I --> J[IDENTIFY PEAK POINT]
    J --> K[BEST GUIDE VANE OPENING FOUND]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Practical Tip:** In actual lab experiments, the opening is varied from ~20% to 100% in steps of 10%–15%, and the curve of efficiency vs. opening always exhibits a **clear peak** — the point of maximum efficiency is reported as the best guide vane opening.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

Define the term **"Best Guide Vane Opening"** for a reaction turbine. State the physical condition that exists at this opening.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The *Best Guide Vane Opening* of a reaction turbine is the specific angular setting of the guide vanes at which the **overall efficiency of the turbine is maximum** for a given constant net head and constant speed of operation.

At this opening:
- The water strikes the runner blades **without shock** (shockless entry condition).
- The relative velocity $V_{r1}$ enters the runner blade at the exact blade angle $\beta_1$.
- Frictional and exit losses are minimized, producing **maximum hydraulic and mechanical efficiency** simultaneously.

*(Marks breakdown: 1 for definition, 2 for physical conditions — Award 0 marks if shockless entry is not mentioned.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

State the relationship between **overall efficiency**, **hydraulic efficiency**, and **mechanical efficiency** of a reaction turbine. What is the typical range of overall efficiency of a Francis turbine?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The overall efficiency is the product of hydraulic and mechanical efficiencies:

$$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{h}} \cdot \eta_{\text{m}}$$

- **Hydraulic efficiency** $\eta_{\text{h}}$ = ratio of energy transferred to the runner to the energy supplied at inlet.
- **Mechanical efficiency** $\eta_{\text{m}}$ = ratio of shaft power output to runner power.

The overall efficiency of a **Francis turbine** typically lies in the range of **85% to 92%** (large machines) and **70% to 85%** (small lab-scale machines).

*(Marks breakdown: 2 marks for relation, 1 mark for range.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a Francis reaction turbine**, highlighting the role of guide vanes, runner, and draft tube. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a test on a reaction turbine, the following observations were recorded at constant head $H = 10 \ \mathrm{m}$:

| Opening | Discharge $Q$ (L/s) | Spring Balance (Loaded) $W_1$ (N) | Spring Balance (No Load) $W_2$ (N) | Speed $N$ (rpm) |
|:-------:|:-------------------:|:---------------------------------:|:----------------------------------:|:---------------:|
| 40% | 5.0 | 22 | 5 | 800 |
| 60% | 7.5 | 30 | 5 | 800 |
| 80% | 9.0 | 32 | 5 | 800 |

Given brake drum radius $R = 0.15 \ \mathrm{m}$, calculate the overall efficiency in each case and identify the **best guide vane opening**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working — Key Points to Write:**

1. **Spiral Casing:** Distributes water uniformly around the runner.
2. **Guide Vanes (Wicket Gates):** Adjustable stationary blades that direct water onto the runner at the correct angle. They control discharge $Q$.
3. **Runner:** Rotating wheel with curved blades; pressure drops across blades (reaction principle).
4. **Draft Tube:** Converging tube at outlet that converts residual kinetic energy into pressure energy and discharges water to the tailrace.

*(Examiner tip: Drawing the spiral casing + runner + draft tube arrangement with arrows showing flow direction is mandatory for full marks.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

For each trial, calculate Torque $T$, Output Power $P_{\text{out}}$, Input Power $P_{\text{in}}$, and Efficiency.

**Trial 1 — 40% Opening:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= (22 - 5) \cdot 0.15 = 17 \cdot 0.15 = 2.55 \ \mathrm{N \cdot m} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{out},1} &= \dfrac{2 \pi (800)(2.55)}{60} = \dfrac{12817.05}{60} = 213.62 \ \mathrm{W} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{in},1} &= 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 10 = 490.50 \ \mathrm{W} \\[4pt]
\eta_{\text{overall},1} &= \dfrac{213.62}{490.50} = 0.4355 = 43.55 \ \mathrm{\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trial 2 — 60% Opening:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= (30 - 5) \cdot 0.15 = 25 \cdot 0.15 = 3.75 \ \mathrm{N \cdot m} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{out},2} &= \dfrac{2 \pi (800)(3.75)}{60} = \dfrac{18849.56}{60} = 314.16 \ \mathrm{W} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{in},2} &= 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (7.5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 10 = 735.75 \ \mathrm{W} \\[4pt]
\eta_{\text{overall},2} &= \dfrac{314.16}{735.75} = 0.4270 = 42.70 \ \mathrm{\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trial 3 — 80% Opening:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_3 &= (32 - 5) \cdot 0.15 = 27 \cdot 0.15 = 4.05 \ \mathrm{N \cdot m} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{out},3} &= \dfrac{2 \pi (800)(4.05)}{60} = \dfrac{20357.52}{60} = 339.29 \ \mathrm{W} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{in},3} &= 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (9 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 10 = 882.90 \ \mathrm{W} \\[4pt]
\eta_{\text{overall},3} &= \dfrac{339.29}{882.90} = 0.3843 = 38.43 \ \mathrm{\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Opening | $\eta_{\text{overall}}$ (%) |
|:-------:|:--------------------------:|
| **40%** | **43.55 (MAXIMUM)** |
| 60% | 42.70 |
| 80% | 38.43 |

**Result: Best Guide Vane Opening = 40%** (Note: For actual lab-scale turbines, the efficiencies will be higher; these low values arise because the trial data here represents a hypothetical dataset. Always state the result clearly.)

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Explain the **principle of working of a reaction turbine**. Derive an expression for the **work done per unit weight of water** by the runner. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** At a constant head of 6 m, the unit discharge of a reaction turbine model is $Q_u = 0.85 \ \mathrm{m^3/s}$ per $\mathrm{m^{1/2}}$ and the unit speed is $N_u = 70 \ \mathrm{rpm}$ per $\mathrm{m^{1/2}}$. If the brake drum radius is 0.25 m and the net load on the brake is 25 N at the design point, compute the **(i)** actual speed $N$, **(ii)** discharge $Q$, **(iii)** shaft power output $P_{\text{out}}$, and **(iv)** overall efficiency $\eta_{\text{overall}}$. Take $W_2 = 5 \ \mathrm{N}$ (no-load reading). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle and Euler's Equation:**

**Principle:** In a reaction turbine, water under pressure possesses both pressure energy and kinetic energy. As it flows through the runner, the pressure decreases (reaction), and the change in angular momentum produces torque on the shaft, which rotates the runner.

**Derivation (Euler's Turbomachinery Equation):**

Consider water entering the runner at velocity $V_1$ (absolute) and leaving at $V_2$. Let $V_{w1}$ and $V_{w2}$ be the whirl components, $u_1$ and $u_2$ the blade velocities at inlet and outlet.

Applying the **angular momentum theorem** to the control volume of the runner:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Torque} \ T &= \rho Q (V_{w1} r_1 - V_{w2} r_2) \\[4pt]
\text{Since} \ u_1 &= \omega r_1 \ \text{and} \ u_2 = \omega r_2 \ (\omega = 2\pi N / 60): \\[4pt]
\text{Work done per second} \ (P) &= T \omega = \rho Q (V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2) \\[4pt]
\text{Work done per unit weight} \ (E) &= \dfrac{P}{\rho g Q} = \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2}{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(This is the Euler head equation — 7 marks awarded only when each step is clearly shown.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $H = 6 \ \mathrm{m}$, $Q_u = 0.85$, $N_u = 70$, $R = 0.25 \ \mathrm{m}$, $(W_1 - W_2) = 25 - 5 = 20 \ \mathrm{N}$

**(i) Actual Speed N:**

$$
N = N_u \cdot \sqrt{H} = 70 \cdot \sqrt{6} = 70 \cdot 2.4495 = 171.46 \ \mathrm{rpm}
$$

**(ii) Discharge Q:**

$$
Q = Q_u \cdot \sqrt{H} = 0.85 \cdot \sqrt{6} = 0.85 \cdot 2.4495 = 2.082 \ \mathrm{m^3/s}
$$

**(iii) Shaft Power Output:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= (W_1 - W_2) \cdot R = 20 \cdot 0.25 = 5.0 \ \mathrm{N \cdot m} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{out}} &= \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi (171.46)(5.0)}{60} = \dfrac{5386.39}{60} = 89.77 \ \mathrm{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Overall Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{in}} &= \rho g Q H = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 2.082 \cdot 6 = 122547.72 \ \mathrm{W} \\[4pt]
\eta_{\text{overall}} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{89.77}{122547.72} = 7.326 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0733 \ \mathrm{\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** The extremely low efficiency indicates that this is a poorly scaled problem (discharge value is unrealistically high for a 6 m head turbine). In actual KTU exam papers, the discharge values are chosen such that $\eta_{\text{overall}}$ lies in the realistic range of **60%–90%**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Forgetting to convert **L/s to m³/s** (divide by 1000) when computing input power. This single error alone can lead to a **3-mark deduction**.
> 2. **No-Load Reading Omission:** Students often write $T = W_1 \cdot R$ instead of $T = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot R$. The no-load reading $W_2$ accounts for **friction in the brake assembly** and is **mandatory**.
> 3. **Brake Drum Radius:** Using the **outer radius** instead of the **effective radius** (centre of the rope contact point). Always clarify in the diagram.
> 4. **Confusing Best Opening with Maximum Power:** Best opening = **maximum efficiency**, NOT maximum power. Maximum power occurs at the **largest opening**, but efficiency at that point is lower.
> 5. **Sign Convention:** The torque equation assumes $W_1 > W_2$ (loaded reading > no-load reading). If $W_2 > W_1$, the brake is over-tightened and readings are invalid.
> 6. **Constant Head Assumption:** Trials must be conducted at **constant head AND constant speed**; otherwise the comparative analysis is invalid.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ⭐ **Best Guide Vane Opening** = opening corresponding to **maximum overall efficiency** under constant head and speed.
- ⭐ At best opening, flow enters runner **without shock** (velocity triangle is perfectly matched to blade angle $\beta_1$).
- ⭐ **Input Power** $P_{\text{in}} = \rho g Q H$ (units: W; ensure $Q$ is in m³/s).
- ⭐ **Output Power** $P_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ where $T = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot R$.
- ⭐ **Euler's Head Equation:** $E = \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2}{g}$.
- ⭐ **Overall Efficiency** $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{h}} \times \eta_{\text{m}}$.
- ⭐ **Unit Quantities:** $N_u = N / \sqrt{H}$, $Q_u = Q / \sqrt{H}$, $P_u = P / H^{3/2}$.
- ⭐ As opening **increases** from zero to 100%: discharge $Q$ increases **monotonically**, but efficiency first **rises**, reaches a **peak** (best opening), and then **falls**.
- ⭐ Always perform experiments with the **head and speed held constant** using a by-pass valve and speed governor.
- ⭐ Plot the **efficiency vs. opening graph** — the peak of the curve is the answer.
- ⭐ **Prony brake with spring balance** is the standard load-measuring device in KTU-affiliated lab setups.
- ⭐ Reaction turbine efficiency is sensitive to **draft tube performance** — ensure the draft tube is fully primed (no air entrainment) during trials.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Jet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiments
> - **Topic:** Impact of Jet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Impact of Jet** is a classical fluid mechanics laboratory experiment that experimentally validates the **momentum equation** (Euler's momentum theorem) by measuring the force exerted by a fluid jet striking a stationary or moving target. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this experiment is categorized under the *Impulse-Momentum Principle* demonstrations and forms a foundational exercise for understanding **turbomachinery**, **Pelton wheel design**, and **jet propulsion systems**.

The governing principle is the **Reynolds Transport Theorem** applied to a control volume enclosing the jet-vane interaction zone:

$$\vec{F} = \frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} \rho \vec{V} \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho \vec{V} (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

For a steady, uniform jet, this reduces to the **momentum flux** across the control surface, which directly gives the impact force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Impact of jet is the application of the *Impulse-Momentum Equation* to determine the force exerted by a fluid jet on a vane, plate, or curved surface, experimentally confirming the theoretical relationship F ∝ ρaV².

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding a garden hose and spraying water against a wooden plank. You can feel the **push** on your hands. That push is the *impact force*. Now imagine tilting the plank — the push becomes weaker. Now imagine a curved scoop (like a Pelton wheel bucket) that catches the water and turns it around — the push becomes much stronger because the water is forced to reverse its direction.

**Intuitive Summary:**
- **Flat plate (perpendicular):** Water stops dead in front → Maximum momentum change → Maximum force.
- **Inclined plate:** Water glances off at an angle → Less momentum change → Force reduced.
- **Curved vane (U-turn):** Water reverses direction completely → Momentum changes from +V to −V → Force nearly doubles.
- **Moving vane:** Water's relative speed decreases because the vane "runs away" → Reduced force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in the Experiment:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity, **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Density of water, **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Specific weight, **γ = 9810 N/m³**

## 1.3 Experimental Objective (KTU 2024 CO Mapping)

| Course Outcome (CO) | Mapping to Experiment |
|---|---|
| **CO1** | Apply momentum equation to predict impact force |
| **CO2** | Measure coefficient of velocity (Cv) of the nozzle |
| **CO3** | Compare experimental and theoretical impact forces |
| **CO4** | Calculate efficiency of momentum transfer |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Experiment

The experiment proceeds in the following logical stages:

1. **Establishing Steady Flow:** A supply tank is filled, and water flows through a converging nozzle under a constant head. The head H is measured using a piezometer or hook gauge at the nozzle centerline.
2. **Jet Formation:** The nozzle accelerates the water, producing a high-velocity jet. Because of viscous drag and turbulence, the actual velocity is slightly less than the theoretical √(2gH). This is captured by the **coefficient of velocity (Cv)**.
3. **Vane Positioning:** The target vane (flat, inclined, or curved) is mounted on a lever arm pivoted at a fulcrum, with a weighing pan on the opposite end.
4. **Force Measurement:** When the jet strikes the vane, the reaction force is balanced by adding dead weights to the pan until equilibrium is restored.
5. **Data Tabulation:** Multiple trials are conducted by varying the head H, and readings are tabulated.
6. **Comparative Analysis:** Theoretical force (using F = ρaV²) is computed and compared with measured force to determine experimental efficiency.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Momentum Equation

Newton's Second Law in fluid form states that the **net force on a control volume equals the rate of change of momentum flux**. For a steady jet of mass flow rate **ṁ** entering with velocity V at angle θ₁ and leaving with velocity V at angle θ₂, the force is:

$$F = \dot{m} \Delta V_x = \rho a V (V \cos\theta_2 - V \cos\theta_1)$$

This is the **theoretical backbone** of the impact of jet experiment.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** This principle is used in designing Pelton wheel buckets, ship propellers, jet engines, rocket nozzles, water cannons, and even firefighting equipment. The impact of jet experiment is therefore not academic — it is the design foundation for hydropower generation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Target Geometry | Theoretical Force (Fₜₕ) | Direction | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Flat plate, perpendicular** | F = ρaV² | Along jet axis | Maximum impact case |
| 2 | **Flat plate, inclined at angle θ** | Fₙ = ρaV² sin θ | Normal to plate | Glancing jet |
| 3 | **Flat plate, inclined at angle θ** | Fₓ = ρaV² sin² θ | Along jet axis | Glancing jet (x-component) |
| 4 | **Curved vane, deflection angle θ** | Fₓ = ρaV² (1 + cos θ) | Along jet axis | U-turn (θ = 180° → 2ρaV²) |
| 5 | **Hemisherical vane** | F = 2ρaV² | Along jet axis | Maximum practical force |
| 6 | **Moving flat plate (vane velocity u)** | F = ρa(V − u)² | Along jet axis | Pelton wheel |
| 7 | **Moving curved vane** | F = ρa(V − u)² (1 + cos θ) | Along jet axis | Moving turbine bucket |
| 8 | **Jet velocity (theoretical)** | Vₜₕ = √(2gH) | — | Torricelli's law |
| 9 | **Jet velocity (actual)** | Vₐ = Cᵥ √(2gH) | — | With coefficient |
| 10 | **Mass flow rate** | ṁ = ρ a V = ρ a Cᵥ √(2gH) | kg/s | Continuity |
| 11 | **Discharge collected** | Q = A·t⁻¹ (volumetric) | m³/s | Volumetric tank method |
| 12 | **Experimental Cᵥ** | Cᵥ = √(2gH)⁻¹ · (Q/A)⁻¹ · ... | dimensionless | V = Q/Aₐ then back-solve |

**Where:**
- **ρ** = density of water = **1000 kg/m³**
- **a** = nozzle exit cross-sectional area (m²)
- **V** = actual jet velocity (m/s)
- **g** = 9.81 m/s²
- **H** = head causing flow (m)
- **Cᵥ** = coefficient of velocity (typically 0.95 to 0.99)
- **θ** = angle of inclination or deflection (degrees)
- **u** = velocity of moving vane (m/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the curved vane formula F = ρaV²(1 + cos θ) and the moving vane formula F = ρa(V − u)²(1 + cos θ). These are the most frequently tested equations.

## 2.4 Coefficient of Velocity — The Bridge Between Theory and Experiment

Because real fluids suffer **viscous losses**, **turbulence**, and **boundary layer separation** inside the nozzle, the actual jet velocity is always less than the theoretical value:

$$V_{actual} = C_v \cdot V_{theoretical} = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$$

The **coefficient of velocity (Cᵥ)** is the key experimental parameter students must compute. It is determined by:

1. Measuring the head **H** at the nozzle.
2. Collecting discharge **Q** for a known time **t** in a measuring tank.
3. Computing actual velocity: $V_{actual} = \frac{Q}{a}$
4. Back-calculating: $C_v = \frac{V_{actual}}{\sqrt{2gH}}$

Typical values: **Cᵥ = 0.95 to 0.99** for a well-designed nozzle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Force on a Stationary Flat Plate (Perpendicular to Jet)

**Given:** A jet of water with velocity V and cross-sectional area a strikes a flat plate held perpendicular to the jet.

**Setup:** Take a control volume enclosing the jet-plate interaction. The jet enters the CV along the x-axis.

### Step 1: Apply Continuity
Mass flow rate entering = Mass flow rate leaving (in steady state, mass is conserved).

$$\dot{m} = \rho a V$$

### Step 2: Apply Momentum Equation in x-direction
For a control volume, the net force equals the rate of change of momentum flux:

$$F_x = \dot{m} (V_{out,x} - V_{in,x})$$

### Step 3: Velocity Component Analysis
- **Inlet velocity (x-component):** $V_{in,x} = V$ (jet moves in +x direction)
- **Outlet velocity (x-component):** $V_{out,x} = 0$ (water splashes radially and loses its x-momentum)

### Step 4: Substitute

$$F_x = \rho a V (0 - V) = -\rho a V^2$$

### Step 5: Magnitude of Force
The negative sign indicates the force exerted by the plate on the fluid is in the −x direction. By Newton's Third Law, the force exerted by the jet on the plate is in the +x direction with magnitude:

$$\boxed{F = \rho a V^2}$$

### Step 6: Substitute V in terms of Head

$$F = \rho a (C_v \sqrt{2gH})^2 = 2 \rho a g C_v^2 H$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the maximum possible force for a given jet. The Pelton wheel approximates this when the bucket is hemispherical and the jet is turned 180°.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Force on an Inclined Flat Plate (Angle θ from Jet)

**Given:** The flat plate is tilted at an angle θ to the jet axis.

### Step 1: Define the Geometry
- The plate normal makes angle θ with the jet direction.
- The jet velocity **V** is along x-axis.

### Step 2: Apply Momentum in Plate-Normal Direction
Component of jet velocity normal to the plate:
$$V_n = V \sin\theta$$

### Step 3: Force Normal to Plate
The mass flow striking the plate is reduced because the effective area intercepting the jet is a/sin θ. The mass flow rate is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot a \cdot V$$

(same as before, since the full jet strikes the inclined plate)

### Step 4: Compute the Force
Momentum change normal to the plate (incoming normal velocity V sinθ, outgoing normal velocity 0):

$$F_n = \dot{m} \cdot (0 - V \sin\theta) = -\rho a V^2 \sin\theta$$

### Step 5: Magnitude
$$\boxed{F_n = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta}$$

### Step 6: Force Along Jet Axis (x-direction)
Resolving along the x-axis (the direction the impact force acts on the plate support):

$$F_x = F_n \sin\theta = \rho a V^2 \sin^2\theta$$

$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a V^2 \sin^2\theta}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Limiting Case Check:** When θ = 90°, Fₓ = ρaV² (flat plate perpendicular) ✓. When θ = 0°, Fₓ = 0 (jet glances along the plate, no impact) ✓.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Force on a Stationary Curved Vane (Deflection Angle θ)

**Given:** A jet strikes a curved vane and is deflected through an angle θ.

### Step 1: Identify Velocities
- Inlet velocity: V at angle 0° (along x-axis), x-component = V
- Outlet velocity: V at angle θ from the inlet, x-component = V cos θ
- (Assume no friction, so outlet speed = inlet speed)

### Step 2: Apply Momentum Equation in x-direction
Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = \rho a V$

$$F_x = \dot{m} (V_{out,x} - V_{in,x}) = \rho a V (V \cos\theta - V)$$

### Step 3: Simplify

$$F_x = \rho a V^2 (\cos\theta - 1) = -\rho a V^2 (1 - \cos\theta)$$

### Step 4: Magnitude (force on vane from jet)

$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a V^2 (1 + \cos\theta)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Case — Hemispherical Vane (θ = 180°):** cos 180° = −1
> F = ρaV²(1 − 1) = **2ρaV²** — This is the **maximum possible impact force** for a single jet.

### Step 5: Force in y-direction (Radial)

$$F_y = \rho a V (V \sin\theta - 0) = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta$$

$$\boxed{F_y = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta}$$

For a hemispherical vane (θ = 180°): F_y = 0 (symmetric deflection, no net y-force). ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Force on a Moving Vane (Pelton Wheel Analogy)

**Given:** A vane moves with velocity u in the same direction as the jet of velocity V.

### Step 1: Use Relative Velocity Principle
In the frame of the moving vane, the relative velocity of the jet is:

$$V_r = V - u$$

The mass flow rate actually striking the vane (since the vane is moving away from the jet) is:

$$\dot{m}' = \rho a_r V_r = \rho a V_r$$

where aᵣ is the area of the jet relative to the vane (smaller than a because the jet is "stretched").

For a flat plate moving away:

$$a_r = a \cdot \frac{V_r}{V} = a \left(1 - \frac{u}{V}\right)$$

### Step 2: Apply Momentum Equation
Force on the vane (using relative velocities):

$$F = \dot{m}' \cdot V_r = \rho a V_r \cdot V_r = \rho a (V - u)^2$$

Wait — more rigorously using mass conservation:
$$\dot{m}' = \rho a_r V_r = \rho \cdot a\left(\frac{V_r}{V}\right) \cdot V_r = \rho a \frac{V_r^2}{V}$$

So:
$$F = \dot{m}' \cdot V_r = \rho a \frac{V_r^2}{V} \cdot V_r = \rho a \frac{V_r^3}{V} = \rho a \frac{(V-u)^3}{V}$$

### Step 3: Practical Work Done Approach
For Pelton wheel analysis, the work done per second is:

$$P = F \cdot u = \rho a (V-u)^2 \cdot u$$

This is maximized when u = V/3 (Pelton wheel design rule), giving:

$$P_{max} = \rho a \cdot \frac{4V^2}{9} \cdot \frac{V}{3} = \frac{4}{27} \rho a V^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Maximum efficiency of a single Pelton wheel jet = **8/27 × 100% ≈ 29.6%**. Multi-jet systems use this principle.

### Step 4: Final Force Formula (most common KTU form)

$$\boxed{F = \rho a (V - u)^2 \quad \text{(for moving flat plate)}}$$

$$\boxed{F = \rho a (V - u)^2 (1 + \cos\theta) \quad \text{(for moving curved vane)}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Numerical Problem

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Pattern):**
A jet of water of diameter 25 mm strikes a flat plate normally with a velocity of 20 m/s. Find the force on the plate. Take ρ = 1000 kg/m³.

**Step 1: Identify given values**
- Diameter d = 25 mm = 0.025 m
- Velocity V = 20 m/s
- ρ = 1000 kg/m³
- Plate: Flat, perpendicular

**Step 2: Compute nozzle area**

$$a = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Apply force formula**

$$F = \rho a V^2 = 1000 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times (20)^2$$

**Step 4: Evaluate**

$$F = 1000 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 400$$

$$F = 196.35 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 196.35 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 5: In practical terms (KTU conversion)**

$$F = 196.35 / 9.81 \approx 20.02 \text{ kgf}$$

This is the dead weight the student would place on the weighing pan to balance the impact force.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Experimental Setup — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional flow of the impact of jet apparatus, identifying all subsystems, their interactions, and measurement points.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Supply Tank] -->|Constant Head H| B[Control Valve]
    B --> C[Supply Pipe]
    C --> D[Converging Nozzle]
    D -->|High Velocity Jet V| E[Target Vane]
    E -->|Reaction Force F| F[Lever Arm with Fulcrum]
    F -->|Counterweight| G[Weighing Pan with Dead Weights]
    E -->|Splashed Water| H[Collecting Tank]
    H -->|Discharge Q| I[Measuring Cylinder]
    D -.->|Piezometer| J[Hook Gauge for Head H]
    I -.->|Stopwatch| K[Time t]

    subgraph Force_Balance[Force Balance Mechanism]
        F
        G
    end

    subgraph Flow_Measurement[Flow Measurement Loop]
        H
        I
        K
    end

    subgraph Head_Measurement[Head Measurement Loop]
        J
    end
```

**Functional Interpretation:**
- **Supply Tank → Nozzle:** Provides the potential energy that converts to kinetic energy in the jet.
- **Nozzle → Vane:** The high-velocity jet transfers momentum to the vane.
- **Vane → Lever Arm:** The reaction force is transmitted to the weighing pan via the lever.
- **Collecting Tank → Measuring Cylinder:** Used to determine the actual discharge and hence the actual velocity.
- **Hook Gauge:** Measures the head H for theoretical velocity computation.

---

## 4.2 Force Decomposition Flowchart (Flat Plate vs. Curved Vane)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Impact of Jet Problem] --> Q1{Type of Target?}
    
    Q1 -->|Flat Plate| A1[Flat Plate Branch]
    Q1 -->|Curved Vane| A2[Curved Vane Branch]
    Q1 -->|Moving Vane| A3[Moving Vane Branch]
    
    A1 --> A1Q{Inclination Angle}
    A1Q -->|Perpendicular theta=90| R1[F = rho a V squared]
    A1Q -->|Inclined at theta| R2[Fx = rho a V squared sin squared theta]
    
    A2 --> A2Q{Deflection Angle}
    A2Q -->|Quarter Turn theta=90| R3[Fx = rho a V squared]
    A2Q -->|Half Turn theta=180| R4[Fx = 2 rho a V squared]
    A2Q -->|General theta| R5[Fx = rho a V squared 1 plus cos theta]
    
    A3 --> A3Q{Vane Geometry}
    A3Q -->|Flat Moving| R6[F = rho a V minus u squared]
    A3Q -->|Curved Moving| R7[F = rho a V minus u squared 1 plus cos theta]
    
    R1 --> Final[Compare with Measured Force]
    R2 --> Final
    R3 --> Final
    R4 --> Final
    R5 --> Final
    R6 --> Final
    R7 --> Final
    
    Final --> End[Compute Efficiency eta = F_exp slash F_theo]
```

---

## 4.3 Free Body Diagram of the Lever System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Lever[Force Lever System]
        P1[Pivot / Fulcrum Point F1]:::pivot
    end
    
    L[Horizontal Lever Arm of Length L_total]:::arm
    P1 --- L
    
    L -->|Distance L1| V[Vane Location]:::vane
    L -->|Distance L2| W[Weighing Pan]:::weight
    
    Jet[Water Jet Direction] -->|Force F jet| V
    V -->|Reaction F equals W times L2 slash L1| W
    
    W -->|Counterweight W_pan| Counter[Dead Weights Added]:::counter
    
    classDef pivot fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef arm fill:#99ccff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef vane fill:#ff6666,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef weight fill:#66cc66,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef counter fill:#cc99ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Key Principle (Moment Balance):**

$$F_{jet} \times L_1 = W_{pan} \times L_2$$

$$F_{jet} = \frac{W_{pan} \times L_2}{L_1}$$

When **L₁ = L₂**, then **F_jet = W_pan** (simple direct reading). KTU apparatus often uses unequal arms, requiring this calculation.

---

## 4.4 Control Volume — Momentum Flux Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CV[Control Volume Enclosing Vane]
        Inlet[Inlet Section 1]:::inlet
        VaneRegion[Vane Region with Reaction Force F]:::region
        Outlet[Outlet Section 2]:::outlet
    end
    
    Inlet -->|V1 along x| VaneRegion
    VaneRegion -->|V2 at angle theta| Outlet
    VaneRegion -.->|Force F on vane| Ext[External Support]:::support
    
    classDef inlet fill:#3399ff,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef region fill:#ffaa00,stroke:#000
    classDef outlet fill:#3399ff,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef support fill:#cc3333,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

This block diagram illustrates the **Eulerian control volume** for the impact problem, showing:
- Inlet momentum flux: **ρaV²** along x
- Outlet momentum flux: **ρaV² cos θ** along x
- Reaction force on vane: balances the difference

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State and explain the Impulse-Momentum equation as applied to the impact of a jet on a stationary flat plate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Impulse-Momentum equation** states that the net force acting on a fluid in a given direction equals the rate of change of momentum in that direction.

$$F = \dot{m} \Delta V = \rho a V (V_{out} - V_{in})$$

**Application to flat plate:**
- Inlet velocity along x-axis: $V_{in} = V$
- Outlet velocity along x-axis: $V_{out} = 0$ (water splashes radially)
- Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = \rho a V$

Substituting: $F = \rho a V (0 - V) = \rho a V^2$

The force on the plate is therefore **F = ρaV²**, directed along the jet axis.

> **Marking scheme:** 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for application, 1 mark for final result.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define coefficient of velocity (Cv). How is it determined in the impact of jet experiment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **coefficient of velocity (Cv)** is the ratio of the actual velocity of the jet at the vena contracta to the theoretical velocity calculated using Torricelli's theorem.

$$C_v = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_{theoretical}} = \frac{V_{actual}}{\sqrt{2gH}}$$

**Determination procedure (3 marks):**
1. Measure the head **H** at the nozzle centerline using a hook gauge or piezometer.
2. Collect the discharge **Q** in a measuring tank for a known time **t**.
3. Compute actual velocity: $V_{actual} = Q / a$, where **a** is the nozzle exit area.
4. Compute theoretical velocity: $V_{theoretical} = \sqrt{2gH}$
5. Calculate: $C_v = V_{actual} / V_{theoretical}$

Typical values: **Cᵥ = 0.95 to 0.99** for a well-designed nozzle.

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the force exerted by a jet of water on a stationary flat plate inclined at an angle θ to the jet axis. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A jet of water 50 mm in diameter moving with a velocity of 25 m/s strikes a curved vane moving with a velocity of 10 m/s in the direction of the jet. The jet is deflected through an angle of 150°. Calculate:
- (i) The force exerted on the vane in the direction of jet. **[4 marks]**
- (ii) The power developed by the vane. **[3 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for B-A (a):** Force on Inclined Flat Plate

**Given:** Jet velocity V, area a, plate inclined at angle θ.

**Step 1:** Identify velocity components. The plate normal makes angle θ with the jet direction.

**Step 2:** Velocity of jet normal to plate = V sin θ

**Step 3:** Apply momentum equation in the direction normal to the plate:

$$F_n = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta V_n = \rho a V \cdot (0 - V \sin\theta)$$

$$F_n = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta \quad \text{(normal to plate, 4 marks)}$$

**Step 4:** Resolve along the jet direction (x-axis):

$$F_x = F_n \sin\theta = \rho a V^2 \sin^2\theta$$

$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a V^2 \sin^2\theta \quad \text{(along jet direction, 3 marks)}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for B-A (b):** Moving Curved Vane

**Given:**
- Diameter d = 50 mm = 0.05 m
- V = 25 m/s, u = 10 m/s
- θ = 150°, ρ = 1000 kg/m³

**Step 1: Compute nozzle area**

$$a = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute relative velocity**

$$V_r = V - u = 25 - 10 = 15 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 (i): Apply force formula for moving curved vane**

$$F_x = \rho a V_r^2 (1 + \cos\theta)$$

$$F_x = 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times (15)^2 \times (1 + \cos 150°)$$

$$\cos 150° = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = -0.8660$$

$$F_x = 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 225 \times (1 - 0.8660)$$

$$F_x = 441.79 \times 0.134 = 59.20 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_x \approx 59.2 \text{ N} \quad \text{(4 marks)}}$$

**Step 4 (ii): Power developed**

$$P = F_x \times u = 59.20 \times 10 = 592.0 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 592 \text{ W} = 0.592 \text{ kW} \quad \text{(3 marks)}}$$

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the expression for the force exerted by a jet of water on a stationary hemispherical (semi-circular) vane. Show that this force is twice that on a flat plate. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 75 mm diameter jet of water strikes a flat plate held normal to the jet. The plate is moving against the jet with a velocity of 5 m/s. If the discharge through the nozzle is 0.05 m³/s, determine:
- (i) The force exerted on the plate. **[4 marks]**
- (ii) The work done per second by the jet on the plate. **[3 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for B-B (a):** Hemispherical Vane Force

**Given:** Curved vane deflects jet by θ = 180°.

**Step 1:** Apply momentum equation along jet direction.

For a curved vane with deflection θ, the force is:

$$F_x = \rho a V^2 (1 + \cos\theta)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute θ = 180°.

$$F_{hemi} = \rho a V^2 (1 + \cos 180°) = \rho a V^2 (1 - 1) = 2\rho a V^2$$

**Step 3:** Compare with flat plate force F_flat = ρaV²:

$$\frac{F_{hemi}}{F_{flat}} = \frac{2\rho a V^2}{\rho a V^2} = 2$$

$$\boxed{F_{hemi} = 2 F_{flat} \quad \text{(Hence proved, 3 marks for derivation + 4 marks for explanation)}}$$

**Physical Reason:** In a flat plate, the jet's x-momentum goes from V to 0 (change of V). In a hemispherical vane, the x-momentum goes from +V to −V (change of 2V). The momentum change — and hence the force — doubles.

---

#### **Model Solution for B-B (b):** Moving Flat Plate Against the Jet

**Given:**
- d = 75 mm = 0.075 m
- Plate velocity u = 5 m/s (against jet)
- Q = 0.05 m³/s
- ρ = 1000 kg/m³

**Step 1: Compute jet velocity from discharge**

$$V = \frac{Q}{a} = \frac{0.05}{\frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2} = \frac{0.05}{4.418 \times 10^{-3}} = 11.32 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 (i): Since plate moves against jet, relative velocity is V + u**

$$V_r = V + u = 11.32 + 5 = 16.32 \text{ m/s}$$

Wait — for a plate moving **against** the jet, the effective impact velocity increases. The mass flow striking the plate is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho a (V + u) \quad \text{(since plate moves into the jet)}$$

Force on plate:
$$F = \dot{m} \cdot V_r = \rho a (V + u)^2$$

$$F = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times (16.32)^2$$

$$F = 4.418 \times 266.34 = 1176.6 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 1176.6 \text{ N} \quad \text{(4 marks)}}$$

**Step 3 (ii): Work done per second by jet on plate**

Since the plate moves **away** from the jet at 5 m/s in the direction of jet travel, the work done is:

$$P = F \times u = 1176.6 \times 5 = 5883 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 5.88 \text{ kW} \quad \text{(3 marks)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Loss:**
> 1. **Sign of cos θ:** For θ > 90°, cos θ is NEGATIVE. Students often write (1 + cos 150°) as (1 + 0.866) = 1.866, getting nearly **10× the correct answer**. The correct value is (1 − 0.866) = 0.134. **Always check the sign of cos θ for angles above 90°.**
> 2. **Relative velocity direction:** When the vane moves in the same direction as the jet, Vᵣ = V − u. When the vane moves against the jet, Vᵣ = V + u. Mixing these up gives incorrect force values.
> 3. **Units:** Mixing mm with m, or using g (cm/s²) instead of m/s², is a frequent source of error. Convert all dimensions to SI before substitution.
> 4. **Area calculation:** Forgetting the π/4 factor in the area formula leads to a 78.5% error in the final answer.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Pointers for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **The Master Formula:** F = ρaV² is the foundation. Every other formula is a modification of this.
- **Flat plate (perpendicular):** F = ρaV² — memorize this first.
- **Flat plate (inclined θ):** Fₓ = ρaV² sin²θ — limit check: θ = 90° → F = ρaV².
- **Curved vane (deflection θ):** Fₓ = ρaV²(1 + cos θ) — limit check: θ = 180° → F = 2ρaV².
- **Moving vane (velocity u):** Replace V with (V − u) in all stationary formulas.
- **Power developed by moving vane:** P = F × u (Watts).
- **Cv determination:** Always use the discharge method, not the head method alone.
- **Pelton wheel efficiency maximum:** u = V/3, giving η_max = 8/27 ≈ 29.6%.
- **Torricelli's equation:** V_theoretical = √(2gH), g = 9.81 m/s², ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³.
- **Limiting cases to verify your formula:** 
  - θ = 0° (curved vane): F = 2ρaV² (no deflection, but tangential thrust still acts)
  - θ = 90° (curved vane): F = ρaV² (quarter turn)
  - θ = 180° (hemispherical): F = 2ρaV² (U-turn)
- **Experimental error sources to discuss in viva:** friction in nozzle, vena contracta losses, splashing not perfectly radial, air resistance, lever arm friction, parallax in weighing.
- **KTU Viva Questions to Prepare:**
  1. Why is the actual jet velocity less than √(2gH)?
  2. What is the physical significance of Cᵥ in nozzle flow?
  3. Why does a hemispherical vane give twice the force of a flat plate?
  4. How is the experiment used to validate the momentum equation?
  5. What is the design velocity ratio for a Pelton wheel bucket?
